title: Problem 8
statement_text: Improve the range of $|S|$ in Theorem 1.
context_text: Theorem 1 establishes that if $q$ is an odd prime power and $S\subseteq\mathbb{F}_{q}$ with $0\in S$ and $|S|>(q+1)/2$, then any $A\subseteq\mathbb{F}_{q}^{n}$ containing no three-term arithmetic progression with common difference in $S^{n}$ satisfies $|A|\leq q^{(1-\varepsilon_{q})n}$. The problem asks whether the condition $|S|>(q+1)/2$ can be weakened.
paper_title: A note on arithmetic progressions with restricted differences

title: Problem 9
statement_text: Is there an arithmetic triangle removal lemma with restricted differences?
context_text: The multicolor sum-free result was used to prove an arithmetic triangle removal lemma in $\mathbb{F}_{q}^{n}$. In line with this, it is natural to wonder if there is a restricted differences version extending Theorem 2.
paper_title: A note on arithmetic progressions with restricted differences

title: Problem 1.1
statement_text: Is it true that for every 2-edge-connected graph $G$, the reconfiguration graph $\mathcal{F}(G,5)$ is connected?
context_text: This is the natural reconfiguration version of Tutte's 5-flow conjecture. The paper notes that Problem 1.1 is incomparable to Tutte's 5-flow conjecture itself, since connectedness of $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ does not imply non-emptiness. The group-flow analogue is posed separately as Problem 1.3.
paper_title: Nowhere-zero flow reconfiguration

title: Problem 2.6
statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph and $k$ be an integer. Prove that if $\mathcal{F}(G,\mathbb{Z}_{k})$ is connected, then $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ is also connected.
context_text: The paper establishes the easier direction (connectivity of $\mathcal{F}(G,k)$ implies connectivity of $\mathcal{F}(G,\mathbb{Z}_k)$) and provides examples showing the group structure matters. The converse direction is open; the authors note they cannot prove it even for $k=4$.
paper_title: Nowhere-zero flow reconfiguration

title: Problem 2
statement_text: Determine $\lim_{r\rightarrow\infty}\frac{c_{r}}{r}$.
context_text: The paper proves that the sequence $(c_r/r)_{r\in\mathbb{N}}$ converges to some limit $L\in[\frac{3}{22},1]$; Problem 2 asks for the exact value of this limit. The problem is stated immediately after the proof of convergence.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs

title: Informal Question on optimal directed tree-width constant for regular digraphs
statement_text: What is the best-possible constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that every $r$-regular digraph $D$ has directed tree-width at least $\varepsilon r$?
context_text: Motivated by Theorem 7 of the paper, which establishes that every $r$-regular digraph has directed tree-width growing with $r$, the authors pose this question about the optimal linear constant in such a lower bound.
notes: Posed in the text as 'it would be interesting to determine' following Problem 2, without a formal labelled environment.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs

title: Conjecture 1.8
statement_text: For every graph $H$, there exists $d>0$ such that for every $\varepsilon\in(0,\frac{1}{2})$ and every graph $G$ with $\mathrm{ind}_{H}(G)\leq(\varepsilon^{d}\lvert G\rvert)^{\lvert H\rvert}$, there is an $\varepsilon$-restricted subset of $V(G)$ with size at least $\varepsilon^{d}\lvert G\rvert$.
context_text: This 'polynomial Nikiforov' statement, introduced without external attribution, unifies Theorem 1.6 and Conjecture 1.7. A graph $H$ satisfying it is called viral; Bucić, Fox, and Pham [4] proved that having the Erdős-Hajnal property is equivalent to being viral.
paper_title: Induced subgraph density. IV. New graphs with the Erdős-Hajnal property

title: Conjecture 6.1
statement_text: Let $h\colon E(K_{n,n})\to\mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that $\|h(e)\|_{1}\leq 1$ for all $e\in E(K_{n,n})$. Then there is a perfect matching $M$ of $K_{n,n}$ satisfying [a bound of order $O(\sqrt{k})$ on $\|\sum_{e\in M}h(e)\|_1$].
context_text: There is a gap between the $O(k^2)$ upper bound for representative matchings in bipartite complete graphs (Section 1.1) and the worst-case lower bound of $\sqrt{k/2}$ constructed in Theorem 5.1. The authors conjecture that the lower bound gives the correct order, so that the true bound is $\Theta(\sqrt{k})$.
notes: The conclusion of the conjecture is truncated in the source text; the full right-hand side bound is not reproduced. Based on the surrounding context the conjectured bound is $O(\sqrt{k})$, matching the lower bound of Theorem 5.1.
paper_title: Colour-balanced subgraphs

title: Conjecture 6.2
statement_text: There exists some absolute constant $C$ such that for all positive integers $k$ and $t$, every colour-balanced $k$-edge-colouring of $K_{2kt}$ admits a perfect matching $M$ satisfying $f_{c}(M)<C$.
context_text: There is a gap between the $O(k^2)$ upper bound from Theorem 1.2 and the constant lower bound constructed in Theorem 5.3 for matchings in complete graphs. The authors conjecture the constant lower bound is tight. They note that a $\sqrt{k}$ lower bound for perfect matchings of $K_{kt,kt}$ implies that new ideas would be necessary to prove this conjecture.
paper_title: Colour-balanced subgraphs

title: Problem 6.3
statement_text: Improve the bounds on $f_{h}(F)$ for bounded-degree spanning forests $F$ in complete graphs when $k\geq 3$.
context_text: For $k=2$, error bounds sharp up to an additive constant are known for spanning forests of maximum degree $\Delta$. For $k>2$ the paper shows the error remains linear in $\Delta$, but no attempt is made to optimise the leading coefficient.
paper_title: Colour-balanced subgraphs

title: Problem 6.4
statement_text: Improve the bounds on $f_{h}(H)$ for a Hamilton cycle $H$ in complete or complete bipartite graphs.
context_text: The general upper bound from Theorem 1.2 gives $O(k^2)$ for Hamilton cycles in complete graphs, and the same bound holds for complete bipartite graphs by the paper's methods. A necklace-splitting argument shows any improvement to the bipartite matching bound yields an equal improvement here, so progress on either problem benefits the other.
paper_title: Colour-balanced subgraphs

title: Problem 1
statement_text: Does every graph $G$ admit a pivot Gray code on its spanning trees?
context_text: A pivot Gray code for spanning trees requires that consecutive spanning trees differ by an edge exchange sharing a common endpoint (the strong revolving door property). Although the corresponding reconfiguration graph is connected for any graph $G$, it remains open whether all graphs admit such a code. Pivot Gray codes have been constructed for fan graphs and more generally for outerplanar graphs.
paper_title: A Gray code for arborescences of tournaments

title: Conjecture 4.1
statement_text: Let $d$ be sufficiently large, and let $n$ be sufficiently large relative to $d$. Suppose that $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices and minimum degree at least $d$, and let $\mathcal{T}$ be a uniformly random spanning tree of $G$. Then, for every tree $T$ it holds that $$\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{T}\cong T)\leq n^{-(1/2-o_n(1))(d-1)}.$$
context_text: The authors conjecture that the optimal constant factor in the exponent of the anticoncentration bound should be $1/2$, matching the anticoncentration of a uniformly random spanning tree of $K_{d,n-d}$. For $K_{d,n-d}$, a spanning tree up to isomorphism is determined by a minimal subtree connecting the $d$ vertices in the small class and the number of leaves attached to each of these $d$ vertices.
notes: The full inequality in the statement was not explicitly reproduced in the extracted theorem block; the bound $n^{-(1/2-o_n(1))(d-1)}$ is inferred from the context text which states the optimal constant should be $1/2$ and that this would match $K_{d,n-d}$.
paper_title: Anticoncentration of random spanning trees in graphs with large minimum degree

title: Conjecture 4.2
statement_text: Let $d$ be sufficiently large, and let $n$ be sufficiently large relative to $d$. Suppose that $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices and minimum degree at least $d$. Then, the number of non-isomorphic spanning trees of $G$ is at least $\Omega(n^{d-1})$.
context_text: The anticoncentration property of Conjecture 4.1 would imply only $n^{(1/2-o_n(1))(d-1)}$ non-isomorphic spanning trees, but $K_{d,n-d}$ has $\Omega(n^{d-1})$ non-isomorphic spanning trees. The authors conjecture that this larger quantity gives an essentially optimal lower bound, with $K_{d,n-d}$ being an extremal example.
paper_title: Anticoncentration of random spanning trees in graphs with large minimum degree

title: Question 5
statement_text: What is the smallest number $\Delta_{0}$ such that every graph $G$ with $\Delta(G)\geq\Delta_{0}$ and $\omega(G)=\Delta(G)-1$ satisfies $\chi(G)=\chi_{\ell}(G)=\chi_{DP}(G)$?
context_text: Theorem 4 of this paper gives an upper bound $\Delta_0 \leq 3\cdot 10^9$ for the correspondence coloring analogue. The equality $\chi(G)=\chi_\ell(G)=\chi_{DP}(G)$ clearly holds for all graphs $G$ with $\omega(G)=\Delta(G)+1$, and the parameters also coincide for graphs with $\omega(G)=\Delta(G)$; the smallest threshold $\Delta_0$ for the case $\omega(G)=\Delta(G)-1$ remains unknown.
paper_title: On Borodin-Kostochka conjecture for correspondence coloring

title: Informal conjecture on sun-free $\chi$-boundedness without net exclusion
statement_text: It could be true that the class of all sun-free graphs is already $\chi$-bounded.
context_text: After stating Problem 1.4, the authors remark that they are not sure why the net needs to be excluded, suggesting that sun-freeness alone might suffice for $\chi$-boundedness without additionally requiring net-freeness.
notes: Expressed as a tentative remark ("it could be true that") following Problem 1.4, not as a labelled conjecture environment.
paper_title: Suns in triangle-free graphs of large chromatic number

title: Conjecture 1
statement_text: $m(n)\sim\frac{4}{3}\cdot\frac{n^{3/2}}{\ln n}$.
context_text: The paper proves $m(n)=\Theta(n^{3/2}/\ln n)$ with matching constants $4/3$ (lower) and $5/3$ (upper) in Theorem 1. The authors state that closing this gap and pinning down the exact constant requires additional ideas, and suspect $4/3$ is correct.
paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: $M_{\mathbb{P}(n)}(n)\sim\frac{1}{6}\cdot\frac{n^{3}}{\ln n}$.
context_text: Closely related to Conjecture 3. The lower bounds in Conjectures 3 and 4 follow from the proofs of the lower bounds in Theorems 1 and 5, respectively. The upper bounds remain open.
paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions

title: Conjecture 5
statement_text: For every $k$ satisfying $\Omega(\sqrt{n})=k<\sqrt{n}$, we have [asymptotic formula for $m_k(n)$ — mathematical conclusion truncated in source].
context_text: If Conjecture 1 holds, it also resolves $m_k(n)$ asymptotically for $k\geqslant\sqrt{n}$ via Theorem 4. The only remaining open case is $k<\sqrt{n}$ with $k\not=o(\sqrt{n})$, which this conjecture addresses.
notes: The mathematical conclusion after 'we have' is truncated in the extracted source; only the quantifier range is visible.
paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions

title: Conjecture 6
statement_text: If $k\ll n$, then [formula for $M_k(n)$], while otherwise, [formula for $M_k(n)$] — mathematical conclusions truncated in source.
context_text: Conjectured asymptotic behaviour of $M_k(n)$ for $k<n$ in view of Theorem 5, splitting into two regimes according to whether $k\ll n$ or not.
notes: The mathematical formulas in both cases are truncated in the extracted source; only the conditional structure is visible.
paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions

title: Conjecture 7
statement_text: For every fixed $n$ and $k$ tending to infinity, we have [asymptotic formula — mathematical conclusion truncated in source].
context_text: Concerns packing of $k$ arithmetic progressions of the form $B_d=\{d,2d,\ldots,nd\}$ for $d=1,2,\ldots,k$ with $n$ fixed. The authors suspect the trivial lower bound of $nk$ is asymptotically correct as $k\to\infty$.
notes: The mathematical conclusion after 'we have' is truncated in the extracted source; context strongly suggests the conjecture is $M_k(n)\sim nk$ as $k\to\infty$ for fixed $n$.
paper_title: Packing arithmetic progressions

title: Problem 1.4
statement_text: Determine or estimate the least integer $f=f_{r}(d,s_{1},\ldots,s_{r})$ such that for any set $P$ of $f$ points in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ there is a partition into $r$ pairwise disjoint sets $P=\bigcup_{i=1}^{r}P_{i}$ such that for any family of sets $C_{1},\ldots,C_{r}$ with $P_{i}\subset C_{i}$ where $C_{i}$ is an $s_{i}$-convex set for every $i\in[r]$ we have that $\bigcap_{i=1}^{r}C_{i}\neq\emptyset$.
context_text: This is a Tverberg-type generalization of Problem 1.3 to $r$ parts and $r$ families of $s_i$-convex sets. Radon's theorem is equivalent to $f(d,1,1)=d+2$ and Tverberg's theorem is equivalent to $f_{r}(d,1,\ldots,1)=(r-1)(d+1)+1$. The paper provides an upper bound via Theorem 1.6 but the exact determination remains open.
notes: The paper proves the upper bound $f_{r}(d,s_{1},\ldots,s_{r})=O\left(dr^{2}\cdot\log r\cdot\prod_{i=1}^{r}s_{i}\cdot\ln(1+\prod_{i=1}^{r}s_{i})\right)$ via Theorem 1.6, partially addressing this problem. A 2025 paper by Chen et al. (reference [9]) is titled 'A Tverberg-type problem of Kalai: Two negative answers to questions of Alon and Smorodinsky', suggesting specific sub-questions of this problem are attributed to the paper authors.
paper_title: Extended VC-dimension, and Radon and Tverberg type theorems for unions of convex sets

title: Question 1.1
statement_text: What are the unavoidable induced subgraphs of graphs with large treewidth?
context_text: This question is the central goal of the series of papers on induced subgraphs and tree decompositions. The answer is known when 'induced subgraphs' is replaced by 'subgraphs' or 'minors' via Robertson and Seymour's grid theorem, but the induced setting is more complex, requiring all basic obstructions (complete graphs, complete bipartite graphs, subdivided walls, and their line graphs) as well as non-basic ones such as Pohoata-Davies graphs, occultations, and layered wheels.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XVI. Complete bipartite induced minors

title: Conjecture 4.1
statement_text: There exists an $\varepsilon>0$ and infinitely many values of $n$ for which there exists a connected vertex transitive digraph of order $n$, whose perimeter gap is at least $\varepsilon n$.
context_text: The paper proves the perimeter gap grows at least as fast as $(1-o(1))\ln n$ and that circumference is always at least $\Omega(n^{1/3})$. Determining the best possible bounds seems far out of reach; this conjecture is proposed as a natural intermediate target, asserting that the logarithmic lower bound on the gap is far from tight.
paper_title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs

title: Conjecture 4.2
statement_text: Let $c(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive graph on $n$ vertices. Let $d(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive digraph on $n$ vertices. Then $d(n)=\Theta(c(n))$.
context_text: The paper's bound of $\Omega(n^{1/3})$ on the circumference of connected vertex transitive digraphs is slightly weaker than the best known $\Omega(n^{9/14})$ undirected bound. This conjecture asserts that the directed and undirected cases are asymptotically the same, proposed as a natural intermediate target.
paper_title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs

title: Question 4.3
statement_text: Does vertex transitivity guarantee that every two longest directed cycles in a connected vertex transitive digraph share a common vertex?
context_text: A major obstacle to translating results from graphs to digraphs is that, unlike 2-connected undirected graphs, strongly 2-connected digraphs may contain vertex-disjoint longest cycles. The authors ask whether vertex transitivity could be a condition guaranteeing intersection of all pairs of longest cycles.
notes: Exact statement not provided in the input; reconstructed from the surrounding prose ('We wonder if vertex transitivity could be such a condition') immediately following Conjecture 4.2.
paper_title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs

title: Question 4.4
context_text: Appears as a labelled question environment in the concluding remarks section alongside Question 4.3, but its full statement was not included in the provided paper content.
notes: Statement not available in the provided input; only the label [qn] was given with no accompanying text.
paper_title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs

title: Problem 9
statement_text: Is it true that for connected graphs $G$, $\operatorname{cop}(G)=o(\mathrm{td}(G))$?
context_text: The authors ask whether a sublinear bound on the cop number in terms of treedepth holds, viewing treedepth as a natural intermediate parameter between vertex cover number and treewidth. They note that the bound $\operatorname{cop}(G)\leqslant\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathrm{td}(G)+1)$ is easily deducible from existing results on treewidth, but improving significantly on it seems difficult.
paper_title: Cops and robber in graphs with bounded vertex cover number

title: Conjecture 4.2
statement_text: There is a function $f$ such that for every integer $k$, there is a polynomial-time algorithm that, given a tournament $T$, correctly concludes that $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\geq k$, or finds an order $\prec$ of $V(T)$ such that $\omega(T^{\prec})\leq f(k)$
context_text: Aboulker et al. [2] proved an approximation version for $k=3$: there is a constant $c$ and a polynomial-time algorithm that either certifies $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\geq 3$ or finds an ordering $\prec$ with $\omega(T^{\prec})\leq c$. The conjecture asks whether such an approximation scheme extends to all $k$.
paper_title: Finding forest-orderings of tournaments is NP-complete

title: Problem 4.4
statement_text: What is the complexity of the $\mathcal{C}$-FAS Problem when $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all paths? when $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of graphs with maximum degree $1$?
context_text: Following the NP-completeness of the $\mathcal{C}$-FAS Problem for forests (Theorem 1.1), it is natural to ask for which classes $\mathcal{C}$ the problem is in P. Paths and matchings (graphs with maximum degree at most 1) are two natural candidates for tractable cases.
paper_title: Finding forest-orderings of tournaments is NP-complete

title: Problem 1
statement_text: Which graph classes have bounded domination-to-2-independence ratio?
context_text: The domination-to-2-independence ratio of a graph class $\mathcal{G}$ is defined as $\sup_{G\in\mathcal{G}}\tfrac{\gamma(G)}{\alpha_{2}(G)}$. Since the fractional relaxations of domination number and $2$-independence number coincide, a bounded ratio would allow both parameters to be approximated via their common fractional relaxation, computable in polynomial time. The problem has been studied for trees, strongly chordal graphs, dually chordal graphs, outerplanar graphs, and other sparse graph classes.
paper_title: Characterization of sparse monotone graph classes with bounded domination-to-2-independence ratio

title: Problem 2
statement_text: Which graph classes have domination tied to 2-independence?
context_text: A graph class $\mathcal{G}$ has domination tied to 2-independence if there exists a function $h$ such that $\gamma(G)\leq h(\alpha_{2}(G))$ holds for every graph $G\in\mathcal{G}$. This is a relaxation of Problem 1, which requires $h$ to be linear; the authors remark that even this relaxed version seems non-trivial.
paper_title: Characterization of sparse monotone graph classes with bounded domination-to-2-independence ratio

title: Problem 5.1
statement_text: For some integers $q\geqslant 4$ and $N$, does there exist a non-transitive $q$-edge-colored $N$-vertex tournament whose longest color-avoiding path has strictly fewer than $f_{q,q-1}(N)$ vertices?
context_text: For $q\geqslant 4$, Proposition 1.5 shows $F_{q,q-1}(n)=G_{q,q-1}(n)$, meaning the largest $(q-1)$-comparable set is always a $(q-1)$-increasing sequence and hence corresponds to a transitive tournament. Problem 5.1 asks whether a non-transitive coloring can do strictly worse than the transitive extremum. The answer is affirmative for $q=3$ via the Hamaker–Stein construction, but Proposition 1.5 rules out any vector-based construction for $q\geqslant 4$, leaving the problem open in that range.
paper_title: Color-avoiding directed paths in tournaments

title: Problem 4.1
statement_text: What is the maximum fractional chromatic number of a $d$-degenerate, $K_{r}$-free graph?
context_text: Given the main theorem of Martinsson [26] on triangle-free $d$-degenerate graphs and its many applications, the authors seek a generalization of Harris' conjecture for $K_{r}$-free graphs. One might guess the answer is $O_{r}\left(\frac{d\log\log d}{\log d}\right)$ by analogy with Johansson's bound for $K_r$-free graphs of maximum degree $\Delta$, but Theorem 1.6 shows this is not the case.
paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems

title: Problem 6.1
statement_text: Can the upper bound $2^{cd}$ on the number of vertices assumed in Theorems 1.1 and 1.2 be weakened to $e^{\omega(d)}$ (with a corresponding improvement of the upper bound on $\chi$ when $n=e^{\omega(d)}$)? What if we additionally assume that the graph has large (constant) girth?
context_text: The perhaps most important open problem left by the paper is whether the exponential requirement on the number of vertices in Theorems 1.1 and 1.2 can be further relaxed. Even improving the constant $c$ in Theorem 1.1 could resolve Erdős's problem on graph Ramsey numbers for all triangle-free graphs by combining with the trivial lower bound $r(G)\geq|V(G)|$.
paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems

title: Problem 6.2
statement_text: Is the following statement true for all $\varepsilon>0$? If $G$ is a $d$-degenerate triangle-free graph with maximum degree $\Delta(G)\leq e^{d^{1-\varepsilon}}$, then we have [statement truncated in source]
context_text: Resolving this problem would likely require localizing the random coloring process used in the paper so that the union bound can be replaced by an application of the Lovász Local Lemma. An affirmative answer would imply results on immersion analogues of Hadwiger's conjecture, as conjectured by Lescure and Meyniel in 1988.
notes: The conclusion of the problem statement is truncated in the provided source; only the hypothesis is visible.
paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems

title: Problem 6.3
statement_text: Is it true that if the chromatic number of a $K_{r}$-free $d$-degenerate graph is $d+1$, then the graph has $e^{\Omega_{r}(d)}$ vertices?
context_text: The main results of the paper establish that triangle-free $d$-degenerate graphs with at most $2^{cd}$ vertices have chromatic number strictly less than $d+1$; this problem asks whether the exponential lower bound on the order of extremal examples extends from the triangle-free case ($r=3$) to all $r$.
paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems

title: Problem 6.4
statement_text: Let $r\geq 4$. Is it true that the maximum fractional chromatic number of $K_{r}$-free $d$-degenerate graphs is $o_{r}(d)$?
context_text: Theorem 1.6 gives a lower bound of $\Omega(d/\log^{(r-2)}d)$ on the fractional chromatic number of $K_r$-free $d$-degenerate graphs, which approaches linear in $d$ as $r$ increases, leaving open whether the maximum is truly sub-linear for each fixed $r\geq 4$.
paper_title: Coloring small locally sparse degenerate graphs and related problems

title: Problem 1
statement_text: Can 3-colouring be solved on diameter-2 graphs in quasi-polynomial time?
context_text: The paper improves the algorithm for 3-colouring diameter-3 graphs, leaving the complexity of 3-colouring diameter-2 graphs as the main outstanding challenge. The authors note this is one of the two 'notorious' open cases identified by Paulusma, and that the polynomial-time solvability of 3-colouring on diameter-2 graphs is the most intriguing open problem in the area. The quasi-polynomial formulation is highlighted as an explicit intermediate target.
paper_title: Faster 3-colouring algorithm for graphs of diameter 3

title: Open Question on Circular Drawing Characterisation
statement_text: Is there a theorem that says ``every appropriately connected digraph $G$ contains no thing of type $X$ if and only if $G$ admits a circular drawing''?
context_text: Thomassen's theorem gives a characterisation of circular drawings in the presence of a specific 2-vertex separator. The authors generalise this into a motivating question and, despite fully characterising weightable digraphs in the paper, explicitly state: ``We still have not come up with the characterization we hoped for of the digraphs with circular drawings.''
notes: No labelled theorem environment; stated as a motivating open question in the introduction and explicitly left unresolved at the end of the introductory discussion.
paper_title: When all directed cycles have the same weight

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: For some (possibly, for every) dense $n$-element point set $A$ in general position in the plane, covering the edge set of $K_{n}[A]$ requires a superlinear number of monotone paths.
context_text: Theorem 1.3 shows that $O(n^{3/2})$ monotone paths suffice to cover $K_n[A]$ for any $\alpha$-dense point set $A$. The authors suspect this upper bound is not tight and that a superlinear lower bound holds. This is contrasted with the worst-case arbitrary point sets, for which a quadratic number of monotone paths is required.
paper_title: Covering Complete Geometric Graphs by Monotone Paths

title: Problem 5.1
statement_text: Does there exist a constant $c>1/2$ with the property that, for infinitely many values of $n$, there are $n$-element point sets $A$ in the plane such that every covering of the edge set of $K_{n}[A]$ by crossing-free paths requires at least $cn$ paths?
context_text: Pach, Saghafian, and Schnider proved that every complete convex geometric graph can be decomposed into $n/2 + o(n)$ plane subgraphs. It is open whether a linear lower bound strictly above $n/2$ holds for crossing-free path coverings over general point sets.
paper_title: Covering Complete Geometric Graphs by Monotone Paths

title: Problem 5.2
statement_text: What is the smallest number $z=z(n)$ such that the edge set of every complete geometric graph on $n$ vertices can be covered by $z$ plane zig-zag paths?
context_text: The authors show that for any finite point set $A$ in general position in the plane, one can cover the edge set of $K_n[A]$ by plane Hamiltonian zig-zag paths, but the minimum number of such paths needed in the worst case is left open.
paper_title: Covering Complete Geometric Graphs by Monotone Paths

title: Question 2
statement_text: Is it true that for every planar graph $H$, Max Independent Set can be solved in quasipolynomial time in the class of graphs excluding $H$ as an induced minor?
context_text: Question 2 is the quasipolynomial-time relaxation of Question 1, for which substantially more positive evidence exists: quasipolynomial-time algorithms are known for $C_t$-induced-minor-free graphs and for $tC_3$-induced-minor-free graphs. The present paper contributes a quasipolynomial-time algorithm for $tC_3 \uplus C_4$-induced-minor-free graphs, running in $n^{O(t^{2}\log n)+f(t)}$.
notes: No parenthetical attribution appears in the theorem header; treated as the paper authors' own question. It is the natural quasipolynomial companion to Question 1.
paper_title: Maximum Independent Set when excluding an induced minor: $K_1 + tK_2$ and $tC_3 \uplus C_4$

title: Conjecture 10
statement_text: The genus distribution of every graph $G$ is unimodal.
context_text: After disproving the LCGD Conjecture, the author notes that the counterexamples constructed are still unimodal. The author suggests that the unimodal weakening of the LCGD Conjecture could, and actually should, still be true.
paper_title: Strong log-convexity of genus sequences

title: Conjecture 11
statement_text: Every graph that triangulates some surface has log-concave genus distribution.
context_text: After proposing unimodality as a positive replacement for the disproved LCGD Conjecture, the author notes this stronger statement as also looking plausible, restricting log-concavity to graphs that triangulate some surface.
paper_title: Strong log-convexity of genus sequences

title: Conjecture 6
statement_text: For every $k\in\mathbb{N}$, every graph $G$ and $X,Y\subseteq V(G)$ satisfies one of the following: $G$ contains $k$ pairwise anti-complete $(X,Y)$-paths; or there exists a set $Z\subseteq V(G)$ with $|Z|\leqslant k-1$ such that $G-N_{G}[Z]$ has no $(X,Y)$-path.
context_text: The authors pose this $(X,Y)$-path strengthening of their main theorem (Theorem 3), seeking to reduce the separator size from $78(k-1)$ to the optimal $k-1$. If true, it would have immediate application to the study of induced minors, in particular implying an Induced Grid Minor Theorem for $K_{1,t}$-induced-subgraph-free graphs conjectured by Dallard et al.
paper_title: An Induced $A$-Path Theorem

title: Problem 1
statement_text: Find the minimal constant $\widehat{\rho}\in[0,1]$ such that each spectrally symmetric graph satisfies $\frac{2|E(G)|}{|V(G)|^{2}}\leq\widehat{\rho}$.
context_text: Motivated by the classical fact that a graph of order $n$ with more than $\frac{n^2}{4}$ edges is not bipartite (and hence its adjacency spectrum is not symmetric), the authors ask for an analogous density threshold for spectrally symmetric orientations. The paper establishes $\frac{13}{18}\leq\widehat{\rho}\leq\frac{10}{11}$ but the exact value remains open.
paper_title: Spectrally symmetric orientations of graphs

title: Conjecture 13
statement_text: Let $k$ be a positive integer. For every $0_{k}$-graph $G$ we have $\chi^{\prime}_{k}(G)\leq k+o(k)$.
context_text: A $0_k$-graph is a graph whose relevant subgraph structure satisfies a $0\pmod{k}$ degree condition. If Conjecture 13 holds, the proof strategy of Theorem 2 can be adapted so that a maximal $0_k$-subgraph $H$ of any graph $G$ is coloured with $\leq k+o(k)$ colours and the remainder (which would then be $(2q(k)-2)$-degenerate) is coloured via Lemma 9, yielding an overall bound approaching $\chi^{\prime}_{k}(G)\leq k+o(k)$ for all graphs.
paper_title: On Modular Edge Colourings of Graphs

also_stated_in.title: On Modular Edge Colourings of Graphs

title: Conjecture 7.1
statement_text: For every $t$, there is an output-polynomial time algorithm for Dom-Enum in incomparability graphs of $S_{t}$-free posets.
context_text: The algorithm of Corollary 5.3 already covers all comparability graphs of $S_{t}$-free posets. The paper asks what can be said about Dom-Enum in $H$-free incomparability graphs; since co-bipartite graphs are incomparability graphs, the question is only interesting for co-bipartite $H$. This conjecture is proposed as the natural counterpart of Theorem 1.2 for the $S_t$-free setting.
paper_title: Enumerating minimal dominating sets in the (in)comparability graphs of bounded dimension posets

also_stated_in.title: Enumerating minimal dominating sets in the (in)comparability graphs of bounded dimension posets

title: Conjecture 7.3
statement_text: For any co-bipartite $H$, there is an output-polynomial time algorithm for Dom-Enum in $H$-free incomparability graphs.
context_text: This is the broadest of the three conjectures in the concluding section, subsuming Conjecture 7.2. The authors note there is no blatant reason why the incomparability condition should be necessary, leaving open whether the result might hold for all $H$-free graphs with co-bipartite $H$.
paper_title: Enumerating minimal dominating sets in the (in)comparability graphs of bounded dimension posets

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: The unique maximal Schnyder wood of the UIHPT is the weak limit of the maximal Schnyder wood of a uniformly random triangulation from $T^m_n$ as $n \to \infty$ and then $m \to \infty$.
context_text: Theorem 1.2 establishes that the maximal Schnyder wood of the UIPT with boundary length $m$ is the weak limit of maximal Schnyder woods of uniformly random triangulations from $T^m_n$ as $n \to \infty$. The authors were unable to extend this convergence result to the UIHPT (letting $m \to \infty$ after $n \to \infty$), and leave it as a conjecture. They discuss potential consequences for geodesic paths and the winding behaviour of monochromatic trees in large finite triangulations.
paper_title: Infinite Schnyder Woods

title: Open question on Schnyder woods for general half-plane triangulations
statement_text: It would be interesting to know whether Schnyder woods exist on all half-plane triangulations, and if not, to characterise the ones for which they do exist.
context_text: The paper proves existence and uniqueness of the maximal Schnyder wood for all infinite triangulations with finite boundary (Theorem 1.1) and for the UIHPT specifically (Theorem 1.3), but does not treat arbitrary triangulations of the half-plane (infinite boundary). The authors note that whenever a maximal Schnyder wood of the half-plane exists it is unique, leaving existence for the general case open.
paper_title: Infinite Schnyder Woods

title: Open question on local limit of uniformly random wooded triangulations
statement_text: It would be interesting to understand the local limit of uniformly random wooded triangulations as the boundary length tends to infinity, and whether such wooded triangulations have a local limit, which would be a random wooded triangulation of the half-plane different from the one constructed in this work.
context_text: The authors remark that uniformly random wooded triangulations (triangulations paired with an arbitrary Schnyder wood) are quite different from maximal Schnyder woods of uniformly random triangulations: tree heights in the former are $\Theta(\sqrt{n})$ in probability, while the expected height of a uniformly random vertex in the latter is of order $n^{3/4}$. Whether these wooded triangulations possess a local limit as the boundary length grows is left open.
paper_title: Infinite Schnyder Woods

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: For every fixed $d$ the following holds for all $d$-norms $\|\cdot\|$ but a meagre set. For all $n>n_{0}(d)$, any set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ that do not all lie in an affine hyperplane determine at least $(d-o(1))n$ distinct distances with respect to $\|\cdot\|$, where the $o(1)$-term tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity.
context_text: The conjecture concerns distinct distances in typical $d$-dimensional norms; the space of $d$-norms is a Baire space under the Hausdorff metric on unit balls, so 'all but a meagre set' is a strong genericity condition. The paper notes this is open already for $d=2$, as the result in [1] only ensures $(1-o(1))n$ distinct distances; the paper proves a weaker version (Theorem 1.6) by combining Theorem 1.2 with the arguments of [1].
paper_title: Distinct Directions and Distinct Distances in $\mathbb{R}^d$

title: Question 5.2
statement_text: Is is true that for every positive integer $\mu$ there exists a polynomial $\mathsf{p}_{\mu}$ such that every $K_{t,t}$-free graph $G$ with $\operatorname{tree\textnormal{-}\mu}(G)\leqslant\mu$ satisfies $\operatorname{tree\textnormal{-}\alpha}(G)\leqslant\mathsf{p}_{\mu}(t)$?
context_text: Lemma 5.1 shows that the smallest integer $\mathsf{N}(s,s,2)$ appearing in the bound from Abrishami et al. is not bounded by any polynomial in both parameters, motivating the question of whether a polynomial bound in $t$ alone (for fixed $\mu$) is achievable. The question asks specifically whether tree-independence number can be bounded polynomially in the induced biclique exclusion parameter $t$ for graphs of bounded induced matching treewidth.
paper_title: Induced matching treewidth and tree-independence number, revisited

also_stated_in.title: Induced matching treewidth and tree-independence number, revisited

title: Question 5.3
statement_text: Is it true that for classes of graphs with bounded induced matching treewidth, tree-independence number is bounded from above by a polynomial function of the induced biclique number?
context_text: The induced biclique number of $G$ is the largest nonnegative integer $t$ such that $G$ contains an induced subgraph isomorphic to $K_{t,t}$. This question is an equivalent restatement of Question 5.2 using the induced biclique number, and is noted as closely related to recent work bounding tree-independence number in terms of clique-like parameters.
notes: Explicitly stated by the authors to be equivalent to Question 5.2.
paper_title: Induced matching treewidth and tree-independence number, revisited

title: Problem 1.1
statement_text: Determine the smallest $m$ such that in every $2$-coloring of the edges of the ordered complete graph $K_{m}$, there is a monochromatic non-nested matching of size $n$.
context_text: Two independent edges $ij$ and $st$ in an ordered complete graph are nested if $i<s<t<j$ or $s<i<j<t$. The answer is known to lie between $3n-1$ and $4n-2$; the presenting author conjectured (in prior work [2]) that the lower bound $3n-1$ is optimal. A Turán-type approach recently improved the upper bound to $(2+\sqrt{3})n$.
paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings

title: Problem 2.1
statement_text: Let $G$ be a random 4-regular graph. Is it true that the coefficient of $P_{G}$ at $\prod_{v\in V(G)}v^{2}$ is a.a.s. non-zero?
context_text: The graph polynomial $P_G$ is uniform and every monomial appearing with a non-zero coefficient has total degree $|E(G)|$; for a 4-regular graph the only relevant monomial is $\prod_{v\in V(G)}v^{2}$. The expected value of the coefficient is zero in the two Hamiltonian cycles model, so a proof would require an anti-concentration inequality.
paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings

title: Problem 3.1
statement_text: Let $G$ be a disjoint union of cycles. Let $H$ be a disjoint union of $K_{4}$'s. Is $G\cup H$ 4-colourable?
context_text: This is a special case of the general strong chromatic number question: if $G$ has maximum degree $d$ and $H$ is a disjoint union of $K_{2d}$'s, is $G\cup H$ $(2d)$-colourable? The quantity $2d$ would be best possible, and it is known that $G\cup H$ is $(3d-1)$-colourable when $H$ is a disjoint union of $K_{3d-1}$'s.
paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings

title: Problem 4.1
statement_text:
Is it true that every diamond-free claw-free cubic graph is strongly 6-edge-colorable?
In other words, is it true that $\chi^{\prime}_{s}(T(G))=6$ for every cubic graph $G$?
context_text: Lv, Li, and Zhang proved that every claw-free subcubic graph other than the triangular prism satisfies $\chi^{\prime}_{s}(G)\leq 8$. A partial solution by Han and Cui establishes strong 6-edge-colorability for truncated prisms.
paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings

title: Problem 5.1
statement_text:
If a cubic graph admits a $2$-homogeneous coloring,
then $4$ colors are always sufficient.
context_text: A $k$-homogeneous coloring is a proper vertex coloring in which exactly $k$ colors appear in the open neighborhood of every vertex. For bipartite cubic graphs it is known that at most 6 colors suffice for a 2-homogeneous coloring; the conjecture asks whether 4 always suffice whenever a 2-homogeneous coloring exists.
notes: Stated as a declarative conjecture inside a labeled Problem environment; a weaker bipartite version appeared in prior work [2].
paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings

title: Problem 5.2
statement_text: There is a finite number of connected bridgeless cubic graphs that do not admit a $2$-homogeneous coloring.
context_text: Not every cubic graph admits a 2-homogeneous coloring; for example, a graph containing a triangular prism with one edge of a triangle subdivided does not. The conjecture claims the set of exceptions is finite.
notes: Stated as a declarative conjecture inside a labeled Problem environment.
paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings

title: Conjecture 6.1
statement_text: Each bridgeless graph admits a $2$-flow $\varphi_{2}$ and a $4$-flow $\varphi_{4}$ such that for any edge $e$, if $\varphi_{2}(e)=0$, then $|\varphi_{4}(e)|\geq 2$.
context_text: The conjecture implies the 5-flow conjecture since $(5\varphi_{2}+\varphi_{4})/2$ is a circular 5-flow, and generalises Seymour's approach in the proof of the 6-flow theorem. The resulting pair is called a $1/2$-flow-pair, and the conjecture has been verified computationally for all cyclically 4-edge-connected snarks up to 34 vertices.
paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings

title: Problem 7.1
statement_text: Every bicritical snark is almost-$\mathbb{Z}_{2}^{2}$-connected and almost-$\mathbb{Z}_{4}$-connected.
context_text: A graph is almost-$A$-connected if all boundaries are admissible for group $A$ except $\partial f=0$. The property has been verified for the Petersen graph and both Blanuša snarks on 18 vertices. The conjecture emerged from exploration of the House of Graphs snark database.
notes: Stated as a declarative conjecture inside a labeled Problem environment.
paper_title: Open problems of the 33rd Workshop on Cycles and Colourings

title: Conjecture 1.1
statement_text: For a $3$-graph $H$, there exists a constant $c$ depending only on $H$ such that $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})\leq n^{c}$ for all $n$ if and only if $H$ is a subgraph of an iterated blowup of an edge.
context_text: The paper proposes a full classification of those $3$-graphs $H$ for which the off-diagonal Ramsey number $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})$ grows polynomially with $n$. The 'if' direction (polynomial upper bound when $H$ is iterated tripartite) was already known to Erdős and Hajnal; the 'only if' direction is new. The problem was first raised explicitly by the first author at an AIM workshop in 2015.
notes: First author (Conlon) posed the problem at a 2015 AIM workshop (ref [5]); the formal conjecture as stated here appears to be the paper's own formulation.
paper_title: When are off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers polynomial?

title: Conjecture 4.2
statement_text: If $k>s$ and $H$ is a $k$-graph which is $s$-tightly connected and not $k$-partite, then there exists a positive constant $c$ such that $r(H,K_{n}^{(k)})\geq t_{s}(n^{c})$, where the tower function $t_{i}(x)$ is defined by $t_{1}(x)=x$ and $t_{i}(x)=2^{t_{i-1}(x)}$ for all $i\geq 2$.
context_text: The authors describe this as 'likely difficult' and note it would extend Theorem 4.1 (proved in the paper). It posits a tower-height lower bound of $t_s$ for the Ramsey number of any $s$-tightly connected $k$-graph that is not $k$-partite, generalising the $k=3, s=2$ case established in Theorem 4.1.
paper_title: When are off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers polynomial?

also_stated_in.title: When are off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers polynomial?

title: Problem 1
statement_text: What is the minimum number of transpositions needed in a $t$-reachable network? What if all transpositions are of the form $(1,\cdot)$?
context_text: The authors determine the exact minimum for $t=2$ (Theorem 2) and give an upper bound of $(2+o_t(1))n$ transpositions for fixed $t\geq 3$, but the asymptotics remain open for large $t$. Lower bounds for star-transposition $t$-reachable networks are also given, but a gap between upper and lower bounds remains even in that restricted setting.
paper_title: Short reachability networks

title: Conjecture 1
statement_text: For $n\geq 2$, the minimum number of lazy transpositions in a 2-uniformity network is $2n-3$.
context_text: The authors construct a 2-uniformity network of length $2n-3$ using the sequence $(1,2,\tfrac{1}{2}),(1,3,\tfrac{2}{n}),(1,2,\tfrac{1}{2}),(1,4,\tfrac{2}{n-1}),\dots,(1,2,\tfrac{1}{2}),(1,n,\tfrac{2}{3}),(1,2,\tfrac{1}{2})$, and conjecture this is optimal. They remark that, if true, it would match nicely with selection networks.
notes: The paper states that after a preliminary version was posted on arXiv, Conjecture 1 was proven (by unspecified authors in the truncated text); it may no longer be open.
paper_title: Short reachability networks

title: Conjecture 6.1
statement_text: All orientations of odd cycles are avoidable.
context_text: In light of Theorem 1.1 (which proves all orientations of $C_3$ and $C_5$ are avoidable), the authors conjecture that the result extends to all odd cycles. A positive resolution would yield that every digraph of large minimum out-degree has a subdigraph with large minimum out-degree whose underlying graph has large odd girth, providing a directed analog to the existence of bipartite subgraphs of high minimum degree.
paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs

title: Question 6.2
statement_text: Are all digraphs which allow a height function not avoidable?
context_text: All digraphs for which the authors proved non-avoidability (including one-directed complete bipartite graphs and oriented trees) allow a height function. This observation motivates asking whether the existence of a height function fully characterizes non-avoidable digraphs.
paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs

title: Question 6.3
statement_text: Which orientations of $C_{4}$ are avoidable?
context_text: The simplest digraph allowing a height function for which avoidability is unknown is the orientation of $C_4$ consisting of two parallel directed paths of length two. The authors believe this is the hardest orientation of $C_4$ and expect its resolution to settle the question for all orientations.
paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs

title: Eulerian-avoidability characterization
statement_text: Which digraphs are Eulerian-avoidable?
context_text: A digraph $F$ is Eulerian-avoidable if there exists $d_F:\mathbb{N}\rightarrow\mathbb{N}$ such that every Eulerian digraph (where each vertex has equal out- and in-degree) with minimum out-degree at least $d_F(k)$ contains an $F$-free subdigraph with minimum out-degree at least $k$. This setting is highlighted as potentially yielding new insights complementing the regular digraph characterization proved in the paper.
notes: Stated as a prose question immediately preceding the numbered Question 6.4; no labelled environment.
paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs

title: Question 6.4
statement_text: Is every orientation of $C_{4}$ Eulerian-avoidable?
context_text: Restricting to Eulerian digraphs (where each vertex has equal out- and in-degree) may make avoidability questions more tractable. The authors ask whether the specific case of $C_4$, which is open in the general setting, becomes resolvable in the Eulerian host digraph setting.
paper_title: Extending Thomassen's conjecture to directed graphs

title: Question 1.2
statement_text: Could it be that $f(\ell)=O(\ell\sqrt{\log\ell})$, matching the bound for standard minors from [Kos82, dlV83]?
context_text: The authors define $f(\ell)$ as the smallest integer $\delta$ such that every graph of minimum degree at least $\delta$ contains $K_{\ell}$ as a cyclic minor. They prove $f(4)=3$, $6\leq f(5)\leq 8$, and more generally $f(\ell)=O(\ell^{2})$, and ask whether this quadratic bound can be improved to match the $O(\ell\sqrt{\log\ell})$ bound known for standard $K_\ell$-minors.
paper_title: Lollipops, dense cycles and chords

title: Question 4.1
statement_text: Is there a simple criterion for a sequence of vertices to be an active path of $K_{n}$?
context_text: The authors introduce active paths as a key tool in the lollipop method. They note that not every Hamiltonian path starting at $c_1$ is active: in $K_6$ with Hamiltonian cycle $(c_1,\dots,c_6)$, the path $Q=c_1c_2c_5c_4c_3c_6$ is not active (there are 6 non-active paths among the 120 starting at $c_1$). The question asks for a simple characterisation of activity.
paper_title: Lollipops, dense cycles and chords

title: Question 4.2
statement_text: Is there a simple way to compute all the active vertices in the cycle of an optimal lollipop?
context_text: Active vertices determine the number of distinct chords produced by the lollipop argument. In the lollipop argument, the number of distinct active vertices (and thus chords) increases with the girth $g$ of the host graph, motivating the search for an efficient procedure to identify them.
paper_title: Lollipops, dense cycles and chords

title: Question 4.3
statement_text: Is there a constant $c>0$ such that every graph with minimum degree 3 contains a cycle of length $\ell$ with at least $c\ell$ chords?
context_text: While high minimum degree guarantees cycles with many chords, the authors ask whether minimum degree 3 alone suffices to guarantee a cycle whose chord count is proportional to its length. The disjoint union of copies of $K_4$ shows one cannot simply require many chords without controlling cycle length, motivating the search for a linearly-chorded cycle.
paper_title: Lollipops, dense cycles and chords

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a class of connected graphs, closed under contracting edges and subdividing edges. For all $L,C$ there exists $C^{\prime}$ such that if there is an $(L,C)$-quasi-isometry from a graph $G$ to a member of $\mathcal{C}$, then there is a $(1,C^{\prime})$-quasi-isometry from $G$ to a member of $\mathcal{C}$.
context_text: Motivated by a theorem of Chepoi, Dragan, Newman, Rabinovich, and Vaxès showing that $(L,C)$-quasi-isometry to a tree implies $(1,C')$-quasi-isometry to a tree, the authors ask for which graph classes $\mathcal{C}$ such a multiplicative-to-additive distortion improvement holds. Closure under edge subdivision and edge contraction is identified as a necessary condition, and the conjecture proposes it is also sufficient. The paper proves a special case: when $\mathcal{C}$ is the class of graphs of bounded path-width.
paper_title: Asymptotic structure. II. Path-width and additive quasi-isometry

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: For every integer $k$ and every graph $G$ on at least $k$ vertices, if $$|E(G)|<k|V(G)|-\tfrac{k(k+1)}{2},$$ then $G$ admits a cut $X$ with $\chi(G[X])<k$. In particular, $\ell_{k}=k$.
context_text: Motivated by the confirmed cases $\ell_1=1$, $\ell_2=2$ (Theorem 1.1), and the conjectured $\ell_3=3$ (Conjecture 1.2), the paper's authors propose this bold generalization asserting that $\ell_k=k$ for all $k$. The main purpose of the paper is then to show that this conjecture is far from correct.
notes: This conjecture is introduced by the paper's own authors and immediately refuted within the same paper: Theorem 1.4 shows $\ell_k \leq (1+\varepsilon)\frac{k}{2}$ for all sufficiently large $k$, so that asymptotically $\ell_k \sim \frac{k}{2}$ rather than $\ell_k = k$.
paper_title: On cuts of small chromatic number in sparse graphs

title: Conjecture 21
statement_text: Circuit Distance is $\mathsf{NP}$-hard for polygons.
context_text: The authors focus on monotone circuit walks as most directly relevant to circuit augmentation schemes, but note the non-monotone variant is natural too. The Circuit Distance problem asks whether there is a circuit walk from $\mathbf{s}$ to $\mathbf{t}$ of length at most $k$ in a polytope $P=\{\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}\colon Ax\leq\mathbf{b}\}$. The authors remark that their proof techniques seem likely to extend to this non-monotone setting.
notes: Polygons are 2-dimensional polytopes; in this case circuit directions coincide with edge directions (as noted in Observation 9 of the paper).
paper_title: Short circuit walks in fixed dimension

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: For all $s \leq t$, $r_{K_s}(K_t) = \binom{r(K_t)}{s}$.
context_text: Motivated by a question of Sam Spiro asking whether an analogue of Chvatal's theorem holds for $r_{K_s}(K_t)$, the authors conjecture that the complete graph on $r(K_t)$ vertices minimises the number of $K_s$ copies among all $K_t$-Ramsey graphs. The paper verifies this conjecture for $s = 3$ and all sufficiently large $t$ as its main result (Theorem 1.3).
paper_title: Triangle Ramsey numbers of complete graphs

title: q-color generalization of Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: The $q$-color generalization of Conjecture 1.2 holds.
context_text: After noting that the $q$-color generalizations of Theorems 1.1, 1.3, and 1.4 all hold by the same proofs, the authors conjecture that the $q$-color analogue of the main conjecture (Conjecture 1.2) likewise holds.
notes: Stated in prose without a formal label. The paper text reads 'the q-color generalization of Theorem 1.2', which is almost certainly a typo for 'Conjecture 1.2', since no Theorem 1.2 exists in this paper. No explicit formula for the q-color version is given in the extracted text.
paper_title: Triangle Ramsey numbers of complete graphs

title: Question 6.1
statement_text: For every finite graph $G$, every ${w\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^{V(G)}}$, and every ${v\in V(G)}$, is there always a ${(w,v)}$-optimal sequence whose length is at most $2^{{\left\lvert{V(G)}\right\rvert}}$?
context_text: The authors prove that for finite graphs the maximum amount of tea at any vertex $v$ can always be achieved after a finite number of sharing moves, but the sharp upper bound on the length of a shortest optimal sequence is unknown. One natural approach—showing that an optimal sequence need not repeat any sharing move—is complicated by Proposition 6.2, which shows that there need not exist an optimal sequence in which no move is a subset of a later move.
paper_title: Sharing tea on a graph

title: Question 2
statement_text: Is it true that for every graph class $\mathcal{C}$ with polynomial expansion, there exists a polynomial function $f$ such that for every integer $r\geqslant 0$ and every graph $G\in\mathcal{C}$, $$\mathrm{scol}_{r}(G)\leqslant f(r,\mathrm{bn}_{r}(G))?$$
context_text: A positive answer to this question would be sufficient to answer Question 1 positively. The polynomial expansion assumption on $\mathcal{C}$ is essential: without it, $\mathrm{scol}_r(G)$ cannot be bounded by any function of $r$ and the depth-$r$ bramble number $\mathrm{bn}_r(G)$, even if the bramble number is replaced by $\mathrm{bn}_{g(r)}(G)$ for an arbitrary function $g$.
paper_title: Shallow brambles

title: Informal question: validity of Conjecture 1.2 for trees and planar graphs
statement_text: Is Conjecture 1.2 (Georgakopoulos–Papasoglu) true when $H$ is a tree, or more generally when $H$ is a planar graph?
context_text: After noting that Conjecture 1.2 was disproved in general with a counterexample $H$ containing a clique of size 15, the authors remark that 'there remains hope that 1.2 is true for some interesting graphs $H$', and parenthetically ask '(Trees? Planar graphs?)'. The paper's own main result handles trees but with a weaker conclusion (bounded line-width rather than no $H$ minor), so the original formulation of Conjecture 1.2 for trees remains open.
notes: Appears as a parenthetical remark in the introduction rather than a formally labelled environment.
paper_title: Asymptotic structure. III. Excluding a fat tree

title: Informal conjecture: coarse Menger for bounded genus
statement_text: It might be true that the coarse Menger conjecture holds for graphs of bounded genus.
context_text: The counterexamples to the coarse Menger conjecture constructed in the authors' earlier paper have unbounded genus. The present paper proves the conjecture for bounded path-width; the authors note that the bounded-genus case is open and mention [13] for some progress in that direction.
notes: Stated as a possibility in running prose ("it might be true … but this is open"); no formal conjecture label.
paper_title: Asymptotic structure. V. The coarse Menger conjecture in bounded path-width

title: Conjecture 1.2 (Coarse Menger conjecture for surfaces)
statement_text:
Let $\mathcal{S}$ be a fixed surface. For all integers $k,c\geq 0$ there exists $\ell>0$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a graph that embeds on $\mathcal{S}$ and let $S,T\subseteq V(G)$. Then either

$\bullet$ there are $k+1$ paths between $S,T$, pairwise $c$-distant; or

$\bullet$ there is a set $X\subseteq V(G)$ with $|X|\leq k$ such that every path between $S,T$ contains a vertex with distance at most $\ell$ from some member of $X$.
context_text: The original Coarse Menger conjecture (attributed to Albrechtsen, Huynh, Jacobs, Knappe and Wollan, and independently to Georgakopoulos and Papasoglu) was shown to be false for general graphs by the same authors (Nguyen–Scott–Seymour) in [11], with counterexamples that are highly non-planar (unbounded genus). Since the counterexamples rely on non-planarity, the authors propose that a surface-restricted version may still hold.
notes: The paper proves a special case (Theorem 1.3): the conjecture holds when $G$ is planar and all vertices of $S\cup T$ lie on the infinite region, with the blocking structure being at most $k$ connected subgraphs whose diameters sum to at most $200k^3c$.
paper_title: Asymptotic structure. VI. Distant paths across a disc

title: Open question on Theorem 1.2 with $L=1$
statement_text: We do not know whether 1.2 holds with $L=1$.
context_text: Theorem 1.2 extends the result of Berger and Seymour (Theorem 1.1) from trees to graphs of bounded tree-width, establishing that a graph admitting a tree-decomposition whose bags are each contained in the union of a bounded number of balls of bounded radius admits an $(L,C)$-quasi-isometry to a graph of bounded tree-width. The authors note that unlike the tree case (where $L=1$ is achievable), it is unknown whether $L=1$ suffices in the general bounded-tree-width setting.
notes: Stated as a parenthetical remark in the introduction rather than a labelled conjecture/question environment. Only the first 4000 characters of the introduction were provided; additional open problems later in the paper may not be captured.
paper_title: Asymptotic structure. I. Coarse tree-width

title: Conjecture 15
statement_text: There exist collections of sets $\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ such that [size/complexity conditions] and such that for every $|A|=s$ with $|A+A|\leq K|A|$, there exists $\ell\leq\log_{2}K$ and $F\in\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ such that $A+A\supseteq F$.
context_text: The authors believe the main covering lemma — showing sumsets of sets with small doubling contain dense subsets of low complexity — will have further applications beyond those discussed in the paper. This conjecture proposes an optimal or strengthened form of that covering lemma, and is motivated in part by a version of Lovett's question (from Green's list of open problems [14]) asking whether sumsets $A+A$ of dense sets contain large subsets that are iterated sumsets $B+B+B+B$.
notes: The statement text as extracted appears truncated: the clause 'such that and such that' suggests that the size or complexity conditions on the collections $\mathcal{F}_{\ell}$ were lost in the source extraction. The mathematical content may be incomplete or garbled.
paper_title: Random Cayley graphs and random sumsets

title: Problem 21
statement_text: Is there an infinite family $\mathcal{G}$ of finite graphs, not containing cliques or independent sets, such that for all $H\in\mathcal{G}$, there is no finite $H$-induced-saturated graph?
context_text: The paper establishes that every finite graph $H$ that is not a clique or independent set admits a countably infinite $H$-induced-saturated graph. In the finite setting the picture is far less clear: beyond $P_4$, cliques, and independent sets, no other finite graph $H$ is known to lack a finite $H$-induced-saturated graph, so it is open whether infinitely many such exceptions exist.
paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs

title: Problem 22
statement_text: For a finite graph $H$ and integer $n$, let $G_{H,n}$ denote the graph with the $H$-free graphs on vertex set $[n]$ as vertices, and with an edge between $G,G^{\prime}\in V(G_{H,n})$ if and only if $|E(G)\triangle E(G^{\prime})|=1$. For which $H$ is $G_{H,n}$ disconnected for all sufficiently large $n$?
context_text: An $H$-induced-saturated graph on $n$ vertices corresponds to an isolated vertex in $G_{H,n}$; disconnectedness is a weaker but potentially more tractable property. The authors note that $G_{P_4,n}$ is connected for all $n\geq 1$, so disconnectedness is not universal.
paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs

title: Problem 23
statement_text: For which finite tournaments $H$, does there exist a countable tournament $G$ that does not contain $H$ as subtournament, yet any tournament obtained from $G$ by changing the direction of a single arc does contain $H$?
context_text: The authors ask for a tournament analogue of induced saturation, where the natural perturbation operation is reversing arcs. They note that when $H$ is a directed triangle there is no finite $G$ but an infinite one exists (the rationals ordered by $<$), and that for the transitive tournament on 3 elements a finite example exists.
paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs

title: Conjecture 24
statement_text: Let $T$ be a finite tournament. If $T$ is not transitive, then there exists a countably infinite $T$-free tournament $S_{T}$ such that every locally finite perturbation of $S_{T}$ has an induced copy of $T$.
context_text: This is proposed as the tournament analogue of the paper's main Theorem 1. The non-transitivity condition is necessary because any tournament on $2^n$ elements contains a transitive subtournament on $n$ elements, so a transitive $T$ cannot be avoided in an infinite structure under locally finite perturbations.
paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs

title: Conjecture 25
statement_text: Let $k\geq 3$ be an integer, and let $H$ be a finite $k$-uniform hypergraph. If $H$ has both an edge and a non-edge, then there exists a countably infinite $k$-uniform hypergraph $G_{H}$ such that every locally finite perturbation of $G_{H}$ has an induced copy of $H$.
context_text: This is the strongest proposed extension of Theorem 1 to $k$-uniform hypergraphs, where a single perturbation consists of adding or removing a single hyperedge. The condition that $H$ has both an edge and a non-edge is the natural analogue of $H$ not being a clique or independent set.
paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs

title: Conjecture 26
statement_text: Let $k\geq 2$ be an integer, and let $c$ be a $k$-colouring of the edges of a finite clique $K$ such that there is an edge of every colour. Then there is a colouring $\chi$ of the edges of the complete countably infinite graph such that every locally finite perturbation contains a copy of $K$ with colouring $c$.
context_text: This is the proposed analogue of Theorem 1 for edge-coloured complete graphs, where a locally finite perturbation changes the colours on a nonempty locally finite subgraph. The condition that every colour appears in $c$ is the natural analogue of $H$ not being a clique or independent set.
paper_title: Infinite induced-saturated graphs

title: Open case: Coarse Menger Conjecture for c=2
statement_text: It is unknown whether Conjecture 1.1 holds when $c = 2$: for all integers $k \geq 1$ there exists $\ell > 0$ such that for every graph $G$ and $S, T \subseteq V(G)$, either there are $k$ paths between $S, T$ pairwise at distance at least $2$, or there is a set $X \subseteq V(G)$ with $|X| \leq k-1$ such that every $S$-$T$ path contains a vertex within distance $\ell$ of some member of $X$.
context_text: The authors note that the case $c = 3$ is of special interest because a positive result for $c = 3$ would imply one for all $c \geq 3$ (via taking the $c$-th power of $G$). They explicitly remark that 'the conjecture remains open when $c = 2$, and we have nothing to say about that case in this paper.'
notes: Stated as a passing remark in the introduction; no formal conjecture label. Refers specifically to the strong-form Conjecture 1.1 restricted to c=2.
paper_title: Asymptotic structure. IV. A counterexample to the weak coarse Menger conjecture

title: Problem 5.2
statement_text: Does there exist a $6$-regular $(4,1)$-graph?
context_text: This question is posed in relation to the still-open case $k=4$ of Erdős' problem. A $(4,1)$-graph is a $4$-vertex-critical graph containing no critical edge; the question asks whether a $6$-regular example of such a graph exists, motivated by structural properties of $4$-vertex-critical graphs established in the paper.
paper_title: Critical edge sets in vertex-critical graphs

title: Conjecture 3.4
statement_text: $t(n)=n-2$ if and only if $n$ is prime.
context_text: The function $t(n)$ denotes the diameter of the swap graph on cyclic permutations when all swaps (not necessarily adjacent) are allowed. Observation 3.1 and Proposition 3.2 together establish that the condition holds whenever $n=2$ or $n$ is an odd prime, providing one direction of the biconditional.
paper_title: Circular sorting

title: Conjecture 3.6
statement_text: If $n>2$ and $\pi\in S_{n}$ satisfies [the condition that $\operatorname{cyc}(\sigma)=2$ for every $\sigma\in\pi C_{n}$], then $n$ is prime and [\pi = \pi_{n,a}$] for some $a\in{\mathbb{Z}}_{n}^{\times}$.
context_text: Conjecture 3.6 is a structural strengthening of Conjecture 3.4: it characterises the permutations $\pi$ achieving $t([\pi])=n-2$ as exactly the permutations $\pi_{n,a}$ (defined via multiplication by $a$ in $\mathbb{Z}_n$) when $n$ is prime. The implication 'Conjecture 3.6 $\Rightarrow$ Conjecture 3.4' holds, and both have been verified for $n\leq 11$.
notes: PDF source — the hypothesis clause of the statement is garbled (a condition on $\pi$ is missing from the extracted text). The statement has been reconstructed from context.
paper_title: Circular sorting

title: Question 3.9
statement_text: Is the function $t(n)$ monotone?
context_text: The function $t(n)$ gives the diameter of the all-swaps graph on cyclic permutations of order $n$. Monotonicity would allow the bounds proved for prime powers to be transferred to composite integers; however, the authors note that adding a fixed point to a permutation can decrease its individual $t$-value, complicating a naive monotonicity argument.
paper_title: Circular sorting

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: There exists a constant $c$ such that for any positive integers $n$ and $\kappa_1 \geq \cdots \geq \kappa_n$, the partition polytope $\mathrm{PP}(\kappa_1, \ldots, \kappa_n)$ has diameter at most $\lceil 4\kappa_1/3 \rceil + c$.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes an upper bound of $\lceil 3\kappa_1/2 \rceil$ on the diameter of $\mathrm{PP}(\kappa_1,\ldots,\kappa_n)$, while the known lower bound (generalizing a construction from [6]) is $\lceil 4\kappa_1/3 \rceil$. The authors suspect the true diameter is closer to the lower bound, motivating this conjecture. They also note that the natural proof strategy—showing diameter at most 4 when $\kappa_1 = 3$—fails, so new techniques will be required.
paper_title: Improved Decomposition Bounds for Partition Polytopes and Odd-Covers

title: Informal Conjecture (second phase transition for all radii)
statement_text: It seems likely that for every radius $r$ there should be a second phase transition around some threshold $t=t(n)$. By which we mean that, if $p=\omega(t(n))$, then $G$ is with high probability reconstructible from its $r$-neighbourhoods, while if $p=o(t(n))$ and $p=\omega(n^{-\frac{2r+1}{2r}})$, then with high probability $G$ is not reconstructible from its $r$-neighbourhoods.
context_text: The paper establishes the existence of this second phase transition for all $r\geq 3$, but the question remains open for $r=1$ and $r=2$, for which only improved (but not matching) bounds are given.
notes: Proved within the paper for $r\geq 3$; remains open for $r=1,2$. The conjecture as universally quantified over all $r$ is therefore still open.
paper_title: Shotgun assembly of random graphs

title: Question
statement_text: [Full statement text was not included in the supplied input; the environment is labelled \texttt{[qu]\ Question} and, from context, concerns the exact threshold for $r$-reconstructibility of $\mathcal{G}(n,p)$ for $r\in\{1,2\}$, the two radii for which the paper provides only upper and lower bounds rather than a sharp threshold.]
context_text: The paper determines the sharp threshold for $r$-reconstructibility for every $r\geq 3$. For $r=2$ it improves bounds of Gaudio and Mossel by polynomial factors, and for $r=1$ it sharpens a result of Huang and Tikhomirov, but in neither case is the exact threshold pinned down.
notes: The labelled [qu] environment confirms an explicit open question is posed; its verbatim statement was not provided in the supplied paper content, so the statement_text above is inferred from context.
paper_title: Shotgun assembly of random graphs

title: Conjecture 6.2
statement_text: For $p>0.55$, among models in $\mathcal{D}_{\geq p}(Q_{3})$ the signs model minimises the probability that $Q_{3}$ is connected.
context_text: The signs model on $Q_3$ assigns each vertex independently '$+$' with probability $\theta$ and '$-$' otherwise, with an edge open iff both endpoints share the same sign (so each edge is open with probability $p=\theta^2+(1-\theta)^2$). Computational evidence strongly suggests this model minimises the connectivity probability of $Q_3$ for $p>0.55$; for smaller $p$ other models perform worse.
paper_title: Improved bounds for 1-independent percolation on $\mathbb{Z}^n$

title: Problem 6.4
statement_text: What is the value of $p_{\mathrm{giant}}$?
context_text: The paper establishes $p_{\mathrm{giant}}\leq 0.5847$ (when each edge of the hypercube $Q_n$ is open with probability at least $0.5847$ and $n$ is large, a giant component exists with high probability). The best lower bound remains $1/2$. Falgas-Ravry and Pfenninger conjectured $p_{\mathrm{giant}}=1/2$, but the exact value is unknown.
paper_title: Improved bounds for 1-independent percolation on $\mathbb{Z}^n$

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: For every connected graph $G$ of order $n$ and $p\geq 2$, $$\mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(G)\geq\mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(P_{n}).$$
context_text: The paper's authors pose this as the natural negative $p$-energy analogue of Conjecture 1 of Tang, Liu, and Wang. Together, Conjectures 1 and 2 generalize Conjecture 3 of Elphick, Farber, Goldberg, and Wocjan, since at $p=2$ both give the bound $n-1$ by symmetry of the path spectrum. The paper settles this conjecture for $p\geq 4$ via Theorem 1.
notes: The theorem-environment text captures only the preamble clause; the displayed formula $\mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(G)\geq\mathcal{E}^{-}_{p}(P_{n})$ is reconstructed from the stated analogy with Conjecture 1, the generalisation claim for Conjecture 3, and the fact that Theorem 1 (proved bound $\mathcal{E}_{p}^{-}(G)\geq n$ for $p\geq 4$, $G\ncong K_n$) is said to settle this conjecture for $p\geq 4$. The case $2\leq p<4$ remains open.
paper_title: Vertex Partitioning and $p$-Energy of Graphs

title: Question 1.4
statement_text: Is the class $\{G:\operatorname{mw}_{1}(G)\leqslant k\}$, with $k$ arbitrary, $\chi$-bounded?
context_text: The proof of $\chi$-boundedness (Theorem 1.2) uses merge-width at radius 2 in an essential way (specifically Lemma 4.1). It is unclear whether radius 1 suffices. A negative answer would require constructing a class of bounded radius-1 merge-width graphs with bounded clique number but unbounded chromatic number; such a class must contain arbitrarily large bicliques.
paper_title: $χ$-Boundedness and Neighbourhood Complexity of Bounded Merge-Width Graphs

title: Conjecture 1.7
statement_text: A class $\mathcal{C}$ has bounded merge-width if and only if every first-order transduction of $\mathcal{C}$ has linear neighbourhood complexity.
context_text: Reidl, Villaamil, and Stavropoulos showed that linear neighbourhood complexity characterises bounded expansion among subgraph-closed classes; Theorem 1.5 of the present paper establishes the left-to-right direction for bounded merge-width. Proving this conjecture would also imply that flip-width and merge-width are equivalent, as conjectured by Dreier and Toruńczyk [DT25, Conjecture 1.17].
paper_title: $χ$-Boundedness and Neighbourhood Complexity of Bounded Merge-Width Graphs

title: Question 4.1
statement_text: Do there exist infinitely many $3$-edge colourable cubic graphs $G$ such that for all edges $(u,v),(x,y)\in E(G)$, [remainder of condition not captured in source]
context_text: The authors verified computationally that the Biggs-Smith graph is the unique cubic edge-transitive graph on at most 166 vertices satisfying the key lemma's conclusion, and that F168D is the only other such graph on at most 300 vertices. This question asks whether infinitely many such graphs exist.
notes: Statement truncated in source data — the condition following 'for all edges $(u,v),(x,y)\in E(G)$' was not captured; likely a PDF extraction issue.
paper_title: A Counterexample to a Conjecture of Lovász

title: Question 4.2
statement_text: Does there exist a $d$-regular $d$-edge colourable graph $G$ where $d\geq 4$ such that for all edges $(u_{1},v_{1}),\dots,(u_{d-1},v_{d-1})\in E(G)$, [remainder of condition not captured in source]
context_text: After finding only two counterexamples to Lovász's conjecture among line hypergraphs of cubic graphs, the authors ask whether counterexamples exist for higher regularity $d\geq 4$, generalising the $r=3$ construction.
notes: Statement truncated in source data — the condition following the list of edges was not captured; likely a PDF extraction issue.
paper_title: A Counterexample to a Conjecture of Lovász

title: Conjecture 4.3
statement_text: For every $r$-partite $r$-uniform hypergraph $\mathcal{H}$ there exists some $k$ with $1\leq k\leq r-1$ such that $\mathcal{H}$ contains $k(r-1)$ vertices whose deletion reduces the matching number by at least $k$.
context_text: Proposed as a weakening of Lovász's (now disproved) conjecture that still implies Ryser's conjecture. The authors verified it holds for the line hypergraph of the Biggs-Smith graph, and note that only two counterexamples to Lovász's original conjecture are known, suggesting a slightly weaker statement may be true.
paper_title: A Counterexample to a Conjecture of Lovász

title: Question 4.4
statement_text: Is it true that if $\mathcal{L}$ is the line hypergraph of an $r$-regular $r$-edge colourable Cayley graph, then $\mathcal{L}$ contains $r-1$ vertices whose deletion reduces its matching number?
context_text: Both known counterexamples to Lovász's conjecture (the Biggs-Smith graph and F168D) are non-Cayley graphs. The authors observe that while symmetry aids in identifying counterexamples, it is unclear why not being a Cayley graph is relevant, motivating this restricted version of Lovász's conjecture.
paper_title: A Counterexample to a Conjecture of Lovász

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$ and size $m$. If $m\geq n+1$, then $$s^{+}(G)\geq n.$$
context_text: Motivated by the difficulty of proving Conjecture 1.1 inductively (because the bound $n-1$ is not preserved under subgraph removal), the authors ask when the strictly stronger bound $s^{+}(G)\geq n$ holds and propose this conjecture after computational investigation. It has been verified for all connected graphs up to 9 vertices and all graphs in the Mathematica database with $n\leq 100$ and $m\geq n+1$.
paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs

title: Conjecture 9.1
statement_text: If $G$ is a unicyclic graph of order $n$ whose cycle has odd length $k$, then $s^{+}(G)>n>s^{-}(G)$, if $k\equiv 3\mod 4$. $s^{+}(G)<n<s^{-}(G)$, if $k\equiv 1\mod 4$.
context_text: The authors compute $s^{+}$ and $s^{-}$ for odd cycles $C_n$ explicitly and observe a parity-of-$n$-mod-4 dependence. They extend this observation to all unicyclic graphs whose unique cycle has odd length and propose this conjecture.
paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs

title: Conjecture 9.2
statement_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$. $s^{+}(G)=n-1$ if and only if $G$ is a tree. $s^{-}(G)=n-1$ if and only if $G$ is a tree or a complete graph.
context_text: After establishing Conjecture 1.1 for several families, the authors propose a characterisation of the equality case $s^{+}(G)=n-1$ (resp. $s^{-}(G)=n-1$) in Conjecture 1.1.
paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs

title: Conjecture 9.3
statement_text: Let $G$ be a connected graph of order $n$ such that $s^{+}(G)=n$. Then $G$ is a bipartite unicyclic graph.
context_text: The authors believe equality never holds in Conjecture 1.2 for non-unicyclic graphs, and propose this characterisation of the equality case $s^{+}(G)=n$ based on computational evidence and the structure of bipartite unicyclic graphs.
paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs

title: Conjecture 9.4
statement_text: For any connected graph $G$ of order $n$ and $\omega(G)\geq 3$, we have $s^{+}(G)\geq n$.
context_text: This conjecture refines Conjecture 1.1 for graphs with clique number at least 3, motivated by the expectation that a large clique forces a large value of $s^{+}$. It would follow from the truth of Conjectures 1.2 and 9.1; the authors identify unicyclic graphs with a triangle as the main obstacle.
paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs

title: Conjecture 9.5
statement_text: The following hold: Let $G$ be a maximal planar graph of order $n\geq 10$. Then $s^{+}(G)\geq 3n$. Let $G$ be a maximal outerplanar graph of order $n\geq 8$. Then $s^{+}(G)\geq 2n$.
context_text: Elphick and Linz noted that Conjecture 1.1 holds for maximal planar graphs and asked whether $s^{+}(G)\geq 3(n-2)$ for such graphs. The present authors propose the stronger bounds $3n$ and $2n$ for maximal planar and maximal outerplanar graphs respectively, showing the coefficients are asymptotically tight via explicit constructions.
paper_title: Refinement of a conjecture on positive square energy of graphs

title: Question 1.7
statement_text: Let $t\geq 6$ be an integer. Does there exist a graph $G$ with $E(\overline{G})\neq\varnothing$ such that $G$ is $H$-free but $G+e$ has an induced subgraph isomorphic to $C_{2t-2}$ for every $e\in E(\overline{G})$?
context_text: The authors prove Theorem 1.6 constructing graphs that are $C_{2t}$-free but where removing any edge creates an induced copy of $C_{2t}$ (half of the induced saturation condition). Question 1.7 asks about the other half: whether the edge-addition condition can also be satisfied, which would be a key step toward solving the induced saturation problem for all even cycles.
paper_title: Halfway to induced saturation for even cycles

title: Question 1.8
statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial $f$ such that for every $t\geq 3$, there is a graph $G$ on at most $f(t)$ vertices with $E(G)\neq\varnothing$, such that $G$ is $H$-free but $G-e$ has an induced subgraph isomorphic to $C_{2t-2}$ for every $e\in E(G)$?
