context_text: The construction of $G_t$ in Theorem 1.6 has doubly exponential size in $t$. The authors remark they do not even have a rationale for expecting the number of vertices to be more than linear in $t$, making this a natural question about the efficiency of the construction.
paper_title: Halfway to induced saturation for even cycles

title: Open Problem: polytime $f(\mathrm{OPT})$-approximation for largest complete minor
statement_text: It is open whether finding the size of a largest complete minor admits a polynomial-time $f(\mathrm{OPT})$-approximation algorithm.
context_text: The best known polynomial-time approximation factor for the size of a largest complete minor is $O(\sqrt{n})$, due to Alon, Lingas and Wahlén [1]. No PTAS exists unless $\mathsf{P}=\mathsf{NP}$ (Wahlén [32]). The authors cite this open problem as direct motivation for studying the analogous question for complete interval minors in ordered graphs, where they obtain a polytime $f(t)$-approximation.
notes: Stated in the introduction as a known open problem without a labelled environment and without explicit attribution to specific authors; captured because the authors explicitly flag it as open and use it to motivate the paper.
paper_title: A Polynomial-Time Approximation Algorithm for Complete Interval Minors

title: Conjecture 1
statement_text: Every 5-critical graph in ${\cal C}$ contains a vertex of degree four.
context_text: The authors observe that 5-critical graphs have minimum degree at least four and that there is a natural way to handle degree-four vertices: if $G\neq K_5$ is 5-critical and $v$ has degree four, one can find non-adjacent neighbours $x,y$ of $v$ and contract them. Conjecture 1 is stated as a stepping stone toward the main Conjecture 2, which implies it because 5-critical graphs contain no separating cliques.
paper_title: On a conjecture concerning 4-coloring of graphs with one crossing

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: If a graph $G$ does not contain separating triangles and has a drawing in the plane with one crossing such that all vertices not incident with the crossed edges have degree at least five, then $G$ is 4-colorable.
context_text: This is the central conjecture of the paper. The authors verify it by computer enumeration for all graphs with at most 28 vertices, prove that it implies Conjecture 1, and note that the assumption on separating triangles is necessary. It generalises the Four Color Theorem to graphs drawable with one crossing under a minimum-degree condition.
paper_title: On a conjecture concerning 4-coloring of graphs with one crossing

title: Conjecture 5
statement_text: Every non-4-colorable graph in ${\cal C}_{0}$ has an internal vertex of degree four.
context_text: The class ${\cal C}_{0}\subseteq{\cal C}$ consists of graphs obtained from 4-connected plane triangulations by choosing two distinct facial triangles $xuv$ and $yuv$ sharing edge $uv$ and adding edge $xy$; vertices $x,y,u,v$ are external and all others internal. This conjecture is equivalent to Conjecture 2 (the main conjecture) and provides a more combinatorially explicit reformulation analogous to how the Four Color Theorem reduces to triangulations.
paper_title: On a conjecture concerning 4-coloring of graphs with one crossing

title: Conjecture 30
statement_text: Every bichromatic-forbidding 4-candidate can be obtained from the diamond by a sequence of the operations described in Lemmas 27, 28, and 29.
context_text: A plane graph with outer face bounded by a 4-cycle is bichromatic-forbidding if no 4-colouring assigns the same colour to opposite pairs of the bounding 4-cycle. A 4-candidate is a specific class of such graphs arising in the structural analysis of non-4-colourable graphs in $\mathcal{C}$. The conjecture proposes a complete recursive generation scheme for this class via three explicit expansion operations.
paper_title: On a conjecture concerning 4-coloring of graphs with one crossing

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: For every integer $k\geq 3$, there exists a set $X$ of vertices and a set $C$ of $k$-colorings of $X$ such that no $X$-rooted-$K_{k}$-minor-free graph $G$ realizes $C$.
context_text: The conjecture asserts that Theorem 3 (the generalization of the DeVos–Seymour result to $X$-rooted-$K_{k+1}$-minor-free $K_{k+2}$-minor-free graphs) does not further extend to $X$-rooted-$K_{k}$-minor-free graphs, i.e., reducible configurations cannot exploit the full global structure when the number of colors falls below what Hadwiger's conjecture postulates. Holroyd's conjecture — that if $G$ is $k$-colorable and every $k$-coloring uses all $k$ colors on $X$ then $K_k$ is an $X$-rooted minor of $G$ — would imply this conjecture if true in general.
paper_title: A note on extendable sets of colorings and rooted minors

title: Conjecture 1.13
statement_text: Let $p = p(n)$ be the probability of edges in $G(n,p)$. The expected number of faces in a random embedding of a random graph $G \in G(n,p)$ is $(1 + o(1))\ln(pn^{2})$.
context_text: In light of the paper's theorems for specific graph families and Monte Carlo experiments, the authors conjecture that a logarithmic bound holds for any usual model of random graphs. Extending the proof of Theorem 1.5 to arbitrary random graphs requires further ideas; Section 9 provides additional discussion.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

also_stated_in.title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.1
statement_text: For any $p(n) : \mathbb{N} \rightarrow [0,1]$, almost all graphs in $G(n,p)$ satisfy $\mathbb{E}[F] = O(\log(n))$.
context_text: Corollary 7.3 establishes a polylogarithmic bound for almost all dense graphs (when $p \geq 1/\mathrm{polylog}\, n$), and Section 8 gives a logarithmic bound for random sparse graphs. The authors conjecture that the logarithmic property holds without any density condition on edges. This conjecture would follow from the stronger Conjecture 1.13.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.3
statement_text: Let $G$ be a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum vertex degree $\Omega(n)$. Then $G$ satisfies $\mathbb{E}[F] = \Theta(\log(n))$.
context_text: Families of sparse graphs with maximum degree $O(1)$ achieving $\mathbb{E}[F] = \Theta(n)$ are known, but no dense graph example with that many average faces has been found. Theorem 1.8 confirms this conjecture for the complete graph $K_n$; the general dense case remains open.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.5
statement_text: The expected number of faces in a non-orientable random embedding of the complete graph $K_{n}$ is at most $\ln(n) + O(1)$.
context_text: The authors extend their investigation to non-orientable surfaces, where a random embedding is defined by choosing a random rotation system together with a random signature assigning each edge a sign independently with probability $1/2$. Computer simulations support a logarithmic behaviour analogous to the orientable case.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Conjecture 9.6
statement_text: Let $F^{-}$ be the random variable for the average number of faces in a non-orientable random embedding of some graph $G$. Then $\mathbb{E}[F^{-}] \leq \mathbb{E}[F]$.
context_text: The authors conjecture that the expected face count in a non-orientable random embedding is always at most that of the orientable random embedding, for any graph $G$. The inequality is verified for toy models (including the dipole and chains of triangles joined by cut edges), and an analysis of Random Process A gives the bound $\mathbb{E}[F^{-}] \leq \frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}[F] + 1$ for the dipole.
paper_title: Random Embeddings of Graphs: The Expected Number of Faces in Most Graphs is Logarithmic

title: Problem 1.4
statement_text: Determine the smallest value of $c$ for which the inequality $\chi(G)\leq c\cdot\chi^{d}(G\boxtimes K_{d+1})$ always holds.
context_text: Theorem 1.2 (for $d=2$) provides a graph showing that $c\geq\frac{30}{29}$ is necessary, while Theorem 1.3 gives the upper bound $c\leq 2$. A bootstrapped version of Theorem 1.2 further shows that assuming $\chi(G)$ or $d$ to be large cannot reduce the constant factor arbitrarily close to $1$.
notes: The theorem environment statement is truncated to 'Determine' in the source; the full problem is reconstructed from the immediately preceding context.
paper_title: Defective coloring of blowups

title: Question 1 (Intersectionwise χ-guarding)
statement_text: Which graph classes have the property that their graph-intersection with every $\chi$-bounded class of graphs is $\chi$-bounded?
context_text: The authors introduce the term "intersectionwise $\chi$-guarding" for such classes. They prove that classes admitting a certain decomposition are intersectionwise $\chi$-guarding, characterize single-forbidden-induced-subgraph intersectionwise $\chi$-guarding classes, and provide necessary conditions on $\mathcal{H}$ for the class of $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs to be intersectionwise $\chi$-guarding.
notes: Extracted from abstract only; no labelled theorem environments or introduction text were supplied. The abstract announces two central questions; the second question is truncated in the provided content and cannot be reliably stated.
paper_title: Intersections of graphs and $χ$-boundedness

title: Question 5.1
statement_text: Is it true that every oriented graph $G$ with $\delta^{0}(G)\geq k/2$ contains every orientation of the $k$-edge path, save for the antidirected orientations?
context_text: The tightness of Stein's conjecture for the antidirected orientation is witnessed by the $k/2$-blowup of the directed triangle. More generally, a regular tournament $T$ on $k+1$ vertices has $\delta^{0}(T)=k/2$ and, by Havet–Thomassé, contains every oriented $k$-arc path except (with three small exceptions) the antidirected one, suggesting that antidirected paths may be the only obstruction at the threshold $\delta^{0}(G)=k/2$.
paper_title: Two-block paths in oriented graphs of large semidegree

title: Conjecture 1.6
statement_text: Every subcubic triangle-free graph avoiding $F^{(1)}_{14}$, $F^{(2)}_{14}$, $F_{11}$, and $F_{22}$ as subgraphs is fractionally $19/7$-colorable.
context_text: The authors pose this as an intermediate conjecture between their main result (Theorem 1.4, giving $11/4$-colorability when only $F^{(1)}_{14}$ and $F^{(2)}_{14}$ are excluded) and Conjecture 1.3 (giving $8/3$-colorability when six graphs are excluded). It is noted that $F_{11}$ and $F_{22}$ both have fractional chromatic number exactly $11/4$, while $F^{(1)}_{19}$ and $F^{(2)}_{19}$ have fractional chromatic number $19/7$, motivating this intermediate bound.
notes: PDF source — the bound $19/7$ and the excluded subgraphs are stated clearly in the text.
paper_title: 11/4-colorability of subcubic triangle-free graphs

title: Question 1.1
statement_text: For which $\varepsilon>0$ and $k>0$ does there exist a bound $c$ such that each simple graph $G$ either contains $C_{2k+1}$ as a subgraph or $\chi(G)\leq c$ (or both)?
context_text: Erdős and Simonovits (1972) asked whether every graph with no fixed odd cycle and large minimum degree has bounded chromatic number, specifically for triangles and $C_5$. The paper authors present Question 1.1 as a generalization of those questions. Thomassen subsequently answered it affirmatively for $C_3$ at $\varepsilon>1/3$ and for all longer odd cycles at all $\varepsilon>0$.
notes: Presented as background context; the specific cases addressed by Thomassen [11,12] are resolved, but the fully general formulation is posed by the paper authors as a unifying question.
paper_title: Orientations of cycles in digraphs of high chromatic number and high minimum out-degree

title: Question 1.2
statement_text: Which orientations of cycles are contained in every digraph with high minimum out-degree and high chromatic number?
context_text: This is the central question of the paper, investigating a directed analogue of the chromatic threshold for cycles. The authors consider digraphs $D$ without loops or parallel arcs with minimum out-degree at least $\varepsilon|V(D)|$ for fixed $\varepsilon>0$. Theorem 1.3 in this paper fully answers Question 1.2.
notes: This question is fully resolved within the same paper by Theorem 1.3; included because it is a labelled question environment and the central research question.
paper_title: Orientations of cycles in digraphs of high chromatic number and high minimum out-degree

title: Question 4.1
statement_text: For which fixed digraphs $H$ does there exist a constant $c_{\varepsilon}\geq 1$ for all $\varepsilon>0$ such that every digraph $D$ without loops or parallel arcs with $\chi(D)\geq c_{\varepsilon}$ and minimum out-degree at least $\varepsilon|V(D)|$ contains $H$ as a subdigraph?
context_text: This is a more general version of Question 1.2, replacing cycle orientations by arbitrary fixed digraphs $H$. Theorem 1.3 characterizes the orientations of cycles for which this question is answered affirmatively. The authors also note that all subdigraphs of directed complete bipartite digraphs (with uniform arc orientation between parts) give an affirmative answer via a Ramsey-type argument.
paper_title: Orientations of cycles in digraphs of high chromatic number and high minimum out-degree

title: Question 4.2
statement_text: For a fixed digraph $H$, what is the minimum value $\varepsilon_{H}\geq 0$ such that there exists a constant $c\geq 1$ such that every digraph $D$ without loops or parallel arcs with $\chi(D)\geq c$ and minimum out-degree at least $\varepsilon_{H}|V(D)|$ contains $H$ as a subdigraph?
context_text: The quantity $\varepsilon_H$ provides a directed analogue of the chromatic threshold studied for undirected graphs. Theorem 1.3 may be interpreted as characterizing the orientations of cycles for which the directed chromatic threshold $\varepsilon_H$ equals zero. The authors also suggest studying variants where high chromatic number is replaced by high dichromatic number.
notes: The authors note that $\varepsilon_H = 1$ is always an upper bound, so the quantity is well-defined for all digraphs $H$.
paper_title: Orientations of cycles in digraphs of high chromatic number and high minimum out-degree

title: Question 1.6
statement_text: What is the infimum $c$ such that for any large enough $t$, there is a graph that admits a quasi-$K_{t}$-minor but no $K_{ct}$-minor?
context_text: Follows from the strong disproof of Conjecture 1.4. The paper establishes the bounds $\frac{1}{2}\leqslant c\leqslant\frac{2}{3}$; the trivial upper bound $c\leqslant 1$ comes from the fact that every quasi-$K_{2t}$-minor-free graph admits a $K_t$-minor.
paper_title: On a recolouring version of Hadwiger's conjecture

title: Question 1.7
statement_text: Is there a constant $c^{\prime}$ such that for every $t$, all the $c^{\prime}\cdot t$-colourings of a graph with no $K_{t}$-minor form a single equivalence class?
context_text: Theorem 1.3 shows $c'\geqslant\frac{3}{2}$ if such a constant exists. The best known upper bound comes from results of Kostochka and Thomason showing degeneracy $O(t\sqrt{\log t})$ for $K_t$-minor-free graphs, combined with the fact that all $k$-colourings of $d$-degenerate graphs are Kempe equivalent for $k>d$.
paper_title: On a recolouring version of Hadwiger's conjecture

title: Question 1.1
statement_text: Given a graph class $\mathcal{G}$, is there a constant $c_{\mathcal{G}}$ such that $$\frac{\gamma(G)}{\rho(G)}\leqslant c_{\mathcal{G}}\quad\text{ for each }\quad G\in\mathcal{G}~{}~{}?$$
context_text: The paper studies the ratio between the dominating number $\gamma(G)$ and the packing number $\rho(G)$ of a graph $G$. A positive answer immediately gives the bound $c_{\mathcal{G}}$ on the integrality gap of both the dominating and the packing integer programs for any $G\in\mathcal{G}$.
paper_title: On graph classes with constant domination-packing ratio

title: Question 9.1
statement_text: Characterize those graph classes $\mathcal{G}$ for which there is a constant $c_{\mathcal{G}}$ such that $\dfrac{\gamma(G)}{\rho(G)}\leqslant c_{\mathcal{G}}$ for each $G\in\mathcal{G}$.
context_text: In the conclusion, after extending both the list of graph classes with bounded $\gamma/\rho$ ratio and those with unbounded ratio (notably showing 2-degenerate graphs have bounded ratio), the authors note that Theorem 6.1 on 2-degenerate graphs rules out any characterization purely in terms of sparse graph classes.
notes: Statement text is partially cut off in the provided content; the formula is inferred from context and from the identical formulation in Question 1.1.
paper_title: On graph classes with constant domination-packing ratio

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: Let $G$ be a strongly 2-connected digraph drawn (possibly with crossings) in an annulus $\Sigma$, such that every directed cycle has winding number one around the annulus in the natural sense. Then $G$ can be drawn in $\Sigma$ without crossings, such that every directed cycle has winding number one.
context_text: This conjecture arises from an analogy with Thomassen's theorem on acyclic digraphs: gluing the north and south sides of a rectangle produces an annulus, and the question becomes whether a drawing with crossings in the annulus can be continuously deformed to a crossing-free drawing while preserving winding numbers of directed cycles. The author states explicitly that 'we cannot prove it.'
paper_title: When all directed cycles have length three

title: Informal Question (NP-characterization of weightable digraphs)
statement_text: Can we give a construction for all weightable digraphs (i.e., an NP-characterization)?
context_text: A digraph $G$ is weightable if one can assign a real weight $w(e)$ to each edge $e$ such that $\sum_{e\in E(C)}w(e)=1$ for each directed cycle $C$. The paper shows weightability is characterizable by excluded subdigraphs (giving a co-NP characterization), but an NP-characterization via explicit construction remains open. For strongly 2-connected digraphs, Conjecture 1.2 would provide such a construction.
notes: Stated as prose question in the introduction without a labelled environment.
paper_title: When all directed cycles have length three

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: For any subcubic graph $H$, $H$-\textsc{ISC} is in $\mathsf{P}$ if and only if $H$ is planar.
context_text: The conjecture is proposed by the paper's authors as a natural open question following their proof that a specific non-planar subcubic graph yields NP-complete induced subdivision containment. It complements known polynomial-time algorithms for planar induced subdivision detection (e.g., for the net and $K_{4}$) and the new hardness results of the paper.
notes: The 'only if' direction (NP-hardness for non-planar subcubic $H$) implicitly assumes $\mathsf{P}\neq\mathsf{NP}$.
paper_title: Induced Disjoint Paths Without an Induced Minor

title: Informal Question on Complexity Gap Between Induced Disjoint Paths Variants
statement_text: Is there a hereditary graph class in which Induced $k$-Disjoint Paths is NP-complete but Induced Disjoint $S$–$T$ Paths with $|S|=|T|=k$ is polynomial-time solvable? The case $k=2$ is of particular interest.
context_text: The authors observe a structural asymmetry: polynomial-time algorithms in the literature tend to apply more broadly to Induced $k$-Disjoint Paths (the linkage variant), while hardness proofs tend to apply more broadly to Induced Disjoint $S$–$T$ Paths (the flow variant with $|S|=|T|=k$). This raises the question of whether the two problems can be separated in complexity on some hereditary class.
notes: Appears in running prose with 'we wonder if' in the preamble to the open questions section (context before Conjecture 1.5); no labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Induced Disjoint Paths Without an Induced Minor

title: Problem 4.1
statement_text: Fix $r,d\geq 2$. Is $R_{2}(2,n)$ superexponential in $n$?
context_text: Theorem 1.1 gives a doubly exponential upper bound on the $d$-dimensional Ramsey numbers $R_r(d,n)$, while the best known lower bound is only singly exponential (from a random colouring argument). Closing this gap—determining whether a simple exponential upper bound suffices or the true growth is faster—is identified as a key open problem.
paper_title: A multidimensional Ramsey Theorem

title: Problem 4.2
statement_text: Does there exist $C>0$ such that $\log R_{2}(2,n)\leq(\log(M_{2}(n))^{C}$?
context_text: Since $M_2(n)\leq R_2(2,n)$ for every $n$, the authors ask whether the two-dimensional Ramsey number $R_2(2,n)$ can be bounded above by a fixed power of $\log M_2(n)$, where $M_2(n)$ is the two-dimensional Erdős–Szekeres number. A singly-to-doubly exponential gap persists for both quantities.
notes: The statement as supplied appears to have a missing closing parenthesis: $(\log(M_{2}(n))^{C}$ is reproduced verbatim from the source.
paper_title: A multidimensional Ramsey Theorem

title: Conjecture 1.7
statement_text: Every triangle-free graph $G$ satisfies $\chi(G)\leq(1+o(1))\frac{\rho(G)}{\ln\rho(G)}$.
context_text: Motivated by Harris's conjecture [27] that every triangle-free $d$-degenerate graph satisfies $\chi_f(G)\leq O(\frac{d}{\ln d})$ and by Molloy's bound $(1+o(1))\frac{\Delta(G)}{\ln\Delta(G)}$ in terms of the maximum degree. Since the spectral radius $\rho(G)$ is sandwiched between the degeneracy and the maximum degree, Theorem 1.6 of this paper (a new spectral upper bound on $\chi_f$) is presented as a first step toward interpolating between these two regimes.
paper_title: Local Shearer bound

title: Conjecture 5.1
statement_text: There exists $C>0$ and a two-coloring of $\mathbb{F}_{2}^{n}\setminus\{0\}$ such that there is no subspace $H$ of size $Cn$ for which all nonzero elements of the subspace are monochromatic.
context_text: Alon's conjecture remains wide open for $\mathbb{F}_{2}^{n}$ and $\mathbb{F}_{3}^{n}$. The finite unions theorem rules out a two-coloring with no monochromatic subspace at all over $\mathbb{F}_{2}^{n}$ for large $n$, so progress requires understanding the quantitative threshold. Establishing this conjecture would be an important step toward proving Alon's conjecture for $\mathbb{F}_{2}^{n}$, though the true bound on the minimum monochromatic subspace size may be considerably smaller.
paper_title: On the clique number of random Cayley graphs and related topics

title: Question 8.3
statement_text: For which sets $\mathcal{S}$ of strings is it true that there is a constant $c=c(\mathcal{S})\in\mathbb{N}$ such that every $c$-padded string $S$ contains either a string in $\mathcal{S}$ or the reverse of a string in $\mathcal{S}$ as a consecutive substring?
context_text: This question arises in the special case where every graph in $\mathcal{H}$ is a $c'$-tassel for some $c'\in\mathbb{N}$, where each tassel has a unique string $\mathcal{S}_{H,v}$ associated to each neck $v$. A set $\mathcal{S}$ satisfying the condition is called a $c$-unavoidable language, and identifying minimal such languages is key to characterising tasselled families. The only singleton $c$-unavoidable languages are $\mathcal{S}=\{0\cdots 01\}$ (up to reversal) and $\mathcal{S}=\{0\cdots 0\}$.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XIII. Basic obstructions in $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs for finite $\mathcal{H}$

also_stated_in.title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XIII. Basic obstructions in $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs for finite $\mathcal{H}$

title: Problem 1.1
statement_text:
Determine or estimate the functions 
(i) $m(d)$,     (ii) $m^{\prime}(d)$,
    (iii) $f(d)$,
    (iv) $f_{\leq 2}(d).$
context_text: $Q_d := \{0,1\}^d$ is the $d$-dimensional hypercube; $f(d)$ counts partitions of its vertex set into subhypercubes; $m(d)$ and $m^{\prime}(d)$ count perfect matchings and all matchings respectively. Problem (iii) was originally raised by Gadouleau at the 29th British Combinatorial Conference in 2022; problem (iv) is introduced by the authors as a closely related variant.
notes: Part (iii) attributed to Gadouleau (2022, unpublished talk); parts (i), (ii), (iv) are the authors' own formulations.
paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes

title: Problem 1.2
statement_text:
What is the order of magnitude of the
following ratios? In particular, which one of them is an
exponential function of $n$? 
(i) $m^{\prime}(d)/m(d)$,   (ii) $f_{\leq 2}(d)/m^{\prime}(d)$,
  (iii) $f(d)/f_{\leq 2}(d),$    (iv) $f(d)/m(d)$.
context_text: The hierarchy $m(d) \leq m^{\prime}(d) \leq f_{\leq 2}(d) \leq f(d)$ holds by definition, where $n = 2^{d-1}$. The paper proves that $f(d)/m(d)$ (part iv) is exponential in $n$, and shows part (i) is subexponential; parts (ii) and (iii) remain open.
paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes

title: Problem 1.9
statement_text: Determine the asymptotic behaviour of the number $f_{2}(d).$
context_text: It is natural to ask what happens if only $2$-dimensional subcubes are allowed in a partition of $Q_d$. The authors believe the same asymptotic bound as given by Proposition 1.7 (for general $r$) holds with $r=2$.
paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes

title: Informal conjecture on $f_2(d)$
statement_text: We believe that the same bound as in Proposition 1.7, with $r=2$, holds [for $f_2(d)$].
context_text: Introduced immediately before Problem 1.9 as motivation for that problem. Proposition 1.7 provides asymptotic bounds for $f_S(d)$ for general fixed dimension $r$; the authors conjecture the $r=2$ case obeys the same estimate.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled environment, directly preceding Problem 1.9.
paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes

title: Problem 1.10
statement_text:
Is it true that
$f(d)/f_{\leq 2}(d)$ is subexponential in $n$?
context_text: This addresses Problem 1.2 (iii), asking whether the gap between counting all subcube partitions and counting those restricted to subcubes of dimension at most 2 grows at most subexponentially in $n = 2^{d-1}$.
paper_title: Partitioning the hypercube into smaller hypercubes

title: Problem 6
statement_text: Let $\alpha$ be a positive real number. Does there exist a polynomial $P_{\alpha}$ such that $\operatorname{unvd}(D)\leqslant P_{\alpha}(|V(D)|)$ for every digraph with maximum average degree at most $\alpha$?
context_text: Fox, He, and Wigderson showed that acyclic digraphs with bounded maximum degree are not linearly unavoidable, ruling out linear bounds. This problem asks whether the weaker polynomial unavoidability still holds for digraphs with bounded maximum average degree, and is complemented by the second question of which families of such digraphs are linearly unavoidable.
paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments

title: Conjecture 7
statement_text: If $\cal F$ is linearly unavoidable and has bounded maximum average degree, then ${\cal F}[k]$ is also linearly unavoidable.
context_text: This conjecture asks whether the $k$-blow-up operation preserves linear unavoidability. The bounded maximum average degree condition is necessary, as shown by an example using disjoint unions of transitive tournaments. The paper proves this conjecture holds for the family of oriented trees.
paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments

title: Problem 8
statement_text: What is the infimum of all the constants $C$ such that for every large enough integer $n$, the $k$-blow-up of every oriented tree of order $n$ is $(C^{k}\cdot kn)$-unavoidable?
context_text: The paper proves in Theorem 18 that the $k$-blow-up of an oriented tree of order $n$ is $(2^{10+18k}\cdot kn)$-unavoidable, but this upper bound is acknowledged to be far from tight. The maximum average degree of the $k$-blow-up of a tree provides a lower bound via Proposition 2.
paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments

title: Conjecture 9
statement_text: There exists a constant $C$ such that $\operatorname{unvd}(D)\leqslant C\cdot\operatorname{unvd}(D-v)$ for every acyclic digraph $D$ and for every vertex $v$ of $D$.
context_text: This conjecture generalizes Proposition 1, which gives $\operatorname{unvd}(D)\leqslant 2\operatorname{unvd}(D-x)$ only when $x$ is a source or sink of $D$, to arbitrary vertices. Together with Sumner's Conjecture 4, it implies Conjecture 10 about $k$-extensions of oriented trees.
paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments

title: Conjecture 10
statement_text: There is an absolute constant $C$ such that for every integer $k$, every $k$-extension of an oriented tree of order $n$ is $C^{k}(2n-2)$-unavoidable.
context_text: This conjecture is implied by Conjecture 9 (multiplicative stability under vertex addition) together with Sumner's Conjecture 4 (that oriented trees are $(2n-2)$-unavoidable). The paper proves a weaker result in Corollary 25, establishing $(2\cdot 3^{\binom{2k+2}{2}}\cdot|V(F)|)$-unavoidability for $k$-extensions of sufficiently large oriented trees.
paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments

title: Conjecture 11
statement_text: If $\cal F$ is linearly unavoidable, then the family of $k$-extensions of digraphs in $\cal F$ is also linearly unavoidable.
context_text: This conjecture, which would follow from Conjecture 9, asserts that the $k$-extension operation preserves linear unavoidability in general. The paper proves this for the specific family of oriented trees (and oriented forests) in Corollary 25.
paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments

title: Problem 12
statement_text: What is the minimum function $f$ such that every $k$-extension of every forest of order $n$ is $(2^{f(k)}\cdot n)$-unavoidable?
context_text: This is the analogue of Problem 8 for extensions rather than blow-ups. Corollary 25 gives $f(k)\leqslant\binom{2k+2}{2}$ (quadratic upper bound), while Proposition 28 shows that the unavoidability of some $k$-extensions of the arcless digraph on $n$ vertices is $2^{k}n-o(n)$, giving a linear lower bound on $f$.
paper_title: Blow-ups and extensions of trees in tournaments

title: Conjecture on $\lambda(d,1)$
statement_text: $\lambda(d,1)=(1+o(1))/e$ where the $o(1)$-term tends to $0$ as $d$ tends to infinity.
context_text: The lower bound in Theorem 2, $(1-2^{-d})^{2^{d}-1}$, approaches $e^{-1}\approx 0.37$ as $d\to\infty$. The authors suspect this lower bound is asymptotically sharp, making $1/e$ the true limit of $\lambda(d,1)$.
notes: Stated in the abstract with the words 'We suspect'; no labelled conjecture environment.
paper_title: On hypercube statistics

title: Open problem on tight bounds for $\lambda(d,s)$
statement_text: Obtain tight bounds for $\lambda(d,s)$ for essentially all admissible values of $d$ and $s$.
context_text: The paper proves several upper and lower bounds on $\lambda(d,s)$ and determines the exact set of $s$ for which $\lambda(d,s)=1$, but tight asymptotic characterisation of $\lambda(d,s)$ for general $s$ remains open.

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: For every graph $H$, $\chi_{\_{\Delta}}(\mathcal{G}_{\_{H}}^{\text{odd}}) = \chi_{\_{\star}}(\mathcal{G}_{\_{H}}^{\text{odd}}) = \overline{\operatorname{td}}(H)-1$.

Conjecture 2 posits that both the defective and clustered chromatic numbers of $H$-odd-minor-free graphs equal the lower bound $\overline{\operatorname{td}}(H)-1$, closing the exponential gap left by the main theorem.

title: Open question on strong 2-kernels in split digraphs
statement_text: Does every split digraph $G$ with no sources admit a strong 2-kernel of size at most $|G|/2$?
context_text: The paper proves (Theorem 2.4) that every split digraph with no sources has a 2-kernel of size at most $|G|/2$. A 2-kernel $K$ is called strong if for every vertex $v$ in the tournament part $T$, either some vertex of $K$ 1-covers $v$, or some vertex of $K \cap T$ 2-covers $v$. The authors note in passing that they do not know whether the size bound survives under this strengthening.

title: Open Problem (Spherical Density Regime)
statement_text: Determine whether efficient constant-factor approximation algorithms exist for the genus of graphs with average degree at most 6 (spherical density regime), or prove that approximating the genus in this regime is hard.
context_text: The authors partition the genus approximation problem into three density regimes. The spherical density regime (average degree $\leq 6$) is described as 'probably most interesting'; no efficient constant-factor approximation algorithm is known for it, and the authors identify resolving this as 'the major open problem'. Conjecture 1.1 (APX-hardness for cubic graphs) is the leading candidate resolution.

title: Problem 1.5
statement_text: Let $k\geq 5$. Is there a graph $G_{k}$ with $\omega(G_{k})<5$, $\zeta(G_{k})=k$ and $\chi(G_{k})=\zeta(G_{k})+3$?
context_text: After disproving Conjecture 1.2, the authors ask whether the gap $\chi(G)-\zeta(G)$ can reach $3$ for graphs with $\omega(G)<5$ and arbitrarily large cochromatic number $\zeta(G)$. Theorem 1.4 guarantees such graphs exist with gap at least $2$ for all large $k$, but gap $3$ remains open.

statement_text: For any fixed integer $c \geq 2$, there exists an integer $t$ such that for infinitely many graphs $H$ with treewidth $t$ and paths $P$, every $c$-colouring of $H \boxtimes P$ has clustering $\Omega(|V(H \boxtimes P)|^{c/(c^2-c+1)})$.

The authors believe this upper bound is asymptotically tight, and furthermore conjecture that there exists a matching construction where the bounded-degree graph is a path.

statement_text: There exists a positive integer $t$ such that for infinitely many graphs $H_1, H_2$ with treewidth $t$, every $3$-colouring of $H_1 \boxtimes H_2$ has clustering $\Omega(|V(H_1 \boxtimes H_2)|^{4/7})$.

The authors conjecture the $4/7$ exponent in the upper bound is asymptotically tight.

title: Open problem: convex drawings vs. pseudocircular drawings
statement_text: The relation between convex drawings and pseudocircular drawings remains open.
context_text: Arroyo, Richter, and Sunohara proved that hereditary-convex drawings are precisely the pseudospherical drawings (i.e., pseudocircular drawings satisfying two additional properties). The analogous characterisation for (non-hereditary) convex drawings in terms of pseudocircular drawings has not been established.

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: A graph drawn on the torus is critical for 5-choosability if and only if it is $6$-critical.
context_text: Theorem 1 establishes this result under the additional assumption that there exists a cyclic system of non-contractible triangles around the torus with consecutive triangles at distance at most four. The authors verified that $K_7$ is the only graph critical for 5-list coloring but not for 5-choosability satisfying the assumptions of Theorem 1, and believe the cyclic system assumption can be dropped entirely.
paper_title: Towards Characterization of 5-List-Colorability of Toroidal Graphs

title: Conjecture 3
statement_text: A toroidal graph is $5$-choosable if and only if it is $5$-colorable.
context_text: This conjecture is stated as equivalent to Conjecture 2 and represents the central goal of the paper: a complete choosability analogue of Thomassen's characterization of 5-colorability on the torus. The computational results of the paper, enumerating minimal obstructions under a cyclic-triangle-system condition, provide supporting evidence.
paper_title: Towards Characterization of 5-List-Colorability of Toroidal Graphs

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: Every graph drawn on the torus with edge-width at least four is 5-choosable.
context_text: This is proposed as an important intermediate step towards Conjecture 3, focusing on toroidal graphs with no non-contractible cycle of length less than four. Postle proved that the edge-width of a graph critical for 5-list coloring is at most logarithmic in its genus, so the conjecture holds when four is replaced by a sufficiently large constant.
paper_title: Towards Characterization of 5-List-Colorability of Toroidal Graphs

title: Conjecture 8
statement_text: There is no critical prism-canvas of spacing at least five.
context_text: This conjecture arises from the paper's computer-assisted enumeration: using the classification of chordless critical cycle-canvases, the authors obtained 510, 2719, 300, and 5 candidates for critical prism-canvases of spacing at most four (after eliminating provably non-critical ones), and conjecture the list is empty at spacing five and beyond.
paper_title: Towards Characterization of 5-List-Colorability of Toroidal Graphs

title: Open Problem: Polynomial-time MWIS on even-hole-free graphs
statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial-time algorithm for the Maximum Weight Independent Set problem on even-hole-free graphs?
context_text: On even-hole-free graphs, Maximum Weight Clique is known to be polynomial-time solvable, but the question of whether a polynomial-time algorithm exists for Maximum Weight Independent Set remains open. This is in stark contrast to perfect graphs, for which polynomial-time algorithms have been known since 1981. The present paper gives a quasi-polynomial-time algorithm via a polylogarithmic tree independence number bound, but the polynomial-time question is not resolved.
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction without a labelled theorem environment; the paper provides quasi-polynomial-time progress but leaves polynomial-time open.
paper_title: Tree Independence Number IV. Even-hole-free Graphs

title: Open Problem: Polynomial-time Coloring on even-hole-free graphs
statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial-time algorithm for the Coloring problem (computing the chromatic number) on even-hole-free graphs?
context_text: On even-hole-free graphs, the question of whether a polynomial-time algorithm for Coloring exists remains open, in stark contrast to perfect graphs where polynomial-time algorithms have been known since 1981. The paper's quasi-polynomial-time results via tree independence number do not resolve this question.
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction without a labelled theorem environment; paired with the MWIS open problem as long-standing open questions for even-hole-free graphs.
paper_title: Tree Independence Number IV. Even-hole-free Graphs

title: Question 6.1
statement_text: Does every graph $G$ have a spanning subgraph with an Alon-Tarsi orientation in which each vertex $v$ has out-degree at least $\frac{1}{2}(\deg_{G}(v)-1)$?
context_text: This question asks whether Conjecture 1.1 could be proved for every graph via the paper's Theorem 1.4 by finding a suitable Alon-Tarsi orientation, which would yield a stronger result. An affirmative answer would further imply that every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices and maximum degree $\Delta$ has a subgraph $H$ with at least $|E(G)|-n/2$ edges satisfying $\operatorname{AT}(H)\leq\lfloor\frac{1}{2}\Delta+\frac{3}{2}\rfloor$.
paper_title: List-avoiding orientations

title: Question 6.2
statement_text: Does there exist a constant $C$ such that every graph $G$ of maximum degree $\Delta$ has a matching $M$ such that $\operatorname{AT}(G-M)\leq\frac{1}{2}\Delta+C$?
context_text: This question is motivated by results showing that removing a matching from a graph can reduce upper bounds on coloring parameters such as the Alon-Tarsi number. It is noted that $C$ cannot be smaller than $\frac{1}{2}$, since for any odd $n$ and any maximum matching $M$ in $K_n$, $\operatorname{AT}(K_n-M)=\frac{n+1}{2}$. The authors ask specifically about matchings, inspired by Zhu's result on planar graphs and the result of Grytczuk and Zhu.
paper_title: List-avoiding orientations

title: Conjecture 4.2
statement_text: For any $1\leq s\leq\frac{\sqrt{r}}{\log r}$ and $n$ large enough we have $f(n,r,s)=\frac{s^{2}(\log r)^{1-o_{r}(1)}}{r}\cdot n$.
context_text: The paper determines $f(n,r,s)$ — the maximum number of vertices guaranteably connected using up to $s$ colours in any $r$-edge colouring of $K_n$ — up to a logarithmic factor. The upper and lower bounds in the regime $s\leq\sqrt{r}/\log r$ differ by an absolute constant between 4 and 8 depending on divisibility properties of $r$; this conjecture posits the precise asymptotic behaviour in that regime. The authors note the gap originates in the proof technique and that under appropriate divisibility assumptions the result can be sharpened to within a factor of $2+\varepsilon$.
paper_title: The power of many colours

title: Question 4.3
statement_text: [Verbatim statement not available in the provided input excerpt.]
context_text: After establishing that the functions $f$ and $g$ behave differently for $s\ll\frac{\sqrt{r}}{\log r}$ and similarly when $s\geq\sqrt{r\log r}$, the authors find it interesting to determine precisely when the two functions start to exhibit different behaviour; Question 4.3 is described as an explicit question in this direction.
notes: Statement not provided in input excerpt; existence inferred from the theorem-environment list and the surrounding context of Conjecture 4.2.
paper_title: The power of many colours

title: Problem 1.1
statement_text: Given a hereditary property $\mathcal{P}$ which misses a bipartite graph and a $(c,t)$-sparse graph $\Gamma$, determine $\mathrm{ex}(\Gamma,\mathcal{P})$.
context_text: The authors introduce the notion of a $(c,t)$-sparse graph $\Gamma$ (one where the edge density between any two vertex subsets of size at least $t$ is at most $1-c$), which generalizes both dense random graphs and $K_{t,t}$-free graphs. This problem asks for a unified Turán-type theory covering both settings, extending a question of Alon, Krivelevich, and Samotij on hereditary properties in random graphs.
paper_title: The largest subgraph without a forbidden induced subgraph

title: Conjecture 6.1
statement_text: For every $c>0$ and integer $\ell$ there exists $C>1$, such that if $\Gamma$ is a $(c,t)$-sparse graph then $\mathrm{ex}(\Gamma,\mathcal{P}_{C_{2\ell}})\leq Ct^{1-1/\ell}n^{1+1/\ell}$.
context_text: The conjecture concerns the induced Turán number for even cycles $C_{2\ell}$ in $(c,t)$-sparse graphs. It is motivated by the fact that the family $\mathcal{F}_{C_{2\ell}}$ of clique quotients consists of all cycles of length between $\ell$ and $2\ell$, and that graphs of girth larger than $2\ell$ on $n$ vertices have at most $O(n^{1+1/\ell})$ edges.
paper_title: The largest subgraph without a forbidden induced subgraph

also_stated_in.title: The largest subgraph without a forbidden induced subgraph

title: Problem 1.1
statement_text: What is the minimum integer $f\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$ such that every $f\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)$-chromatic oriented graph has an acyclic $k$-chromatic subgraph?
context_text: Addario-Berry, Havet, Sales, Reed, and Thomassé observed that if a $k$-chromatic oriented graph has no directed cycle it contains every oriented tree of order $k$, and suggested that proving acyclic subgraphs of high chromatic number exist in $k$-chromatic oriented graphs could yield improved bounds towards Burr's conjecture. The trivial two-part partition gives $f(k)\leq k^{2}$, subsequently refined to $f(k)\leq k^{2}-2k+2$, but substantially improving this bound seems to require new ideas.
paper_title: Acyclic subgraphs of tournaments with high chromatic number

title: Conjecture 4.1
statement_text: Every $n$-vertex tournament has an acyclic subgraph with chromatic number at least $n^{3/4-o\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(1}\right)}$. That is, $g\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(k}\right)\leq k^{4/3+o\mathopen{}\mathclose{{}\left(1}\right)}$.
context_text: Having established $k^{4/3-o(1)}\leq g(k)\leq k^{9/5+o(1)}$, the authors suspect the lower bound is closer to the truth and propose this conjecture. The lower bound is achieved via a randomized projective-plane construction, and the authors also wonder whether a simpler explicit construction on $n=q^{2}$ vertices attains the same bound.
paper_title: Acyclic subgraphs of tournaments with high chromatic number

title: Problem 3.1
statement_text: Determine whether or not there exists a function $r_{0}\colon\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{R}$ such that the following holds. For each $n\geq 1$, let $\chi_{1},\ldots,\chi_{m}\colon\binom{[n]}{2}\to\mathcal{C}_{r}$ be independent uniformly random $r$-edge-colorings. If $r=r(n)$ satisfies $r(n)<r_{0}(n)$, then with high probability, there does not exist a rainbow stacking of $\chi_{1},\ldots,\chi_{m}$. If $r=r(n)$ satisfies $r(n)>r_{0}(n)$, then with high probability, there exists a rainbow stacking of $\chi_{1},\ldots,\chi_{m}$.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 shows that the existence of rainbow stackings exhibits a sharp threshold, with the transition occurring within an interval of length roughly $(2m-1)/3$. The authors pose whether the transition is even sharper — i.e., whether a single threshold function $r_0$ governs the transition exactly. The critical value of $r$ is on the order of $n/\log n$, making such a sharp transition more dramatic than, for example, the 2-point concentration of the independence number of $G(n,1/2)$.
paper_title: Rainbow Stackings of Random Edge-Colorings

title: Question 3.3
statement_text: Is it true that when $n$ is odd, every pair of proper edge-colorings of $K_{n}$ admits a rainbow stacking?
context_text: Proposition 3.2 constructs, for each even $n\geq 4$, a pair of proper edge-colorings of $K_n$ with no rainbow stacking (based on a construction from transversals in Latin squares over $\mathbb{F}_2^k$). The question asks whether the odd case is categorically different. The answer is known to be affirmative for $n=3$ (by inspection) and $n=5$ (by computer search); a general resolution is expected to be difficult.
paper_title: Rainbow Stackings of Random Edge-Colorings

title: Open Problem (Introduction)
statement_text: Determine the correct dependence of $ES_{\ell}(n)$ on $\ell$.
context_text: The paper establishes that $(3\ell-1)\cdot 2^{n-5} < ES_{\ell}(n) < \ell^{2}\cdot 2^{n+C\sqrt{n\log n}}$, leaving a gap in the dependence on $\ell$. In the regime where $n$ is fixed and $\ell\to\infty$, the cups-caps theorem gives $ES_{\ell}(n)=O(\ell)$, which is best possible up to constants, but the general joint dependence on both parameters remains open.
notes: Stated in running prose at the end of the introduction: "It remains an interesting open problem to determine the correct dependence of $ES_{\ell}(n)$ on $\ell$."
paper_title: Big line or big convex polygon

title: Problem 1.1
statement_text: Given a fixed $k$-uniform hypergraph $G$, determine the integer $h$ (if it exists) such that $r(G;q)$ grows as a tower of height $h$ as $q$ tends to infinity.
context_text: In the graph case, $r(G;q)$ grows as a tower of height $1$ if $G$ is bipartite (with at least two edges) and as a tower of height $2$ otherwise. For 3-uniform hypergraphs, Abbott and Williams showed that $r(K_4^{(3)};q)$ grows as a tower of height $3$. The paper's main theorem (Theorem 1.4) resolves this problem for $k=3$ in full generality.
paper_title: The growth rate of multicolor Ramsey numbers of $3$-graphs

title: Problem 4.1
statement_text: Does there exist, for every $\ell\geq 1,$ a $3$-uniform hypergraph $G_{\ell}$ with $r(G_{\ell};q)=2^{q^{\ell+o(1)}}$?
context_text: For 3-uniform hypergraphs in the exponential-growth class $\mathcal{U}$, the upper and lower bounds often have different powers of $q$ in the exponent. Known cases include $r(\mathrm{Star}^{(3)}(4);q)=2^{q^{1+o(1)}}$ (exponent $\ell=1$) and a construction from Proposition 3.3 achieving exponent $\ell=2$, but for $\ell\geq 3$ the best upper bound is only of the form $2^{q^{\ell+1}\mathrm{polylog}(q)}$.
paper_title: The growth rate of multicolor Ramsey numbers of $3$-graphs

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: For every integer $t\geq 2$ we have $h(t)=\left\lceil\frac{3}{2}(t-1)\right\rceil$. In other words, if a hypergraph $H$ does not contain $K_{t}$ as a minor, then $\chi(H)\leq\left\lceil\frac{3}{2}(t-1)\right\rceil$.
context_text: The function $h(t)$ is the smallest integer such that every $K_t$-minor-free hypergraph is $h(t)$-colorable. The paper proves the lower bound $h(t)\geq\left\lceil\frac{3}{2}(t-1)\right\rceil$ by an explicit construction, and conjectures this lower bound is tight. The conjecture remains open even for $t=3$, where the best known upper bound is $4$.
paper_title: Coloring hypergraphs with excluded minors

title: Problem 1
statement_text: Is every $K_{3}$-minor-free hypergraph $3$-colorable?
context_text: Conjecture 2 predicts $h(3)=3$, but the best known upper bound for the chromatic number of $K_3$-minor-free hypergraphs is $4$ (from Corollary 2 of the paper). Problem 1 isolates the smallest open case of Conjecture 2.
paper_title: Coloring hypergraphs with excluded minors

title: Conjecture 10
statement_text: For each proper hereditary family $\mathcal{F}$ of graphs there are $C_{\mathcal{F}},d_{\mathcal{F}}>0$ such that if $0<\varepsilon<1/2$, every graph on $n$ vertices which is $\varepsilon^{d_{\mathcal{F}}}$-close to $\mathcal{F}$ contains an $\varepsilon$-homogeneous set of size at least $\varepsilon^{C_{\mathcal{F}}}n$.
context_text: A graph on $n$ vertices is $\alpha$-close to a family $\mathcal{F}$ if one can add or delete at most $\alpha n^2/2$ edges to obtain a graph in $\mathcal{F}$. This conjecture, introduced as a natural extension for hereditary families, defines the polynomial close Rödl property; a family with this property also has the polynomial Rödl property.
paper_title: Equivalence between Erdős-Hajnal and polynomial Rödl and Nikiforov conjectures

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: Let $H$ be a connected $k$-graph on $n$ vertices. Then $c(H) = O\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{n}{k}}\right)$.
context_text: The authors introduce this as a generalisation of Meyniel's conjecture to $k$-uniform hypergraphs, motivated by a blow-up construction that shows $c(H) = \Omega\!\left(\sqrt{n/k}\right)$ is achievable. They further observe that for any polynomial $k = n^\alpha$ with $\alpha < 1$, Conjecture 1.4 implies Meyniel's conjecture.
notes: PDF source — LaTeX reconstructed from abstract statement and the formal conjecture environment; statement is unambiguous from context.
paper_title: Catching a robber on a random $k$-uniform hypergraph

title: Problem 5.1 (cover-free families)
statement_text: Are there $(1,r)$-cover-free families $\mathcal{F}\subseteq\mathcal{P}(t)$ with $t\in\mathcal{O}(r^{2}\log\lvert\mathcal{F}\rvert/\log r)$?
context_text: The paper's lower bound for deterministic non-adaptive reconstruction ($\Omega(\Delta^{3}\log n/\log\Delta)$) is tied to a longstanding gap for cover-free families: $\Omega(r^{w+1}\log n/\log r)\leq t(n,w,r)\leq\mathcal{O}(r^{w+1}\log n)$. Closing this gap for $(1,r)$-cover-free families would directly improve the graph-reconstruction bounds.

title: Problem 5.2 (randomised non-adaptive optimality)
statement_text: Is there a randomised non-adaptive algorithm which uses $\mathcal{O}(\Delta^{2}\log(n/\Delta))$ queries to reconstruct any graph of maximum degree $\Delta$ with high probability?
context_text: The paper proves an $\Omega(\Delta^{2}\log(n/\Delta))$ lower bound for randomised non-adaptive algorithms, while the existing upper bound of Konrad, O'Sullivan, and Traistaru is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta^{2}\log n)$. The question asks whether the new lower bound is tight, i.e., whether the $\log\Delta$ gap in the $n$-dependence can be closed.
notes: Parser assigned the label 'Problem 5.0' to all three problems in Section 5; this is likely Problem 5.2 or the second of several unnumbered problems.

title: Problem 5.3 (adaptive vs non-adaptive gap)
statement_text: Is there a deterministic adaptive algorithm that uses $o(\Delta^{3}\log(n/\Delta))$ queries to reconstruct any graph of maximum degree $\Delta$?
context_text: For adaptive algorithms the best lower bound drops to $\Omega(\Delta^{2}\log(n/\Delta)/\log\Delta)$ in both the deterministic and randomised settings, while the upper bounds match the non-adaptive case. This gap suggests adaptive algorithms might outperform non-adaptive ones, motivating the question of whether adaptivity yields a provable saving.
notes: Parser assigned the label 'Problem 5.0' to all three problems in Section 5; this is likely Problem 5.3 or the third of several unnumbered problems.
