title: Conjecture 4.1
statement_text: There exists an absolute constant $a>0$ such that, for all $3$-graphs $G$ and $H$, $r^{p}(G,H)\geq r(G,H)^{a}$ whenever $p>r(G,H)^{o(1)}$.
context_text: The authors introduce the notion of pair constructions $\chi_{f,g}$ of complexity $p$, observing that stepping-up and inducibility constructions both fit this paradigm. They conjecture that any Ramsey coloring of order $N$ yields one of order $N^{\Omega(1)}$ with much smaller complexity, which would explain the prevalence of low-complexity constructions in Ramsey theory and potentially illuminate the possible growth rates of $r(H, K_{n}^{(3)})$.
paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers

title: Conjecture 4.3
statement_text: There exists a $3$-graph $H$ for which $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})=2^{\Theta_{H}(n)}$.
context_text: The paper establishes $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})=2^{\Theta_{H}(n\log n)}$ for many $3$-graphs $H$, but no example achieving the purely exponential rate $2^{\Theta(n)}$ is known. The authors note that a specific constructed $3$-graph with $\frac{1}{3}<m_{\textnormal{pair}}(H)<\frac{1}{2}$, to which Theorem 1.1 does not apply yet $r(H,K_{n}^{(3)})\geq 2^{\Omega_H(n)}$ holds by Theorem 1.2, may be a promising candidate.
paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers

title: Conjecture 4.6
statement_text: For every $c>0$, there exists $s$ such that $r(K_{s}^{(3)},K_{n}^{(3)})\geq 2^{\Omega_{s}(n^{c})}$.
context_text: The authors ask what growth rates are possible for off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers, noting that for the family $\mathcal{H}$ of links of nonbipartite graphs the rate $2^{\Theta_{\mathcal{H}}(n)}$ is achievable. They contrast this with the tripartite setting, where $r(H,K_{n,n,n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_H(n^2)}$ for all $H$, and note the conjecture fails with $K_{n,n,n}^{(3)}$ in place of $K_{n}^{(3)}$.
paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers

title: Conjecture 4.7
statement_text: There is an absolute constant $C>0$ such that $r(F,K_{n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_{F}(n^{C})}$ for every fixed linear $3$-graph $F$.
context_text: Following Theorem 1.4, which disproves the polynomial upper bound for linear $3$-graphs, the authors propose the weaker conjecture that $r(F,K_{n}^{(3)})$ remains subexponential for linear $F$. They note possible further strengthenings (replacing $n^C$ by $n^{o(1)}$ or extending to $123$-inducible $3$-graphs) but are less certain about those variants.
paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers

title: Informal conjecture on tripartite off-diagonal Ramsey numbers
statement_text: It may be the case that $r(H,K_{n,n,n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_{H}(n\log n)}$ for all $H$.
context_text: The authors note that $r(H,K_{n,n,n}^{(3)})\leq 2^{O_H(n^2)}$ for all $H$, and using the methods of [5] one can show a stronger bound $2^{O_H(n^{2-\epsilon})}$ for some $\epsilon>0$ depending on $H$. They suspect the true bound is $2^{O_H(n\log n)}$, which would help explain why the threshold $2^{\Theta(n\log n)}$ is so prevalent and hard to surpass in constructions for $K_n^{(3)}$.
notes: Introduced with 'We suspect that it may even be the case' in the discussion following Conjecture 4.6.
paper_title: On off-diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers

title: Conjecture 3
statement_text: $\vec{a}(n)=\Theta(\sqrt{n\log n})$.
context_text: The paper establishes $\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\varepsilon\right)\sqrt{n\log n}\leq\vec{a}(n)\leq\frac{107}{8}\sqrt{n}\log n$, a logarithmic gap between lower and upper bounds. The authors believe the upper bound can be sharpened to $C\sqrt{n\log n}$ by analysing a suitable orientation of the triangle-free process $G_{\triangle}$.
paper_title: Minimum acyclic number and maximum dichromatic number of oriented triangle-free graphs of a given order

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: $\vec{t}(n)=\Theta\sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}$.
context_text: Conjecture 4 follows from Conjecture 3, since $\vec{t}(n)\geq n/\vec{a}(n)$ and $\vec{t}(n)\leq t(n)$. The paper establishes $\frac{8}{107}\frac{\sqrt{n}}{\log n}\leq\vec{t}(n)\leq\left(\sqrt{2}+o(1)\right)\sqrt{\frac{n}{\log n}}$, leaving a logarithmic gap on the lower side.
notes: The LaTeX in the source appears to be missing parentheses around the $\Theta$ argument; reproduced verbatim as given.
paper_title: Minimum acyclic number and maximum dichromatic number of oriented triangle-free graphs of a given order

title: Problem 11
statement_text: For which graphs $H$ does there exist an $H^{\prime}$ such that $\pi_{G}^{(H^{\prime})}$ determines $\pi_{G}^{(H)}$ for every graph $G$?
context_text: Asgarli et al. conjectured (their Conjecture 5.2) that $\pi_{G}^{(K_{2})}$ determines the chromatic polynomial $\pi_{G}^{(K_{1})}$, i.e., that equal edge-count polynomials of colouring graphs imply equal chromatic polynomials. This broader problem asks for which pairs $(H, H')$ such a determination holds, motivated by Remark 10 in the paper.
paper_title: A note on graphs of $k$-colourings

title: Problem 12
statement_text: Is there a finite set of graphs $H_{1},\dotsc,H_{t}$ such that, for almost every $G\in\mathcal{G}(n,\frac{1}{2})$, if $\pi_{G^{\prime}}^{(H_{i})}=\pi_{G}^{(H_{i})}$ for all $i$ then $G^{\prime}\cong G$?
context_text: Theorem 5 of the paper shows that no finite family of generalised chromatic polynomials can distinguish all graphs; this problem asks whether the situation is better for typical (Erdős–Rényi random) graphs. It is posed as a weakening of the Bollobás–Pebody–Riordan conjecture that almost every graph is determined by its chromatic polynomial.
paper_title: A note on graphs of $k$-colourings

title: Conjecture 1.6
statement_text: The class of (even hole, diamond)-free graph has bounded tree-$\alpha$.
context_text: The paper's main result handles (even hole, diamond, pyramid)-free graphs; extending to (even hole, diamond)-free graphs (dropping the pyramid-free condition) is posed as the next natural step. No polynomial-time algorithm for MWIS is yet known for the larger (even hole, diamond)-free class, and the authors suggest that the methods developed here might be extendable to settle this conjecture.
paper_title: Tree independence number I. (Even hole, diamond, pyramid)-free graphs

title: Conjecture 1.7
statement_text: The class of (even hole, $K_{t}$)-free graph has logarithmic treewidth.
context_text: It is known that (even hole, $K_3$)-free graphs have treewidth at most 5, while (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs do not have bounded treewidth due to layered-wheel constructions. Removing diamonds from the (even hole, $K_4$)-free class restores bounded treewidth; this conjecture posits that for general $t$ the (even hole, $K_t$)-free class has logarithmic treewidth, in line with an upcoming preprint of some of the same authors.
paper_title: Tree independence number I. (Even hole, diamond, pyramid)-free graphs

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: Maximum Independent Set is solvable in polynomial time in $\mathcal{O}_{k}$-free graphs.
context_text: The paper proves that sparse $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs have logarithmic treewidth, making Maximum Independent Set polynomial in the sparse case (Corollary 1.2). A quasipolynomial algorithm is also given for general $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs, stopping just short of resolving this conjecture in full generality.
paper_title: Sparse graphs with bounded induced cycle packing number have logarithmic treewidth

title: Open Question: bounded twin-width for sparse $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs
statement_text: Is there a function $f:\mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that every $\mathcal{O}_{k}$-free graph that does not contain $K_{t,t}$ as a subgraph has twin-width at most $f(t,k)$?
context_text: The paper shows that sparse $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs have logarithmic treewidth, but leaves open whether their twin-width is also bounded as a function of $t$ and $k$. The authors note that $\mathcal{O}_2$-free graphs without $K_{3,3}$ already yield a new family of counterexamples to bounded treewidth from forbidden induced subgraphs alone.
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction without a labelled theorem environment; language is explicit ('we leave as an open question').
paper_title: Sparse graphs with bounded induced cycle packing number have logarithmic treewidth

title: Informal conjecture: MIS tractable in bounded-iocp graphs
statement_text: Maximum Independent Set could be tractable in graphs with bounded $\mathrm{iocp}$ (induced odd cycle packing number).
context_text: The paper establishes tractability of MIS for graphs with bounded induced cycle packing number ($\mathrm{icp}$, i.e., $\mathcal{O}_k$-free graphs) in the sparse case, and quasipolynomial time in general. The authors remark that extending this to bounded $\mathrm{iocp}$ would be a surprising and formidable generalisation of Conjecture 1.3 and of the known polynomial-time result for bounded $\mathrm{ocp}$ (odd cycle packing number).
notes: Stated as a speculative remark in prose ('As far as we can tell, MIS could even be tractable in graphs with bounded iocp'); authors treat it seriously as a potential generalisation.
paper_title: Sparse graphs with bounded induced cycle packing number have logarithmic treewidth

title: Conjecture 1.12
statement_text: For every undirected tree, there exists some orientation that has the tournament anti-Sidorenko property.
context_text: After characterising exactly which orientations of stars are tournament anti-Sidorenko (Theorem 1.11), the authors note it is natural to ask whether every undirected tree admits at least one anti-Sidorenko orientation. They confirm the conjecture for all undirected trees with at most one vertex of even degree (Proposition 1.13).
paper_title: Variations on Sidorenko's conjecture in tournaments

title: Question 1.3
statement_text: Do there exist $c, \varepsilon > 0$ with the following property? Let $\mathcal{B} = (B_1, B_2, B_3)$ be a blockade in a graph $G$, and let $W$ be its width. If there is no $\mathcal{B}$-transversal triangle, then there exist distinct $i, j \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $X \subseteq B_i$ and $Y \subseteq B_j$, such that $X, Y$ is a pure pair, and $|X|, |Y| \geq \varepsilon W^c$.
context_text: This is a variant of a conjecture of Conlon, Fox and Sudakov. Settling Question 1.3 was the authors' initial goal, though they were unable to do so. It is also equivalent to the $k=3$ case of Theorem 1.4 (which handles cycles of length $k \geq 4$), and can be reduced to the sparse case where every vertex in $B_i$ has at most $|B_j|/100$ neighbours in $B_j$ for all distinct $i, j \in \{1,2,3\}$.
paper_title: Pure pairs. IX. Transversal trees

title: Informal: which graphs H admit polynomial-size pure pairs
statement_text: For which ordered graphs $H$ can we get $|X|, |Y| \geq \varepsilon W^c$ (for some fixed $c > 0$ depending only on $H$) as the pure pair conclusion when there is no ordered $\mathcal{B}$-transversal copy of $H$ in a blockade of width $W$?
context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.2 (Erdős–Hajnal–Pach), the authors ask for which $H$ the pure pair conclusion can be made polynomial in $W$. They note that $c = 1$ (linear pure pairs) is achievable only for $|H| \leq 2$, and flag the polynomial question as 'more promising'. The bulk of the paper provides partial answers for trees and caterpillars.
notes: PDF source — the exact threshold formulas around this informal question may be partially garbled; overall intent is clear from context.
paper_title: Pure pairs. IX. Transversal trees

title: Informal: characterization of graphs with the strong transversal property
statement_text: Which graphs $H$ have the strong transversal property, i.e., for which $H$ does there exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every graph $G$ with an $(\varepsilon W, \varepsilon W)$-cohesive blockade $\mathcal{B} = (B_1, \ldots, B_{|H|})$ of local degree less than $\varepsilon W$ contain a $\mathcal{B}$-transversal copy of $H$?
context_text: The question opens Section 2. The authors note that $H$ must be a forest, and give partial results in Theorem 2.1: paths have the property; certain caterpillar-like trees do; trees with a vertex of degree $d$ satisfying $2^{d-1} \geq |H|$ do not. No complete characterization is given, and the converse of the component-wise criterion fails.
notes: Posed as a driving question for Section 2 without a formal label or environment.
paper_title: Pure pairs. IX. Transversal trees

title: Conjecture 1.8
statement_text: For every forest $H$, there exists a function $f:\mathbb{N}\rightarrow\mathbb{N}$ such that every $H$-free graph $G$ satisfies $\eta(G)\leq f(\omega(G))$.
context_text: Proposed as an analogue of the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture ($\chi$-boundedness) for $\eta$-boundedness: just as $H$-free graphs are conjectured to be $\chi$-bounded for every forest $H$, the authors conjecture the same for $\eta$-boundedness. The paper proves this conjecture for an assortment of special forests, including stars and paths $P_t$ with $t\leq 5$.
paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs

also_stated_in.title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs

title: Conjecture 1.13
statement_text: For every integer $t\geq 6$, the class of $P_{t}$-free graphs is $\eta$-bounded.
context_text: After proving $\eta$-boundedness for $P_5$-free graphs (Theorem 1.12), the authors note that it remains open whether $P_t$-free graphs are $\eta$-bounded for $t\geq 6$, and state this as a conjecture paralleling the known $\chi$-boundedness of $P_t$-free graphs for all $t$.
paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs

title: Conjecture 1.17
statement_text: There exists $d\in\mathbb{N}$ such that every $P_{5}$-free graph $G$ satisfies $\eta(G)\leq\omega(G)^{d}$.
context_text: A special case of Conjecture 1.16 singled out because of its connection to the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture: by Theorem 1.6, a polynomial $\eta$-bound for $P_5$-free graphs would imply the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture for $P_5$-free graphs. The bound in the paper's main theorem (Theorem 1.12) is super-exponential.
paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs

title: Conjecture 1.20
statement_text: For every integer $t\geq 3$, $M_{t}$-free graphs are polynomially $\eta$-bounded.
context_text: Proposed as a strengthening of Theorem 1.19; the authors note that it is not even known whether $M_3$-free graphs are $\eta$-bounded, making this a particularly bold conjecture even for the base case.
paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs

title: Open problem on $\eta$-boundedness for disjoint union of two stars
statement_text: It remains open whether the disjoint union of two stars satisfies Conjecture 1.8, i.e., whether the class of $(S_{a}\cup S_{b})$-free graphs is $\eta$-bounded for all $a,b\geq 1$.
context_text: For the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture, $\chi$-boundedness of an $H$-free class reduces to $\chi$-boundedness of the component-free classes. The authors do not know whether the analogous reduction holds for Conjecture 1.8, and in particular whether the disjoint union of two stars satisfies $\eta$-boundedness.
notes: Stated as 'it remains open' in prose following Conjecture 1.13, without a labelled environment.
paper_title: Hitting all maximum stable sets in $P_5$-free graphs

title: Conjecture 1.1
statement_text: For every $d\in\mathbb{Q}$, $d>1$, and $n\in\mathbb{N}$, there exists a graph $G$ with $$e(G)\leq Cn^{2-1/d}$$ edges which is $\mathcal{H}_{d}(n)$-universal, where $C=C(d)$.
context_text: The authors initiate the study of universality for graphs with bounded density, unifying earlier results on bounded-degree graphs, forests, and degenerate graphs. They note that a simple counting argument shows $e(G)=o(n^{2-1/d})$ is insufficient, making the conjectured bound tight up to the constant $C$. The paper proves constructions achieving $O_d(n^{2-1/(\lceil d\rceil+1)})$ edges, approaching but not yet matching this conjecture.
notes: The complete edge bound $e(G)\leq Cn^{2-1/d}$ appears in the introduction prose; the theorem-environment extraction was truncated. Statement reconstructed from the introduction.
paper_title: Universality for graphs with bounded density

also_stated_in.title: Universality for graphs with bounded density

title: Question 1.1
statement_text: Given a graph $\Gamma$, can we decompose it as the joined union of smaller graphs?
context_text: This question is motivated by the study of multilayer networks and phase oscillators, where decomposing a network as a joined union of smaller graphs allows solutions to be broadcast from a reduced representation system to the full network. The paper addresses this question specifically for Cayley graphs using tools from group theory and ring theory.
notes: The paper answers this question in the special case of Cayley graphs; the general question for arbitrary graphs is classical and studied via modular decomposition theory.
paper_title: On prime Cayley graphs

title: Question 4.28
statement_text: When is $X_{R}$ prime?
context_text: $X_R$ denotes the Cayley graph constructed from a finite ring $R$ using its group of units and a connection set derived from the ring structure. This question is the central driving question of Section 4, and is reduced first to the semisimple case (showing non-semisimple $R$ gives non-prime $X_R$) and then to a combinatorial question about tensor products of complete graphs.
notes: This question appears to be answered within the paper via Theorem 4.35; it is posed as the key structural question leading to the main results.
paper_title: On prime Cayley graphs

title: Question 4.31
statement_text: Let $n_{1},\ldots,n_{d}$ be positive integers. When is $\prod_{i=1}^{d}K_{n_{i}}$ prime?
context_text: For a semisimple ring $R = \prod_{i=1}^d k_i$ (a product of finite fields of sizes $q_i$), the associated Cayley graph satisfies $X_R \cong \prod_{i=1}^d K_{q_i}$, reducing Question 4.28 for the semisimple case to this combinatorial question about tensor products (Kronecker products) of complete graphs. The vertex set of $\prod_{i=1}^d K_{n_i}$ consists of $d$-tuples $(s_1,\ldots,s_d)$ with two vertices adjacent iff they differ in every coordinate.
notes: This question is addressed by Theorem 4.35 within the paper; it is the combinatorial core to which the algebraic problem of Question 4.28 is reduced.
paper_title: On prime Cayley graphs

title: Problem 1
statement_text: What is the value of $r_{t}$?
context_text: The quantity $r_{t}$ denotes the best approximation ratio achievable for FCPP (Forward Connected Pairs Problem) in strongly connected digraphs. The paper establishes $r_{t}>0$ as its central result, but the exact value of $r_{t}$ remains open. The paper shows $r_{t}\geq 1/9$ via its bi-tree construction.
paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees

title: Problem 2
statement_text: What is the maximum $c_{b}$ for which every strongly connected directed graph on $n$ vertices has a balanced bi-tree of size at least $c_{b}.n$?
context_text: The paper proves $c_{b}\geq 1/3$ (Theorem 4), where both the out-tree $|T^{+}|$ and in-tree $|T^{-}|$ of the bi-tree each have size at least $n/6$. The exact optimal constant $c_{b}$ is unknown.
paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees

title: Problem 3
statement_text: Can we compute a left-maximal dfs-tree in $o(n^{2})$ time in minimally strongly connected digraphs? What about the complexity in the general case?
context_text: The Left-DFS tree is a key algorithmic ingredient introduced in the paper for constructing balanced bi-trees. The current construction runs in $O(n^{2})$ time; whether this can be improved is left open, both for the minimally strongly connected special case and for general strongly connected digraphs.
paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees

title: Problem 4
statement_text: Is there a polytime constant approximation algorithm for RFCPP?
context_text: RFCPP (Request Forward Connected Pairs Problem) is the variant of FCPP where only a specified set $R\subseteq\binom{V}{2}$ of pairs needs to be forward connected. Since FCPP (the case $R=\binom{V}{2}$) is in APX, the question is whether a constant-factor approximation also exists for RFCPP, and in particular whether a linear fraction of requests in $R$ can always be satisfied.
paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees

title: Conjecture 1
statement_text: Every strong digraph $D$ on $n$ vertices has a $O(\log n)$ size forward cover.
context_text: A forward cover is a small set of vertex orderings such that every pair of vertices is forward connected in at least one ordering. The conjecture is verified for bi-oriented graphs (undirected graphs with each edge replaced by two arcs) using a centroid-decomposition argument. The general case for arbitrary strongly connected digraphs remains open.
paper_title: Temporalizing digraphs via linear-size balanced bi-trees

title: Conjecture 6.1
statement_text: For a 3-uniform $n$-vertex hypergraph $H$ with $d_{1}(H)=d$, [statement truncated in source]
context_text: Theorem 1.5 gives $r(H;q)\leq\exp^{(k-1)}(O_{k,q}(d))n$ for $k\geq 4$-uniform hypergraphs with first skeletal degeneracy $d$. For $k=3$ the analogous bound from the proof is $\exp^{(2)}(O_{3,q}(d))n$; Conjecture 6.1 posits a stronger bound for 3-uniform hypergraphs, stronger than a result of Bradač–Fox–Sudakov [3] in which skeletal degeneracy is replaced by $\sqrt{e(H)}$. The inequality $d_{1}(H)\leq O(\sqrt{e(H)})$ shows that 6.1 would imply that result.
notes: Statement body truncated in the provided source after the hypothesis clause; exact bound conjectured is not fully reproduced.
paper_title: Ramsey and Turán numbers of sparse hypergraphs

title: Conjecture 6.2
statement_text: There is a constant $c_{k}>0$ such that for every $k$-uniform $k$-partite hypergraph $H$, [statement truncated in source]
context_text: Theorem 1.4 gives $\Omega_{k}(n^{k-C_{k}/d_{1}(H)})\leq\operatorname{ex}(n,H)\leq O_{H}(n^{k-c_{k}/d_{1}(H)^{k-1}})$. The complete $k$-partite hypergraph $K^{(k)}_{d,\ldots,d}$ shows the upper bound is tight in $d_{1}$, while the bipartite hedgehog $H_{d}^{(k)}$ shows the lower bound is tight in $d_{1}$. This gap motivates replacing $d_{1}(H)$ by the finer quantity $d_{\mathsf{max}}(H)=\max_{1\leq i<k}d_{i}(H)$, and Conjecture 6.2 asserts that the Turán exponent is controlled by $d_{\mathsf{max}}(H)$.
notes: Statement body truncated in the provided source after the setup clause; the precise bound in terms of $d_{\mathsf{max}}(H)$ is not fully reproduced.
paper_title: Ramsey and Turán numbers of sparse hypergraphs

title: Conjecture 6.4
statement_text: Let $L$ be a $d\times d$ Latin square. Define $H_{L}$ to be the 3-uniform 3-partite hypergraph on vertex set $[d]\sqcup[d]\sqcup[d]$ where $(i,j,k)\in E(H_{L})$ if and only if $L(i,j)=k$. Then $\mathrm{ex}(n,H_{L})=n^{3-\Theta(1/d)}$.
context_text: For every $d\times d$ Latin square one has $d_{1}(H_{L}),d_{2}(H_{L})=\Theta(d)$, so $d_{\mathsf{max}}(H_{L})=\Theta(d)$, yet Theorem 1.4 only yields $\mathrm{ex}(n,H_{L})\leq O(n^{3-c/d^{2}})$. The 1-skeleton of $H_{L}$ is $K_{d,d,d}$, the same as that of the complete 3-partite hypergraph $K^{(3)}_{d,d,d}$, so current techniques cannot distinguish $H_{L}$ from $K^{(3)}_{d,d,d}$ at the level of the Turán exponent. Conjecture 6.4 predicts that the exponent is $3-\Theta(1/d)$, matching $d_{\mathsf{max}}(H_{L})=\Theta(d)$ via Conjecture 6.2.
paper_title: Ramsey and Turán numbers of sparse hypergraphs

title: Conjecture 6.5
statement_text: There is a constant $c>0$ such that for all $d\geq 4$ the following holds. If $G$ is a (not necessarily linear) 3-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices with at least $n^{3-c/d}$ edges, then there is a linear subhypergraph of $G$ with $d$ vertices and at least $d^{2}/100$ edges.
context_text: Proving that $\mathrm{ex}(n,H_{L})\leq O(n^{3-c/d})$ would require finding $H_{L}$ — a linear hypergraph — in any sufficiently dense host. The current barrier is that the techniques only find $K^{(3)}_{d,d,d}$, a non-linear structure. If Conjecture 6.5 holds, finding any dense enough linear subhypergraph (with quadratically many edges) would be the key missing step. The conjecture is known without the linearity constraint, since a hypergraph Kővári–Sós–Turán argument gives $K^{(3)}_{1,(d-1)/2,(d-1)/2}$ in any 3-uniform hypergraph with $n^{3-c/d}$ edges.
paper_title: Ramsey and Turán numbers of sparse hypergraphs

title: Open Problem: characterisation of ℓ-holed graphs for ℓ ∈ {4, 5, 6}
statement_text: Give a complete structural description of $\ell$-holed graphs (graphs in which every hole has length exactly $\ell$) for $\ell \in \{4, 5, 6\}$, analogous to Theorem 1.3 for $\ell \geq 7$.
context_text: The paper establishes a complete structural description of $\ell$-holed graphs for all $\ell \geq 7$ (Theorem 1.3), but explicitly states: "We were not able to do this for $\ell = 4, 5, 6$." The case $\ell = 5$ is highlighted separately: "when all holes have length five, we do not know a complete description, but such graphs were studied [12] in the context of algebraic combinatorics and commutative algebra."
notes: Stated as an admitted limitation rather than a formal labelled environment. The supplied PDF text is truncated (ends mid-sentence); later sections of the paper may contain additional explicitly labelled conjectures or questions that could not be extracted.
paper_title: Graphs with all holes the same length

title: Conjecture 3
statement_text: For every positive integer $\ell$, there is a polynomial-time algorithm that given a planar near-Eulerian-triangulation $G$ with the outer face of length at most $\ell$ and a precoloring $\varphi$ of the vertices incident with the outer face decides whether $\varphi$ extends to a 4-coloring of $G$.
context_text: The paper provides only a necessary topological condition for precoloring extension in general, and resolves the problem completely only for outer faces of length at most five and in the case where all vertices of the outer face have odd degree and are colored with three colors. The authors conjecture that combining the topological condition with further ideas will resolve the disk case in full, and that a polynomial-time decision algorithm exists for any fixed outer face length $\ell$.
paper_title: Precoloring extension in planar near-Eulerian-triangulations

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: For every positive even integer $\ell$, there exists an integer $d$ such that the following claim holds. Let $G$ be a planar near-Eulerian-triangulation with the outer face bounded by a cycle $C$ of length $\ell$ and let $U$ be an independent set disjoint from $C$ such that $G-U$ is bipartite. Moreover, suppose that $G-U$ has a set $S$ of $d$ faces of length at least six such that there is no 4-cycle in $G-U$ separating a face of $S$ from the outer face, and for distinct faces $s_{1},s_{2}\in S$, the distance between $s_{1}$ and $s_{2}$ in $G-U$ is at least $d$ and there is no closed walk of length less than $\ell$ in $G-U$ separating both $s_{1}$ and $s_{2}$ from the outer face. Then any viable 4-coloring of $C$ extends to a 4-coloring of $G$.
context_text: This conjecture captures a 'sufficiently generic' structural condition under which the necessary topological condition for precoloring extension is also sufficient. It is posed as an intermediate step toward Conjecture 3, asserting that when $G-U$ is bipartite and contains sufficiently many well-separated long faces, every viable precoloring of the outer face extends to a full 4-coloring.
paper_title: Precoloring extension in planar near-Eulerian-triangulations

title: Problem 41
statement_text: Determine the profile and neighborhood complexities of planar graphs up to a constant factor. For profile complexity, it is known to be in $\Omega(r^{3})$ (Theorem 36) and in $\operatorname{\mathcal{O}}(r^{4})$ (Theorem 2). For neighborhood complexity, it is known to be in $\Omega(r^{2})$ (Theorem 36 and Lemma 8) and in $\operatorname{\mathcal{O}}(r^{4})$ (Theorem 2).
context_text: The paper closes with two open problems. The first asks for tight bounds on the profile and neighborhood complexities of planar graphs; the existing lower bounds ($\Omega(r^3)$ for profile, $\Omega(r^2)$ for neighborhood) and the paper's new upper bound ($\mathcal{O}(r^4)$ for both) leave a small gap that the authors consider tantalizingly close.
paper_title: Neighborhood complexity of planar graphs

title: Problem 42
statement_text: Are the profile and neighborhood complexities of $K_{t}$-minor-free graphs in $r^{\operatorname{\mathcal{O}}(t)}$?
context_text: The second open problem asks whether the polynomial bound for proper minor-closed classes can be sharpened so that the degree of the polynomial grows only logarithmically (or polynomially) in the size $t$ of the excluded complete minor, rather than being a fixed polynomial independent of $t$.
paper_title: Neighborhood complexity of planar graphs

title: Question 1.4
statement_text: Is $\Delta(1,2,2)$ a hero in oriented complete multipartite graphs?
context_text: The paper characterizes heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs up to the status of $\Delta(1,2,2)$. If the answer is no, heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs are precisely those described in Theorem 1.3; if the answer is yes, a different complete characterization involving $\Delta(1,2,2)$ holds.
paper_title: Heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs

title: Question 5.4
statement_text: Let $H$ and $F$ be digraphs such that $\Delta(1,1,H)$ is a hero in $Forb_{ind}(F)$ and $H$ is a hero in $Forb_{ind}(K_{1}+F)$. Then $\Delta(1,1,H)$ is a hero in $Forb_{ind}(K_{1}+F)$.
context_text: This question asks whether a compositional hero property transfers across the $K_1 + F$ operation. It is motivated by the observation that knowing heroes in $Forb_{ind}(\bar{K}_t)$ does not straightforwardly help in understanding heroes in $Forb_{ind}(\bar{K}_{t+1})$, even though such a transfer would be natural.
paper_title: Heroes in oriented complete multipartite graphs

title: Question 10
statement_text: Does there exist, for each integer $d$, an integer $\chi$ such that for every graph $G$ with $\chi(G)\geqslant\chi$ and every tournament $T$ on the same vertex set, there is a vertex $v$ for which $G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]$ has degeneracy at least $d$?
context_text: In the closing remarks the authors note they have been unable to determine whether high chromatic number forces an out-neighbourhood with high degeneracy. The paper's disproof of Conjecture 1 shows chromatic number is not preserved, but degeneracy is a weaker measure and may behave differently.
paper_title: Chromatic number is not tournament-local

title: Conjecture 11
statement_text: For every graph $G$ with sufficiently large chromatic number, and every tournament $T$ on the same vertex set, there exists a vertex $v$ such that $G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]$ contains a cycle.
context_text: The authors suspect that the case $d=2$ of Question 10 is true, i.e., high chromatic number should force some out-neighbourhood to contain a cycle. This is the weakest non-trivial instance of the degeneracy question, since bipartite graphs (which arise in their counterexamples) are acyclic-free but 1-degenerate.
paper_title: Chromatic number is not tournament-local

title: Conjecture 12
statement_text: For every positive integer $k$, there exists a $\chi$ such that if $T$ is the uniformly random tournament on vertex set $[N]$, then with high probability (as $N\to\infty$), for every graph $G$ on $[N]$ with $\chi(G)\geqslant\chi$ there is a vertex $v\in[N]$ for which $G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]\geqslant k$.
context_text: The counterexamples to Conjecture 1 use very structured tournaments; the authors conjecture that a random tournament cannot be used as such a counterexample, i.e., that the Scott–Seymour phenomenon holds with high probability for the random tournament.
notes: The statement as extracted likely contains a typographical omission: '$G[N^{+}_{T}(v)]\geqslant k$' should read '$\chi(G[N^{+}_{T}(v)])\geqslant k$'.
paper_title: Chromatic number is not tournament-local

title: Conjecture 13
statement_text: There exists $f(N)$ satisfying $f(N)=o(\log N)$ such that for every $N$-vertex graph $G$ with $\chi(G)\geqslant f(N)$, and every tournament $T$ on the same vertex set, there is a vertex $v$ for which $\chi(G[N^{+}_{T}(v)])\geqslant 3$.
context_text: Theorem 2 shows a collection of at most $\lceil\log_2(N)/\lfloor\chi/2-1\rfloor\rceil$ out-neighbourhoods suffices to force chromatic number at least 3; the authors ask whether $o(\log(N)/\chi)$ out-neighbourhoods (or even a single vertex) suffice when the chromatic number threshold itself grows with $N$ as $o(\log N)$.
paper_title: Chromatic number is not tournament-local

title: Optimal disjoint-edges bound in complete simple topological graphs
statement_text: Is it true that every complete $n$-vertex simple topological graph contains $\Omega(n)$ pairwise disjoint edges?
context_text: Aichholzer et al. [2] showed that every complete $n$-vertex simple topological graph always contains $\Omega(n^{1/2})$ pairwise disjoint edges. The paper notes that improving this lower bound to $\Omega(n)$ is an open problem, and Corollary 1.3 provides a new nearly-polynomial lower bound under a density assumption.
notes: Stated in passing as an open problem without a labelled theorem environment; PDF source.
paper_title: A structure theorem for pseudo-segments and its applications

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: For every forest $H$ of maximum degree at most three, MWIS admits a QPTAS and a subexponential-time algorithm in the class of graphs that do not contain any subdivision of $H$ as an induced subgraph.
context_text: Following Theorems 1.1 and 1.2, which establish QPTASes and subexponential-time algorithms for MWIS in $H$-free graphs where every component of $H$ is a path or a subdivided claw, the authors conjecture a broader generalization to all forests of maximum degree at most three and graphs excluding any subdivision of $H$ as an induced subgraph. The paper itself proves Conjecture 1.3 for $H$ being a forest with at most three vertices of degree three (with a $2^{O(|V(G)|^{40/41}\log|V(G)|)}$ subexponential bound), but the full generality remains open.
paper_title: Quasi-polynomial time approximation schemes for the Maximum Weight Independent Set Problem in H-free graphs

title: Converse to Theorem 1.1 (3-Coloring dichotomy for $H$-free graphs)
statement_text: If every component of $H$ is a path, then the $3$-Coloring Problem restricted to $H$-free graphs can be solved in polynomial time.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 (Holyer; Kamiński and Lozin) establishes that if $H$ has at least one component which is not a path then the $3$-Coloring Problem on $H$-free graphs is NP-hard. Immediately after stating this theorem the paper notes: 'The converse to Theorem 1.1 is wide open.'
notes: Not a labelled environment; the open problem is identified by the remark 'The converse to Theorem 1.1 is wide open.' The statement of the converse is implicit and reconstructed from Theorem 1.1.
paper_title: List-$k$-Coloring $H$-free graphs for all $k>4$

title: Full dichotomy for $k$-Coloring on $H$-free graphs ($k\geq 3$)
statement_text: For every integer $k \geq 3$, characterise the graphs $H$ for which the $k$-Coloring Problem restricted to $H$-free graphs is polynomial-time solvable versus NP-hard.
context_text: The paper observes that, in contrast with the List-$k$-Coloring Problem (now settled for all $k>4$), 'for the $k$-Coloring Problem, no value of $k\geq 3$ is known for which the "easy" choices of $H$ are completely distinguished from the "hard" ones.' This identifies a broad open problem motivating the paper's List-$k$-Coloring results.
notes: Not a labelled environment; the open problem is implicit in the motivating remark in the introduction. Statement reconstructed from context.
paper_title: List-$k$-Coloring $H$-free graphs for all $k>4$

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: For every fixed $t \geq 1$, the family of $t$-clock-free graphs is clean.
context_text: A $t$-clock is a clock consisting of a hole $C$ and a vertex $v$ such that there are two neighbours $x, y \in V(C)$ of $v$ where the distance between $x$ and $y$ along $C$ is at least $t$. The main result of the paper (Theorem 1.1) establishes cleanness for $t = 2$ (all clocks); Conjecture 1.3 proposes that cleanness persists even when clocks with close non-adjacent pairs are allowed, for every fixed threshold $t$.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XIV. Non-adjacent neighbours in a hole

title: Question: finite families yielding clean H-free classes
statement_text: For which finite families $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs is the class of all $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs clean?
context_text: After proving Theorem 1.5 — which characterizes all single graphs $H$ for which the class $\mathcal{F}_H$ of $H$-free graphs is clean (precisely the subdivided star forests) — the authors pose the next natural step. They observe that any finite set $\mathcal{H}$ containing a subdivided star forest yields a clean class, but note that the converse fails (e.g., $\mathcal{H}=\{H, K_3\}$ for the unique double star on six vertices is also clean). A full description is deferred to a companion paper [6] by four of the five authors.
notes: Posed as a natural next step in running prose (no labelled theorem environment). The companion paper [6] is announced to resolve it; PDF source is truncated after Section 2, so later sections may contain additional items not captured here.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree-decompositions VII. Basic obstructions in $H$-free graphs

title: Complexity of Rotation Distance Between Binary Search Trees
statement_text: What is the computational complexity of computing the rotation distance between two binary search trees (equivalently, the flip distance between two triangulations of a convex polygon)?
context_text: The paper identifies the rotation distance problem as a central motivation for studying shortest paths on polymatroids. Despite a known polynomial-time 2-approximation and several FPT algorithms, the problem is neither known to be NP-hard nor known to admit a polynomial-time exact algorithm, making it a widely open question.
notes: Stated in prose as 'another widely open question'; not a newly posed problem but an existing open question highlighted as a primary motivation. PDF source — math notation appears clean for this item.
paper_title: Shortest paths on polymatroids and hypergraphic polytopes

title: Complexity of Flip Distance Between Rectangulations
statement_text: What is the computational complexity of computing the flip distance between two rectangulations of a unit square?
context_text: Rectangulations are tessellations of the unit square by axis-aligned rectangles, whose flip graphs are skeletons of polytopes that are polymatroids (and special cases of quotientopes). Since they fall under Problem 2 (shortest paths on a polymatroid), the authors note explicitly that the complexity of the flip distance problems between rectangulations remains open.
notes: Stated in prose as 'The complexity of these problems is open.' PDF source — math notation appears clean for this item.
paper_title: Shortest paths on polymatroids and hypergraphic polytopes

title: Open Problem: sharpness of the treedepth bound in Theorem 1
statement_text: Whether the bound $O(thb)$ in Theorem 1 — that every graph of treewidth less than $t$ with no complete binary tree of height $h$ as a minor and no $2^b$-vertex path has treedepth $O(thb)$ — can be improved remains an open problem.
context_text: Theorems 2 and 3 together imply Theorem 1, and their individual bounds are each sharp up to constant factors. However, it is unknown whether the combined bound $O(thb)$ in Theorem 1 is optimal or can be reduced.
notes: Stated as prose without a labelled theorem environment. PDF source — exponent notation inferred from context.
paper_title: Tight bound on treedepth in terms of pathwidth and longest path

title: Conjecture 10
statement_text: For all functions $f(n) : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ with $n \leq f(n) \leq r(K_n)$ and for all $\varepsilon > 0$, there is a sequence of graphs $(G_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ satisfying $|V(G_n)| = n$ and $(1-\varepsilon)f(n) \leq r(G_n) \leq (1+\varepsilon)f(n)$ for all sufficiently large $n$.
context_text: The paper's main theorem achieves every growth rate up to a multiplicative constant $C_k$; Conjecture 10 asks whether the multiplicative gaps in $\mathcal{R}^c_n$ can be made $1+o(1)$, i.e., whether any prescribed growth rate can be approximated arbitrarily closely. The authors suggest that edge-deletion results (such as a conjecture of Wigderson) might provide a route to this.
notes: PDF source; mathematical notation is clean.
paper_title: Graphs with arbitrary Ramsey number and connectivity

title: Informal Conjecture on h(n)
statement_text: The current best lower bound $h(n) = \Omega(n^{1/4})$ is tight, i.e., $h(n) = \Theta(n^{1/4})$.
context_text: The bounds $\Omega(n^{1/4}) \leq h(n) \leq n^{1/2+o(1)}$ were both known by 1990, with the lower bound due to Bosznay [6] and the upper bound to Erdős and Sárközy [10]. The lower bound follows from a simple construction: fixing integer $q$, the set $\{n_i = iq^3 + i(i+1)/2 : i = 1,\ldots,q-1\}$ is a non-averaging subset of $[n]$ with $n = q^4$. The authors state 'which we suspect to be tight' immediately after citing this bound.
notes: Stated as running prose: 'The current best lower bound, h(n) = Ω(n^{1/4}), which we suspect to be tight'. No labelled conjecture environment.
paper_title: Homogeneous structures in subset sums and non-averaging sets

title: Polynomial degree-bounding function for hereditary degree-bounded classes
statement_text: Is there always a polynomial degree-bounding function for every hereditary degree-bounded class of graphs? That is, for every hereditary degree-bounded class $\mathcal{F}$, does there exist a polynomial $g$ such that $d(G) \leq g(\tau(G))$ for every $G \in \mathcal{F}$?
context_text: Corollary 1.4 establishes that every hereditary degree-bounded class admits an exponential degree-bounding function of the form $C^{s^3}$, which is the first bound of any type on the rate of growth. The paper notes explicitly in both the abstract and the introduction that it is open whether a polynomial always suffices. The analogous question for $\chi$-boundedness has a negative answer (Briański, Davies, and Walczak).
notes: Stated in prose in the abstract and in the introduction without a labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Induced $C_4$-free subgraphs with large average degree

title: Polynomial bound in k for C4-free subgraphs
statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial $p(k)$ such that every graph with average degree at least $p(k)$ contains a $C_4$-free subgraph with average degree at least $k$?
context_text: Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov proved that every graph with average degree at least $k^{Ck^2}$ contains a $C_4$-free subgraph of average degree at least $k$, and also gave a lower bound of $k^{3-o(1)}$. The paper describes determining whether a polynomial in $k$ suffices as 'a very interesting problem'.
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction without a labelled theorem environment; the gap between the lower bound $k^{3-o(1)}$ and the upper bound $k^{Ck^2}$ makes this a natural open problem.
paper_title: Induced $C_4$-free subgraphs with large average degree

title: Possibility 1.6
statement_text: For all $c > 0$, there exists $\xi > 0$ with the following property. For every graph $H$ with congestion at most $\xi$, there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that for every graph $G$ with $|G| > 1$ that is $H$-free and $\bar{H}$-free, there is a pure pair $A, B$ in $G$ with $|A| \geq \varepsilon|G|$ and $|B| \geq \varepsilon|G|^{1-c}$.
context_text: Theorem 1.3 (from an earlier paper) gives a pure pair where one set has linear size, and the authors ask whether an analogous strengthening of their main result 1.4 holds: can one of the two sets always be taken to have linear size rather than polynomial size $\varepsilon|G|^{1-c}$? The authors explicitly state they were unable to decide this. The proof technique for 1.4 cannot be directly extended to resolve 1.6 because a key inductive argument requires strictly increasing shrinkage parameters, leaving no room for the stronger conclusion.
notes: PDF source — labeled 'Possibility' rather than 'Conjecture' or 'Question' in the paper; math in 1.6 is readable but the congestion bound $c/(9+15c)$ in the nearby Theorem 1.4 appears garbled in the PDF extraction.
paper_title: Pure pairs. VIII. Excluding a sparse graph

title: Open question on Theorem 3.3 with stronger coherence
statement_text: Is Theorem 3.3 true with the hypothesis that $G$ is $(\varepsilon|G|^{1-c},\, \varepsilon|G|^{1-c})$-coherent replaced by the stronger hypothesis that $G$ is $(\varepsilon|G|^{1-c},\, \varepsilon|G|)$-coherent?
context_text: In Section 4, the authors note that the stronger coherence condition $(\varepsilon|G|^{1-c}, \varepsilon|G|)$-coherent would suffice for almost all of the proof of Theorem 3.3, and they write the proof using only this weaker form wherever possible. Resolving this question in the affirmative would be a step toward proving Possibility 1.6.
notes: PDF source — stated as prose ('we do not know whether') without a labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Pure pairs. VIII. Excluding a sparse graph

title: Open case of Erdős's problem for fixed small k
statement_text: For every fixed $k \geq 4$ and arbitrarily large $r$, does there exist a $k$-chromatic vertex-critical graph $G$ such that $\chi(G - R) = k$ for every $R \subseteq E(G)$ with $|R| \leq r$?
context_text: Theorem 1 resolves Erdős's problem for all sufficiently large $k$ (as a function of $r$), but leaves open the regime where $k \geq 4$ is held fixed as a small constant while $r$ grows. The authors explicitly flag this as an interesting remaining open case.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled environment: 'Our result still leaves open Erdős' question when k ≥ 4 is fixed as a small value and r tends to infinity, and this remains an interesting open case of the problem.'
paper_title: Vertex-critical graphs far from edge-criticality

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: For any $d \geq 3$ and any fixed $k$, the set of all limit points of the vectors $(\lambda_1(G_i), \lambda_2(G_i), \ldots, \lambda_k(G_i))$ for an infinite sequence $G_i$ of $d$-regular graphs is exactly the set $$B(d,k) = \{(\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_k) : d = \mu_1 \geq \mu_2 \geq \mu_3 \geq \ldots \geq \mu_k \geq 2\sqrt{d-1}\}.$$
context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes the case $k=2$ (the set of limit points of $\lambda_2$ for $d$-regular graphs is $[2\sqrt{d-1}, d]$). The authors conjecture this extends to vectors of the top $k$ eigenvalues for any fixed $k$, noting that the containment $\subseteq B(d,k)$ is already known but they have not been able to prove that every point of $B(d,k)$ is a limit point even for $k=3$. Theorem 1.5 shows every point of a slightly smaller set (with $2\sqrt{d-1}$ replaced by $2\sqrt{d-1} + 1/\sqrt{d-1}$) is indeed a limit point of $d$-regular graph sequences.
paper_title: The limit points of the top and bottom eigenvalues of regular graphs

title: Problem 3
statement_text: Does every graph on $n$ vertices admit a strongly-separating path system of size $2n$?
context_text: The upper bound of $19n$ from Theorem 2 is likely far from optimal. Balogh et al. showed that the complete bipartite graph $K_{\varepsilon n,(1-\varepsilon)n}$ cannot be strongly separated by fewer than $2(1-2\varepsilon)n$ paths, but no graph with a larger lower bound is known, suggesting the optimal constant might be $2$.
paper_title: Separating the edges of a graph by a linear number of paths

title: Problem 4
statement_text: Does every properly edge-colored graph $G$ admit $O(|V(G)|)$ rainbow paths that cover $E(G)$?
context_text: This question arose from initial attempts to solve Conjecture 1; a rainbow path is a path containing no two edges of the same color. An affirmative answer would yield an alternative proof of Conjecture 1 via a path decomposition argument, and the authors note that a version with 'trails' replacing 'paths' can already be verified.
paper_title: Separating the edges of a graph by a linear number of paths

title: Question 3.5
statement_text: Is it true that for every tournament $T$, there exists $\prec\in\mathfrak{S}(T)$ such that $\prec$ is both a $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-ordering and a $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-ordering?
context_text: The paper establishes that for any $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-ordering $\prec$ of a tournament $T$, one has $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}(T)\leq\chi(T^{\prec})\leq\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}(T)^{2}$. The question asks whether a single ordering can simultaneously witness both the clique number and the dichromatic number.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

title: Question 3.10
statement_text: Is it true that if a class of tournaments $\mathcal{T}$ is polynomially $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded, then so is $\mathcal{T}^{subst}$.
context_text: The hereditary closure of $\{\widetilde{S}_{n},n\in\mathbb{N}\}$ equals $\{TT_{1},TT_{2},\overrightarrow{C_{3}}\}^{subst}$, which satisfies an exponential bound, and it is open whether the order of magnitude $(3/2)^n$ of $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}(\widetilde{S}_n)$ can be reduced to a polynomial. The question asks whether polynomial $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-boundedness is preserved under substitution closure.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

title: Conjecture 3.12
statement_text: Let $k\geq 1$. The class of tournaments with twin-width at most $k$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded.
context_text: For every integer $k\geq 2$, the tournament $S_k$ has twin-width $1$ and dichromatic number $k$, so tournaments with bounded twin-width can have arbitrarily large dichromatic number. Nevertheless, the authors conjecture that $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-boundedness holds. Conjecture 3.13 is presented as a sufficient condition implying this conjecture.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

statement_text: There exists a function $f$, such that for every tournament $T$, there exists an ordering $\prec^{*}$ of $V(T)$ such that:
context_text: This conjecture is introduced as a strengthening implying Conjecture 3.12 on $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-boundedness of bounded-twin-width tournaments. It concerns the existence of a single ordering simultaneously witnessing relevant structural and coloring properties.

statement_text: There exists a function $f$ such that, for every tournament $T$, there exists a $BST$-ordering $\prec$ of $T$ such:
context_text: $BST$-orderings are identified as natural candidates for the ordering of Conjecture 3.13. This conjecture specialises Conjecture 3.13 to $BST$-orderings, which arise from the theory of binary search trees on tournaments.

title: Conjecture 4.3 (Gyárfás-Sumner for Tournaments)
statement_text: A tournament $H$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-binding if and only if $H$ has a backedge graph which is a forest.
context_text: The authors propose this as the directed analogue of the celebrated Gyárfás-Sumner Conjecture, where a tournament $H$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-binding if the class of tournaments not containing $H$ as a subtournament is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded. The 'only if' direction is proved as Theorem 4.4, and it is shown that it suffices to prove the 'if' direction for trees.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

title: Conjecture 4.11
statement_text: Let $(M,\prec)$ be an ordered graph with maximum degree $1$. Then the class of $(M,\prec)$-free ordered graphs is $\chi$-bounded.
context_text: This conjecture on ordered graphs arises as a sufficient condition for proving that certain tournament classes are $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded, via the correspondence between backedge graphs and $\chi$-boundedness established in the paper. It follows from Property 4.10 that Conjecture 4.11 would imply results about $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-binding tournaments.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

title: Conjecture 5.3 (Large dom implies an ω̄-cluster)
statement_text: There exist two functions $f$ and $\ell$ such that, for every integer $k$, every tournament $T$ with $\operatorname{dom}(T)\geq f(k)$ contains a subtournament $X$ with $|X|\leq\ell(k)$ and $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(X)\geq k$.
context_text: This conjecture is intermediate between Theorem 5.1 (large dom implies a $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-cluster) and Conjecture 5.2 (large dom implies a dom-cluster), since $\operatorname{dom}(T)\leq\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\leq\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}(T)$. Conjecture 5.3 implies Conjecture 5.6 via Theorem 5.7.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

title: Conjecture 5.6
statement_text: There exists a function $g$ such that, for every integer $t$, if $T$ is a tournament such that for every $v\in V(T)$, $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(N^{+}(v))\leq t$, then $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\leq g(t)$.
context_text: This is the clique-number analogue of Theorem 4.8 (Harutyunyan-Le-Thomassé-Wu), which asserts that bounded local dichromatic number implies bounded global dichromatic number. Theorem 5.7 shows that Conjecture 5.3 implies Conjecture 5.6.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

title: Question 5.9
statement_text: Is there a function $\ell$ such that, for every tournament $T$, if $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)\geq k$, then $T$ has a subtournament $A$ such that $|A|\leq\ell(k)$ and $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(A)\geq k$.
context_text: This is a stronger form of Conjecture 5.8 in which the function $f$ is taken to be the identity. The authors could not disprove it, and note that Conjecture 5.10 (infinitely many $k$-$\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-critical tournaments) would answer it negatively.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

title: Conjecture 5.10
statement_text: For every integer $k\geq 3$, there is an infinite number of $k$-$\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-critical tournaments.
context_text: A tournament $T$ is $k$-$\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}$-critical if $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T)=k$ and $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\omega}}(T-v)=k-1$ for every $v\in V(T)$. For $k=1,2$ there is exactly one such tournament; the conjecture asserts infinitely many exist for all $k\geq 3$. If true it answers Question 5.9 negatively; if false it answers it positively.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

title: Conjecture 6.1
statement_text: If a class of digraphs $\mathcal{C}$ is $\operatorname{\overrightarrow{\chi}}$-bounded, then so is its closure under substitution.
context_text: In the concluding section the authors generalise Question 3.10 (which asked the same for tournaments) to all digraphs. They note that Theorem 3.3 does not hold for general digraphs, making the digraph setting harder, and discuss partial positive evidence from the tournament case.
paper_title: Clique number of tournaments

title: Conjecture (product structure with bounded tree-depth and tight clique size)
statement_text: For any hereditary graph class $\mathcal{G}$ that admits $O(n^{1-\epsilon})$ separators, every $n$-vertex graph in $\mathcal{G}$ is a subgraph of the strong product of a graph $H$ with bounded tree-depth and a complete graph of size $O(n^{1-\epsilon})$.
context_text: The abstract states that the paper investigates this conjecture. The proven results in the paper achieve the conclusion either with an extra $n^{\delta}$ factor in the clique size (for $H$ with constant tree-depth, any fixed $\delta\in(0,\epsilon)$) or with tree-depth $O(\log\log n)$ for $H$ when $\delta=0$; moreover, both limitations are shown to be best possible via isoperimetric inequalities for grid graphs. The conjecture in its strongest form — constant tree-depth of $H$ with clique size exactly $O(n^{1-\epsilon})$ — is investigated but not fully resolved.

title: Girth Conjecture (strengthening of Theorem 3)
statement_text: For every triangle-free graph $F$, there is a constant $c_F$ and graphs $G$ of arbitrarily large chromatic number and the same girth as $F$ such that every $F$-free induced subgraph of $G$ is $c_F$-colourable.
context_text: Theorem 3 guarantees, for triangle-free $F$, a triangle-free $G$, but does not control the girth of $G$. The authors ask whether $G$ can be taken to have the same girth as $F$, conjecture that this is so, but are unable to prove it. As a partial result they prove Theorem 4, the weaker analogue where girth is replaced by odd girth.

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: For every $d, \Delta \in \mathbb{N}$ there exists a constant $C = C(d, \Delta) > 0$ such that the following holds. If $k \in \mathbb{N}$, $G$ is a graph with $\Delta(G) \leq \Delta$ and $X, Y \subseteq V(G)$, then there exists either (1) $k$ $X$-$Y$-paths pairwise at distance at least $d$ in $G$, or (2) a set of less than $Ck$ vertices in $G$ which separates $X$ and $Y$.
context_text: This conjecture is derived as a consequence of the stronger (c independent of k) form of Conjecture 1.2 for graphs of bounded maximum degree $\Delta$: the ball $B_G(Z, cd)$ can be bounded in size by $\Delta^{cd+1}k$, yielding a linear separator of size $Ck$. The paper proves this conjecture in the first non-trivial case $d = 2$ as its first main result (Theorem 1.5).
notes: PDF source. The $d = 2$ case is proved within the paper (Theorem 1.5); the conjecture for $d \geq 3$ remains open.
paper_title: On an induced version of Menger's theorem

title: Open problem on happy triples for $l < k/2$
statement_text: It would be interesting to consider the best upper bound on the number of happy triples in a graph with $k$ edges and maximum degree at most $l$, for the regime when $l < k/2$.

The authors note that their algorithmic/dynamic-programming approach gives little insight into the $l < k/2$ regime; for example, for $l=3$ and $k=3t$ the extremal graph output is $K_{t,3}$, which does not actually have maximum degree 3 for $t \geq 4$.

title: Question 1
statement_text: Under what conditions is a finite $k$-dimensional cube complex $X \hookrightarrow \mathbb{R}^k$ reconstructible up to combinatorial type from its boundary distances?
context_text: This question arises as the natural higher-dimensional generalisation of Haslegrave's 2D result (Theorem 1), where a planar quadrangulation with all internal vertices of degree at least 4 is determined by its boundary distances. The paper answers it for $k=3$ via Theorem 2 under the CAT(0) condition, while the cases $k \geq 4$ are noted to involve non-trivial complications discussed in Section 10.
paper_title: Boundary rigidity of 3D CAT(0) cube complexes

title: Open Problem: m(C_t × C_s) for all t, s
statement_text: Determine $m(C_t \times C_s)$ for all $t, s$.
context_text: Proposition 3.2 establishes that $m(C_3 \times C_s) \leq 8$ for all $s > 36$ (since $K_3 = C_3$), answering a question raised in [6] about whether the connectivity code for large tori is strictly smaller than the trivial edge-connectivity bound $2^4 = 16$. The paper explicitly notes that the broader problem of determining $m(C_t \times C_s)$ for all $t$ and $s$ remains open.
notes: PDF source; $\times$ denotes the Cartesian (box) product of graphs. The special case $t=3$ ($K_3=C_3$) is resolved by Proposition 3.2; all other cases remain open.
paper_title: Connectivity Graph-Codes

title: Conjecture 2.1
statement_text: $p_{\mathrm{intersecting}}(t)$ tends to $0$ as $t$ grows.
context_text: This is a strengthening of Levine's Conjecture 1.1 in the setting where winning sets are intersecting families on $\{0,1\}^n$ rather than dictatorships. Since $p_{\mathrm{intersecting}}(t,n) \geq p_{\mathrm{dict}}(t,n)$, this conjecture is strictly stronger than Conjecture 1.1.
paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs

title: Conjecture 2.2
statement_text: $p_{\mathrm{monotone}}(t)$ tends to $0$ as $t$ grows.
context_text: This is progressively stronger than Conjecture 2.1, as $p_{\mathrm{monotone}}(t) \geq p_{\mathrm{intersecting}}(t) \geq p_{\mathrm{dict}}(t)$, where winning sets are all balanced monotone families on $\{0,1\}^n$. The authors describe these two conjectures as 'progressively stronger than Conjecture 1.1'.
paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs

title: Conjecture 2.6
statement_text: $$\lim_{t \to \infty} \lim_{n \to \infty} \bar{\alpha}\!\left(K(n)^{\square t}\right) = 0.$$
context_text: This is a graph-theoretic restatement of Conjecture 2.1, using the observation that $p_{\mathrm{intersecting}}(t,n) = \bar{\alpha}(K(n)^{\square t})$, where $K(n)$ is the Kneser graph on $\{0,1\}^n$ (edge between $x$ and $y$ if their supports are disjoint) and $\square$ denotes the Hamming product of graphs.
notes: PDF source — the Hamming product symbol appears as a garbled glyph (cid:3) in the extraction; rendered here as \square per context.
paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs

title: Conjecture 2.8
statement_text: $\varepsilon^*(\alpha) > 0$ for all $\alpha \in (0, 1/2)$.
context_text: Here $\varepsilon^*(\alpha) = \inf_{G:\,\bar{\alpha}(G)\geq\alpha}(\bar{\alpha}(G) - \alpha^*(G))$, where $\alpha^*(G)$ is defined via an optimal partition of $V(G)^r$ and a positively-correlated random-subset distribution with Bernoulli$(1/2)$ marginals. This conjecture would imply Conjecture 2.6 and hence Levine's conjecture.
notes: PDF source — some definitions in Section 2.4 rely on notation that may be partially garbled; the conjecture statement itself is clearly readable.
paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs

title: Conjecture 2.9
statement_text: $\varepsilon^{**}(\alpha) > 0$ for all $\alpha \in (0, 1/2)$. In other words, there exists a monotone non-decreasing function $\varepsilon^{**} : (0,1/2) \to (0,1/2)$ such that the following holds: if $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with maximum independent set of size $\alpha n$, $W$ is a binomial random subset of $V(G)$, and $I_W$ is the maximal independent set contained in $W$, then $$\alpha^{**}(G) = E_W\!\left[|I_W|/n\right] \leq \alpha - \varepsilon^{**}(\alpha).$$
context_text: This is the special case of Conjecture 2.8 where the random-subset distribution is simply binomial (each vertex included independently with probability $1/2$), which the authors describe as 'a fundamental problem in the study of independent sets in graphs'. The paper proves this for $\alpha > 1/4$ (Theorem 2.10) and for regular graphs with $\alpha > 1/8$ (Theorem 2.11).
paper_title: The success probability in Levine's hat problem, and independent sets in graphs

statement_text: It would be interesting to extend the result $r_2(G; 2) \leq 2^{c\sqrt{m}}$ (for any graph $G$ with $m$ edges and no isolated vertices) to more than two colors.
context_text: Sudakov [22] resolved Erdős's conjecture by showing $r_2(G; 2) \leq 2^{c\sqrt{m}}$, but the argument works only for two colors. The paper notes this gap immediately after mentioning the resolution of the conjecture.

title: Informal Conjecture (Equality in Theorem 1.1 near the zero-rate threshold)
statement_text: We suspect that there may even be equality in Theorem 1.1 when $s$ is sufficiently close to $(1 - 1/q)r$, i.e., $R(q+1; r, s) = A_q(r, s) + 1$, though our methods fall somewhat short of proving this.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 (from prior work [7]) establishes the lower bound $R(q+1; r, s) \geq A_q(r, s) + 1$ for all positive integers $q, r, s$ with $r > s$. The current paper proves Theorem 1.2, an upper bound showing $R(q+1; r, s) \leq (1+\epsilon)A_q(r, s - cj)$ when $s$ is close to $(1-1/q)r$, but the gap between lower and upper bounds remains. The authors conjecture the lower bound is essentially sharp in this intermediate regime.

title: Gap in $r$-dependence of sign-pattern bound
statement_text: The bound in Theorem 2.2 must have at least a linear dependence on $r$ if it is to depend polynomially on $m$; the current bound is exponential in $r$. It would be interesting to close this gap.
context_text: Proposition 2.5 demonstrates that $r = 2^{m+1}$ functions suffice to realise all $2^m$ proper sign patterns even when $N = 1$ and $\Delta = s = 2$ are fixed, yielding a linear lower bound on the $r$-dependence that is far below the exponential upper bound in Theorem 2.2. The paper notes this discrepancy and calls for closing it.

paper_title: Ordering Candidates via Vantage Points

title: Gap in connected-component count for sums of square roots
statement_text: For $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ a linear combination of $r$ square roots of everywhere-positive quadratic polynomials, the number of connected components of $\mathbb{R} \setminus V(f)$ is at most $2^{r-1}+1$, while at least $2r-1$ components are achievable. It would be interesting to narrow the gap between this linear lower bound and the exponential upper bound.
context_text: As a simpler companion question to the sign-pattern gap, the authors fix $N = m = 1$ and $\Delta = s = 2$. The upper bound $2^{r-1}+1$ follows from the same conjugate-multiplication trick as Theorem 2.2; an explicit construction with parameters $0 < a_1 < \cdots < a_{r-1}$ growing extremely quickly achieves $2r - 1$ connected components, giving a linear lower bound versus an exponential upper bound.
notes: PDF source — mathematical expressions (e.g. $2^{r-1}+1$ vs $2r-1$) may be garbled; values interpreted from the authors' explicit statement 'linear lower bound and exponential upper bound'.

title: Five-point protrusive-but-not-witnessed construction
statement_text: Does there exist a 5-element set $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ such that some protrusive ordering of $C$ is not contained in $\Psi(C)$?
context_text: The authors construct a 6-element set $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ for which $\Psi(C)$ is strictly smaller than the set of all protrusive orderings, showing that the equality $\Psi(C) = \{\text{protrusive orderings of } C\}$ (which holds for $d=1$, $n \le 4$, and vertex-transitive polytopes) fails in general. Whether 5 points suffice for a counterexample is explicitly left open.
paper_title: Ordering Candidates via Vantage Points

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: A tournament has the strong EH-property if and only if it admits a numbering for which the backedge graph is a forest.
context_text: The paper proves (Theorem 1.4) that the forward direction holds: every tournament with the strong EH-property admits a numbering for which the backedge graph is a forest. The authors initially thought the converse unlikely but failed to find a counterexample. This would be a tournament analogue of Theorem 1.3, which characterises graphs with the strong EH-property as those for which one of $H$ or $\bar{H}$ is a forest.
paper_title: Pure pairs. X. Tournaments and the strong Erdos-Hajnal property

title: Informal question: can the D5-freeness hypothesis in Theorem 1.6 be dropped?
statement_text: Perhaps the second sentence in 1.6 (the condition that $H$ is $D_5$-free) can be omitted, but that remains open.
context_text: Theorem 1.6 states that if a tournament $H$ admits a numbering $(v_1,\ldots,v_n)$ whose backedge graph $B$ has at most three edges, and $H$ is $D_5$-free, then $H$ has the strong EH-property. The $D_5$-freeness condition excludes exactly the case where the three backedges form the unique three-edge backedge graph of $D_5$; the authors leave open whether this exclusion is truly necessary.
paper_title: Pure pairs. X. Tournaments and the strong Erdos-Hajnal property

title: Question 1.2
statement_text: Is the maximum separable index among permutations avoiding a pattern of size $k$ polynomial in $k$?
context_text: The authors' main theorem gives a doubly exponential upper bound $2^{2^{O(k)}}$ on the separable index of permutations avoiding a pattern of size $k$. Lower bounds from Fox's results on growth of pattern-avoiding classes show the maximum separable index is at least $\Omega(k^{1/4-\varepsilon})$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$, leaving a large gap. The question asks whether a polynomial upper bound might hold.
paper_title: Factoring Pattern-Free Permutations into Separable ones

title: Question 1.3
statement_text: What is the parameterised complexity of computing $\mathrm{sep}(\sigma)$, and of approximating it within a constant factor?
context_text: The paper's main result implies an FPT approximation for the separable index $\mathrm{sep}(\sigma)$ via pattern recognition, but the approximation function is triple exponential in $k$. The authors ask whether the complexity of computing or approximating $\mathrm{sep}(\sigma)$ exactly can be improved, possibly to an exact algorithm.
paper_title: Factoring Pattern-Free Permutations into Separable ones

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: For every fixed positive $p \leq 1$, Erdős-Rényi random graph $G(n, 1-p)$ a.a.s. has correspondence chromatic number $\Theta(n/\log n)$.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 gives the upper bound $O(n/\sqrt{\log n})$, and both extreme cases of the correspondence assignment—matching the list-coloring setting and a fully random assignment for $K_n$ (Theorem 1.3)—only require $O(n/\log n)$ colors. The authors suspect the upper bound can be improved to match the lower bound $\Omega(n/\log n)$ coming from the independence number. They also note that a disproof would yield a new example separating list and correspondence chromatic numbers.
notes: PDF source; special characters (Erdős-Rényi) rendered via ASCII in extraction but mathematical content is unambiguous.
paper_title: Correspondence coloring of random graphs

title: Informal Belief on Non-Optimality of p-Goodness Bound
statement_text: The bound $n \geq 2^{k^{10p}}$ in Theorem 1.1 is not best possible.
context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.1, the authors remark that they do not expect the bound $n \geq 2^{k^{10p}}$ to be best possible, and indicate that they discuss this further in the concluding remarks (Section 4).
notes: PDF source — exponent reconstructed as $k^{10p}$ from surrounding proof; stated in prose as 'We do not expect the bound $n \geq 2k^{10p}$ to be best possible'.
paper_title: Ramsey goodness of books revisited

title: Question on Eliminating the Regularity Lemma from Nikiforov–Rousseau's General Theorem
statement_text: Is it possible to completely eliminate the use of the regularity lemma from the proof of the general Ramsey goodness theorem of Nikiforov and Rousseau (Theorem 2.1 of \cite{28})?
context_text: The authors show that their techniques can avoid the regularity lemma for Theorem 1.3, a special case of Nikiforov–Rousseau's most general goodness result. They ask whether this elimination can be extended to the full generality of that theorem, noting it would be very interesting to see how far these ideas can be pushed.
notes: Posed in prose in the introduction as 'for example, is it possible to completely eliminate the use of the regularity lemma from the proof of [28, Theorem 2.1]?'.
paper_title: Ramsey goodness of books revisited

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: The $\mathcal{H}$-square-root problem is NP-complete for the class $\mathcal{H}$ of planar graphs.
context_text: The authors prove NP-completeness of the H-square-root problem for the class of 6-apex graphs (a proper minor-closed class), and note that 6-apex graphs lie at the bottom of the bounded-expansion hierarchy. They ask whether apex vertices are truly necessary or whether planarity alone already makes the problem hard, and state they believe the latter is the case.
paper_title: Square roots of nearly planar graphs

title: Question 1
statement_text: Can the upper bound on $\operatorname{utw}(\mathcal{G}_{X})$ in Equation 1 be improved? In particular, is $\operatorname{utw}(\mathcal{G}_{X})$ at most a polynomial function of $\operatorname{td}(X)$?
context_text: The paper's main theorem (Theorem 1) gives an upper bound on the underlying treewidth of $X$-minor-free graph classes in terms of $\operatorname{td}(X)$. This question opens the concluding open-problems section and asks whether that bound can be tightened to a polynomial dependence on $\operatorname{td}(X)$.
paper_title: The grid-minor theorem revisited

title: Question 2
statement_text: Is there a function $f$ such that for every graph $X$ there exists a function $c$ such that for every positive integer $t$ and for every graph $G$ with $\operatorname{tw}(G)<t$ that does not contain $X$ as a topological minor, there exists a graph $H$ of treewidth at most $f(\operatorname{td}(X))$ such that $G\mathrel{\ooalign{\raise 0.75348pt\hbox{$\subset$}\cr\raise-3.87495pt\hbox{\scalebox{0.9}{$\sim$}}\cr}}H\boxtimes K_{c(t)}$?
context_text: This question asks whether Theorem 2 can be extended from the excluded-minor setting to the excluded-topological-minor setting. Campbell et al. proved a weaker version with $\operatorname{tw}(H)\leqslant|V(X)|$, and that bound is tight for complete graphs.
notes: The subgraph-isomorphism symbol uses \ooalign and is reproduced verbatim from the LaTeX source.
paper_title: The grid-minor theorem revisited

title: Question 3
statement_text: Is there a function $g$ such that for every graph $X$, there is a constant $c$ such that for every $X$-minor-free graph $G$, $\chi_{p}(G)\leqslant c\cdot p^{g(\operatorname{td}(X))}$ for every $p\geqslant 1$?
context_text: This question concerns $p$-centered colorings of $X$-minor-free graphs. The paper gives a positive answer when $X$ is apex, but cannot extend the proof to arbitrary excluded minors because the technique using chordal partitions does not transfer to $p$-centered colorings.
paper_title: The grid-minor theorem revisited

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: Every sufficiently large integer is polite.
context_text: Conjecture 1.5 appears early in the paper with no surrounding context recovered. The term 'polite' is likely a graph-theoretic notion defined in the paper (possibly related to Ramsey multiplicity or the Turán coloring extremality), but the context was not captured in extraction.
notes: Statement contains no mathematical notation and context is entirely absent — likely PDF extraction artifact; 'polite' may be a technical term defined in Definition 5.1 of this paper whose full content was not provided.
paper_title: Ramsey multiplicity and the Turán coloring

title: Conjecture 5.3
statement_text: There exist a mixed blowup coloring of $K_{r(k)-1}[2]$ for infinitely many $k$.
context_text: The conjecture arises from Lemma 5.2, which reduces finding a mixed blowup coloring to exhibiting two Ramsey colorings of $K_{r(k)-1}$ that differ on a single vertex. The authors note they cannot establish this for any $k$, partly because $r(k)$ is only known for $k=3,4$, and for both values the Ramsey coloring of $K_{r(k)-1}$ is unique with all vertices having the same red and blue degree.
paper_title: Ramsey multiplicity and the Turán coloring

title: Conjecture 5.4
statement_text: If $k\geq 4$ and $t$ is sufficiently large in terms of $k$, then any $(k,t)$-generalized lollipop is a two-color bonbon.
context_text: Theorem 1.2 proves that adding $t\geq(1000kh)^{10}h^{10k}$ pendant edges to a $k$-critical graph $H_0$ with $h$ vertices yields a bonbon, but the proof requires $t$ at least exponentially large in $k$. Moreover, if $H_0=K_k$ then some lower bound on $t$ is necessary since adding $o(k^2/\log k)$ pendant edges to $K_k$ does not produce a bonbon. The conjecture proposes that sufficiently many pendant edges always suffice for any $k$-critical base graph.
paper_title: Ramsey multiplicity and the Turán coloring

also_stated_in.title: Ramsey multiplicity and the Turán coloring

title: Question 1.1
statement_text: Which finite families $F$ of digraphs are $\vec{\chi}$-finite?
context_text: A family $F$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite if there is a constant $c$ such that all $F$-free digraphs have dichromatic number at most $c$. This is the overarching open question motivating the study of heroes in hereditary classes of digraphs, and all main results of the paper contribute to it.
notes: Stated without a parenthetical attribution in the theorem header; the surrounding prose credits Aboulker, Charbit, and Naserasr [2] with first systematically studying this question.
paper_title: On heroes in digraphs with forbidden induced forests

title: Open problem: hero status of $\Delta(1,m,m')$ for degree-4 oriented stars
statement_text: Determine for which integers $m, m' \geq 1$ the set $\{\Delta(1, m, m'), F\}$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite when $F$ is an oriented star of degree $4$.
context_text: Theorem 1.6 shows that if $F$ is an oriented star of degree 4 and $\{H, F\}$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite, then either $H$ is a transitive tournament or $H = \Delta(1, m, m')$ for some $m, m' \geq 1$. The transitive tournament case is fully settled by Theorem 1.4 (Chudnovsky–Scott–Seymour), but the authors explicitly state that the case $H = \Delta(1, m, m')$ remains open.
notes: Open status identified in prose immediately after Theorem 1.6; no explicit labeled environment.
paper_title: On heroes in digraphs with forbidden induced forests

title: Open problem: hero status of $\Delta(1,2,2)$
statement_text: Determine whether $\Delta(1,2,2)$ is a hero in $\{K_1 + \vec{P}_2\}$-free digraphs, i.e., whether $\{\Delta(1,2,2),\, K_1 + \vec{P}_2\}$ is $\vec{\chi}$-finite.
context_text: Theorem 1.11 (Aboulker, Aubian, Charbit) almost completely characterizes heroes in $\{K_1+\vec{P}_2\}$-free digraphs, and Theorem 1.12 of this paper extends the same structural characterization to $\{H, rK_1+\vec{P}_3\}$-free digraphs; in both settings the sole unresolved case is $H = \Delta(1,2,2)$.
notes: Open status noted twice: once after Theorem 1.11 and again after Theorem 1.12.
paper_title: On heroes in digraphs with forbidden induced forests

title: Conjecture 1.7
statement_text: For all positive integers $l$ and $k$, $t(l, k) = l^{k-1}$.
context_text: Here $t(l,k)$ denotes the smallest integer such that every colouring of the edges of any tournament with more than $t(l,k)$ vertices contains an $l$-flash or a $k$-rainbow. Since $t(l,k) \geq f(l,k) \geq l^{k-1}$, this is a tournament strengthening of the Lefmann–Rödl–Thomas Conjecture 1.2. The paper proves two upper bounds on $t(l,k)$ confirming it asymptotically for $l = \omega(k^2)$, and also shows that some tournaments are strictly worse than transitive tournaments (Theorem 1.10).
paper_title: Flashes and rainbows in tournaments

title: Grassmannian Subvariety Construction Problem
statement_text: For each claw-free incomparability graph $G$, there should exist a subvariety of the Grassmannian whose cohomology class is represented by $X_G$ and whose K-theoretic structure sheaf class is represented by $\overline{X}_G$. It would be very interesting to have an explicit construction of such subvarieties.
context_text: The authors lift Gasharov's theorem to K-theory by showing that the Kromatic symmetric function of a claw-free incomparability graph is $\overline{s}$-positive. The existence of both a cohomological and a K-theoretic representative suggests a geometric interpretation in terms of Grassmannian subvarieties.
