notes: Stated as 'there should be' and 'it would be very interesting'; no labelled theorem environment. PDF source — overline notation for $\overline{X}_G$ may be garbled in extraction.
paper_title: The Kromatic Symmetric Function: A $K$-theoretic Analogue of $X_G$

title: Problem 1
statement_text: Characterize the class $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs $H$ such that $f_\ell(H') = v(H')-1$ for all $H' \subseteq H$.
context_text: The authors ask for a structural characterization of graphs $H$ for which the list chromatic number of $H$-minor-free graphs matches the trivial lower bound $v(H)-1$. The problem is posed in the spirit of perfect graph theory, seeking the largest subgraph-closed class with this property; the paper's main results (Theorems 2 and 4) strongly limit the horizon for positive instances.
paper_title: On the choosability of $H$-minor-free graphs

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: For any simple graph $G$ of order $n$, $E[F] \leq \frac{1}{3}n + 1$.
context_text: The paper proves $E[F] \leq \frac{\pi^2}{6}n$ for simple graphs (Theorem 8), but no examples approaching this constant are known. A chain of triangles connected by cut edges achieves $E[F] = \frac{1}{3}n+1$, which the authors conjecture is the true optimal bound.
notes: PDF source; fraction $\frac{1}{3}$ appears as '1\n3' in raw PDF text but context is unambiguous.
paper_title: Expected number of faces in a random embedding of any graph is at most linear

title: Informal Conjecture (every forest is good)
statement_text: Perhaps every forest is good (i.e., for every forest $H$, the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded).
context_text: After recalling that Esperet's conjecture that every $\chi$-bounded hereditary class is polynomially $\chi$-bounded was disproved, the authors ask which hereditary classes are polynomially $\chi$-bounded and raise in particular whether the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture can be strengthened to polynomial $\chi$-boundedness. They note that among trees, only those not containing $P_5$ are currently known to be good, and that it is not even known whether $P_5$ is good.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VI. Adding a four-vertex path

title: Conjecture 2.3
statement_text: Let $\psi, \sigma : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ be non-decreasing polynomials, let $q \geq 0$ an integer, and let $H$ be a broom. Then there is a non-decreasing polynomial $\phi : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that if $G$ is a graph, and $\chi(G) > \phi(\omega(G))$, and $G$ contains no $(\psi, q)$-scattering, then $G$ [contains] a $\sigma$-nondominating copy of $H$.
context_text: The authors remark that Conjecture 2.3 is 'an appealing possible strengthening of 2.2 that we could not prove.' Theorem 2.2 (which is proved) requires $H$ to be a broom and $J$ a path, finding both; Conjecture 2.3 would give a $\sigma$-nondominating copy of a broom alone without needing a path. This would extend the self-isolating property (proved for paths as Corollary 2.4) to brooms.
notes: PDF extraction: the phrase 'then G a σ-nondominating copy of H' is missing the word 'contains'; reconstructed in brackets. Statement otherwise clear.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VI. Adding a four-vertex path

title: Conjecture 1.6
statement_text: Every forest is multibounding.
context_text: A graph $H$ is multibounding if for every $d \geq 1$ there is a polynomial $f(t)$ such that, for all $t \geq 1$, every $H$-free graph with no $K_d(t)$ subgraph satisfies $\chi(G) \leq f(t)$. Every multibounding graph must be a forest satisfying the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture, and any forest satisfying the polynomial-bound Conjecture 1.2 is multibounding. The paper establishes that paths, brooms, and disjoint unions of multibounding forests are multibounding, motivating the general conjecture.
notes: Explicitly introduced with 'We propose: 1.6 Conjecture' in the paper.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VIII. Excluding a path and a complete multipartite graph

title: Conjecture on the asymptotic of n(2,d)
statement_text: A general conjecture is posed in the final section which, together with the lower bound $n(2,d) > (1-o(1))\frac{d^2}{2}$, would imply that $\lim_{d\to\infty} \frac{n(2,d)}{d^2} = \frac{1}{2}$.
context_text: The authors establish the lower bound $n(2,d) > (1-o(1))\frac{d^2}{2}$, improving the previous bound $n(2,d) > \frac{d^2}{4}$. They remark that determining the precise asymptotic order of $n(2,d)$ seems to be a hard task (as noted earlier by Alon and by Huang–Sudakov), and refer to a formal conjecture in the final section concerning lamination-based constructions.
notes: The formal conjecture appears in Section 3 (the final section on total laminations and computational experiments), which is not present in the provided PDF extract. Only the informal forward-reference in the introduction is available; math may be garbled due to PDF source.
paper_title: New bounds on the maximum number of neighborly boxes in R^d

title: Open question on existence of lim n(2,d)/d^2
statement_text: Does $\lim_{d\to\infty} \frac{n(2,d)}{d^2}$ exist?
context_text: The authors conjecture that if the limit exists it equals $\frac{1}{2}$, but explicitly remark that even the existence of the limit is currently unknown. The paper provides the lower bound $n(2,d) > (1-o(1))\frac{d^2}{2}$ via Theorem 2.
notes: Explicitly stated in the introduction: 'as yet we do not even know whether the limit above exists'. PDF source — math notation may be garbled.
paper_title: New bounds on the maximum number of neighborly boxes in R^d

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: For every planar graph $G$ of odd maximum degree $\Delta \geq 7$ the edges of $G$ can be partitioned into $\frac{\Delta-1}{2}$ linear forests and one matching.
context_text: This conjecture refines the known linear arboricity bound for planar graphs with odd maximum degree by replacing one linear forest with the weaker structure of a matching. The case $\Delta \geq 11$ follows from existing results; the paper resolves $\Delta = 9$ (Theorem 2), leaving $\Delta = 7$ as the sole remaining open case.
paper_title: Partitioning edges of a planar graph into linear forests and a matching

title: Polynomial bounds for path-induced copy theorem (1.2)
statement_text: Is there any hope for a comparable strengthening of 1.2, giving a polynomial bound on the chromatic number of graphs with no path-induced copy of a rooted tree $(T,r)$ and with clique number at most $t$?
context_text: Scott, Seymour and Spirkl proved (Theorem 3.2) that excluding an induced tree and $K_{t,t}$ as a subgraph yields a polynomial bound $f(t)$ on average degree. The authors ask whether an analogous polynomial bound on chromatic number can be obtained for 1.2, which imposes the weaker hypothesis of bounded clique number at the cost of weakening the conclusion to a path-induced copy.
notes: Question appears as rhetorical prose without a labelled environment; the sentence 'Is there any hope for a comparable strengthening of 1.2?' is the explicit formulation.
paper_title: A note on the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture

title: Polynomial bounds for the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture
statement_text: It is possible that the Gy\'{a}rf\'{a}s-Sumner conjecture holds with polynomial bounds; that is, 1.1 holds with a bound on the chromatic number that is polynomial in $t$.
context_text: The authors note that polynomial bounds on the $\chi$-bounding function have recently been established for trees not containing $P_5$ as an induced subgraph, and suggest that the full Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture may admit a polynomial $\chi$-bounding function in $t$ for every fixed tree $T$.
notes: Stated in prose as 'it is possible that…' without a labelled conjecture environment.
paper_title: A note on the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture

title: Question 1.1
statement_text: Let $\mathcal{H}$ be a family of graphs closed under isomorphism. Is it true that $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n)$ tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity if and only if $\mathcal{H}$ contains a graph with an even number of edges? Equivalently: is it true that for any fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges, $d_H(n)$ tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity?
context_text: The authors define $d_H(n)$ as the maximum fraction of all graphs on $[n]$ forming an $H$-code (a family with no two members whose symmetric difference is a copy of $H$). If every member of $\mathcal{H}$ has an odd number of edges then $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n) \geq 1/2$, motivating the question of whether containing an even-edge graph is exactly the condition that forces $d_{\mathcal{H}}(n) \to 0$. The question is stated as the central open problem and noted in the concluding remarks to remain wide open.
paper_title: Graph-codes

title: Informal question: is $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$?
statement_text: Is it true that $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$?
context_text: In the concluding remarks the authors single this out as an interesting special case of Question 1.1. The companion question of whether $d_{K_4}(n) \geq 1/n^{o(1)}$ is answered affirmatively within the paper by appealing to an edge-coloring construction of Hunter and Mubayi [5] (modifying [11] and [4]); the upper-bound direction $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$ remains open.
notes: The lower bound $d_{K_4}(n) \geq 1/n^{o(1)}$ is established in the paper; only the question of whether $d_{K_4}(n) = o(1)$ remains open.
paper_title: Graph-codes

title: Open problem: tighter bounds for linear graph-codes
statement_text: Establish tighter bounds for the maximum possible cardinality of a linear family of graphs on $[n]$ in which no symmetric difference is a copy of a fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges.
context_text: The paper notes that for any fixed graph $H$ with an even number of edges the maximum size of a linear $H$-code is $o\bigl(2^{\binom{n}{2}}\bigr)$, but the proof via Ramsey's Theorem yields very weak quantitative bounds. Theorem 1.6 gives a tight result for the family $\mathcal{K}$ of all cliques as a model for what such tight results could look like.
paper_title: Graph-codes

title: Open problem: binary vectors with cube-length forbidden symmetric difference
statement_text: What is the maximum possible cardinality of a collection of binary vectors whose coordinates are indexed by the elements of the ordered set $[n]$, where no symmetric difference of two distinct members of the collection forms an interval of length which is a cube of an integer?
context_text: This is posed as an illustrative example of extending graph-code questions to other combinatorial structures. The corresponding Cayley graph has $2^n$ vertices and is triangle-free by Fermat's Last Theorem for cubes; its independence number is $o(2^n)$ by the Furstenberg–Sárközy Theorem and its extensions.
paper_title: Graph-codes

title: Open problem: minimum edge-coloring with odd intersection property
statement_text: Determine or estimate the smallest number of colors in an edge coloring of $K_n$ in which every copy of a given graph $H$ (or every copy of any member of a prescribed family $\mathcal{H}$ of graphs) intersects at least one color class by an odd number of edges.
context_text: The authors propose this in the concluding remarks as a natural variant of classical Ramsey Theory questions arising from the coloring method used throughout the paper to produce lower bounds on independence numbers of Cayley graphs.
paper_title: Graph-codes

title: Informal Conjecture (chordal modulator characterization for even-hole-free graphs)
statement_text: Every chordal graph of clique number at most three modulates even-hole-free graphs.
context_text: Theorem 1.2 (Sintiari–Trotignon) shows that a graph $H$ can modulate even-hole-free graphs only if $H$ is a chordal graph of clique number at most three; the authors state that the converse may also be true. The paper proves the forest case (Theorem 1.6) but explicitly notes that extending to all chordal graphs of clique number at most three remains open.
notes: Stated as "as far as we know, the converse may also be true" in the introduction; confirmed open by the subsequent remark "while the former remains open".
paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions VIII. Excluding a forest in (theta, prism)-free graphs

title: Informal conjecture on sharp threshold for linear-proportion reconstruction
statement_text: The correct threshold for the existence of a reconstructible set of size $\Omega(n)$ is $p = (1+\varepsilon)/n$.
context_text: Theorem 1.2a is proved with constant 42, i.e., $p \geq 42/n$ suffices. The authors note in footnote 2 that the best their methods could give is $9/n$, and they believe the true threshold is $(1+\varepsilon)/n$, with further discussion deferred to Section 4 on open problems.
notes: Appears in footnote 2 as an informal belief. Section 4 (open problems) is not present in the extracted text, so additional conjectures from that section may be missing.
paper_title: Reconstructing a point set from a random subset of its pairwise distances

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: For every forest $H$, there is a polynomial $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\omega(G))$ for every $H$-free graph $G$.
context_text: This conjecture is presented as what the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture (1.1) and Esperet's conjecture (that every $\chi$-bounded hereditary class has a polynomial bounding function) would jointly imply for forest-free graph classes. Esperet's conjecture has been disproved in full generality but remains open for classes excluding a forest, and Conjecture 1.3 is known only for a few special families of trees.
notes: Stated in the paper without a parenthesised attribution; it is a natural combination of Conjecture 1.1 and Esperet's conjecture restricted to forest-free classes.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. V. Excluding a tree of radius two and a complete multipartite graph

title: Question: Extending Theorem 1.6 to paths
statement_text: Is it true that if $H$ is a path, then for every integer $d \geq 1$, there is a polynomial $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\tau_d(G))$ for every $H$-free graph $G$?
context_text: This question, suggested by a referee and included by the authors, asks whether the main result (Theorem 1.6 for trees of radius two) can be extended to paths—another prominent family of forests for which polynomial bounds in terms of $\tau_2(G)$ are already known (via results of Bonamy et al. and Scott-Seymour-Spirkl).
notes: Explicitly labelled as an open question suggested by a referee and incorporated into the paper.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. V. Excluding a tree of radius two and a complete multipartite graph

title: Question: Polynomial bound analogue of Esperet's conjecture for $\tau_d$
statement_text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a hereditary class of graphs, and $d \geq 1$. Suppose that there is a function $f$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(\tau_d(G))$ for each $G \in \mathcal{C}$. Can we always choose $f$ to be a polynomial? What if $d = 2$?
context_text: This question, suggested by a referee and included by the authors, asks for an analogue of Esperet's conjecture in the setting of $\tau_d(G)$ in place of $\omega(G)$. It is motivated by the polynomial bounds proved in Theorem 1.6 and asks whether being $\tau_d$-bounded always implies being polynomially $\tau_d$-bounded.
notes: Explicitly labelled as an open question suggested by a referee and incorporated into the paper.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. V. Excluding a tree of radius two and a complete multipartite graph

title: Conjecture 4
context_text: The authors prove $D(n,3) = \Omega(n^2/e^{O(\sqrt{\log n})})$ and a general lower bound $D(n,k) = n^{2\lfloor k/3\rfloor}/e^{O_k(\sqrt{\log n})}$, but were unable to show that lower and upper bounds almost match for $k \geq 4$. In particular, it is open whether the 4-configuration graph can have super-quadratic diameter, while the upper bound is $o(n^3)$.
paper_title: Extremal Independent Set Reconfiguration

title: Question 8
statement_text: What is the asymptotic behavior of $\max_k D(n, k)$?
context_text: The authors prove that for every $n$ and every $k$ with $3n/10 \leq k \leq 2n/5$, $D(n,k) = \Omega(2^{n/5})$, and remark that it is quite surprising that this exponential lower bound holds for such a wide range of values of $k$.
paper_title: Extremal Independent Set Reconfiguration

title: Conjecture on Sufficiency of Conditions E, R, T for Generic Tuples
statement_text: When $\Lambda$ is generic, Conditions E, R, and T are necessary and sufficient for an $m \times (n+1)$ matrix to be the multiplicity matrix of some polynomial $P$ with respect to $\Lambda$.
context_text: Theorem 1 of the paper establishes that Conditions E, R, T are necessary and sufficient for a $2 \times (n+1)$ matrix to be a multiplicity matrix. The authors note that for $m > 2$ these three conditions are not sufficient in general (almost all matrices satisfying E, R, C for large $m = n$ are not multiplicity matrices), but conjecture sufficiency is restored when $\Lambda$ is generic. Further discussion is deferred to Section 8.
notes: Stated in running prose in Section 3 without a labelled theorem environment; the authors write 'we conjecture that when $\Lambda$ is generic, Conditions E, R, and T are necessary and sufficient'.
paper_title: Counting Dope Matrices

title: Conjecture 5
statement_text: Every simple graph $G$ satisfies $\chi''_\ell(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 2$.
context_text: This is the list coloring analogue of the Total Coloring Conjecture (Conjecture 4), asserting that the list total chromatic number never exceeds $\Delta + 2$. It is stated without a bibliographic citation in the paper, appearing as a natural companion to Conjecture 4. The paper's main result (Corollary 1) proves Conjecture 5 for all planar graphs with $\Delta \geq 8$.
notes: PDF source — math notation reconstructed from Unicode. No citation appears in the conjecture header; the paper proves this conjecture for planar graphs with $\Delta \geq 8$ as its central result.
paper_title: Every planar graph with $Δ\geqslant 8$ is totally $(Δ+2)$-choosable

title: Problem 1.6
statement_text: What is the smallest constant $\alpha$ such that every $Z_3$-flow-critical graph $G$ satisfies $|E(G)| \leq \frac{5}{2}|V(G)| + \alpha g(G) + O(1)$?
context_text: Theorem 1.5 establishes $\alpha \leq 5/2$, while the example of $K^+_{3,n-3}$ (which has Euler genus $\lceil\frac{n-5}{2}\rceil$) shows $\alpha \geq 1$, giving $1 \leq \alpha \leq 5/2$. The authors remark that the dependence on genus in Theorem 1.5 is likely not optimal.
paper_title: On density of $Z_3$-flow-critical graphs

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: If $B$ is a bipartite oriented graph with a homomorphism $B \to \vec{K}_2$, then $B$ has the directed Sidorenko property.
context_text: Observation 3.1 establishes that having a homomorphism to a directed edge is a necessary condition for the directed Sidorenko property. The authors conjecture this condition is also sufficient for bipartite oriented graphs, giving a directed analogue of Sidorenko's conjecture.
notes: PDF source.
paper_title: A note on directed analogues of the Sidorenko and forcing conjectures

title: Conjecture 1.7
statement_text: If $B$ is an oriented graph with a homomorphism to an edge and whose underlying undirected graph has a cycle, then $B$ has the directed forcing property.
context_text: Theorem 1.6 establishes that admitting a homomorphism to a directed edge and having a cycle in the underlying undirected graph are both necessary conditions for the directed forcing property. The authors conjecture these two conditions together are also sufficient, providing a directed analogue of the Skokan–Thoma forcing conjecture.
notes: PDF source.
paper_title: A note on directed analogues of the Sidorenko and forcing conjectures

title: Equivalence of asymmetric and classical Sidorenko properties
statement_text: It is conjectured that the asymmetric Sidorenko property and the classical Sidorenko property are equivalent for bipartite graphs.
context_text: The asymmetric Sidorenko property is a priori stronger than the classical Sidorenko property. The authors note that essentially all bipartite graphs known to satisfy the classical Sidorenko property are also known to satisfy the asymmetric version, lending credence to the belief that the two properties coincide.
notes: Reported as a widely held community belief without explicit attribution to specific authors; no formal labelled environment.
paper_title: A note on directed analogues of the Sidorenko and forcing conjectures

title: Extension to H-free graphs
statement_text: Determine the maximum possible number of edges of an $H$-free graph on $n$ vertices with matching number at most $s$, for general forbidden subgraphs $H$ (extending Theorem 1.1 beyond the case $H = K_{k+1}$).
context_text: At the opening of Section 3, the authors note it may be interesting to extend Theorem 1.1 by replacing the forbidden clique $K_{k+1}$ by other forbidden subgraphs $H$. Proposition 3.1 settles this for color-critical $H$ when $s$ and $n$ are sufficiently large, but the problem for general $H$ (including non-color-critical graphs and small parameters) remains open.
notes: No labeled environment; posed as a research direction at the start of Section 3, partially addressed by Proposition 3.1 in the same section.
paper_title: Turán graphs with bounded matching number

title: Problem 1.2
statement_text: Fix an integer $r \geq 4$. Is it true that every $K_r$-free segment graph on $n$ vertices has an induced subgraph on $\Omega_r(n)$ vertices which is $K_{r-1}$-free?
context_text: Motivated by the approach of coloring $K_r$-free string graphs to prove Conjecture 1.1: if each color class could be made $K_4$-free, Ackerman's result would bound the edges per class by $O(n)$. Although Krawczyk–Walczak showed $K_r$-free string graphs can require $\Omega_r(\log\log n)$ colors with $K_{r-1}$-free classes, the question of finding a linearly large $K_{r-1}$-free induced subgraph in a $K_r$-free segment graph remains open. The paper's Theorems 1.5 and 1.6 address analogous questions for string graphs (with a $\log^2 n$ loss), but the segment-graph case with a linear guarantee is unresolved.
paper_title: Quasiplanar Graphs, String Graphs, and the Erdos-Gallai Problem

title: Conjecture 14
statement_text: There exist functions $g : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{N}$ and $h : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that for any $c > 1$, every graph $G$ with growth $f_G(r) \leq cr$ is isomorphic to a subgraph of $T \boxtimes K_{g(c)}$ for some tree $T$ with growth $f_T(r) \leq h(c)r$.
context_text: Theorem 13 shows that every graph $G$ with growth $f_G(r) \leq cr$ is a subgraph of $T \boxtimes K_{\lfloor 882c^3 \rfloor}$ for some tree $T$, but the tree $T$ itself may have exponential growth. Conjecture 14 proposes a rough characterisation of graphs of linear growth: if true, every subgraph $H$ of $T \boxtimes K_{g(c)}$ has growth $f_H(r) \leq g(c)h(c)r \in O(r)$, making the characterisation tight.
paper_title: Graphs of Linear Growth have Bounded Treewidth

also_stated_in.title: Graphs of Linear Growth have Bounded Treewidth

title: Open Problem (characterizing edge probabilities for Theorem 1.1)
statement_text: Characterize all edge probabilities $p = p(n)$ for which the assertion of Theorem 1.1 holds.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 is proved for fixed $p \in (0,1)$ and (via Proposition 2.1) for $p = p(n)$ satisfying $n^{-c} \leq p \leq 1-n^{-c}$. The authors note that hereditary (even monotone) properties exist for which the fraction of edges of $G(n,p)$ lying in a maximum in-$\mathcal{P}$ subgraph changes, whp, several times as $p$ increases from 0 to 1.
notes: PDF source; stated as an informal open problem in the concluding remarks without a labelled environment.
paper_title: Largest subgraph from a hereditary property in a random graph

title: Open Problem (accurate estimate in the bipartite case)
statement_text: Provide a more accurate estimate for $\mathrm{ex}(G, \mathcal{P})$ when $\mathcal{P}$ misses a bipartite graph (i.e., when Theorem 1.1 gives only $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p),\mathcal{P}) = o(n^2)$ whp).
context_text: When the minimum chromatic number $k$ of a graph not in $\mathcal{P}$ equals 2, Theorem 1.1 yields $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p),\mathcal{P}) = o(n^2)$ whp but does not determine the precise order of magnitude. The authors ask for a sharper asymptotic in this regime.
notes: PDF source; stated as an open problem in the concluding remarks without a labelled environment.
paper_title: Largest subgraph from a hereditary property in a random graph

title: Informal Conjecture (polynomial saving in the bipartite case)
statement_text: It seems plausible that in the bipartite case $\mathrm{ex}(G, \mathcal{P}) \leq n^{2-\varepsilon}$ for some $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(\mathcal{P}) > 0$.
context_text: When $\mathcal{P}$ misses a bipartite graph, Theorem 1.1 gives only $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p),\mathcal{P}) = o(n^2)$ whp. The authors speculate that the saving over the trivial $O(n^2)$ bound is in fact polynomial, not merely sub-polynomial.
notes: PDF source; signaled by the phrase 'It seems plausible that'. Math notation appears clean despite PDF extraction.
paper_title: Largest subgraph from a hereditary property in a random graph

title: Open problem: closing the gap for $d(s)$
statement_text: It remains an interesting open problem to close the gap between the lower and upper bounds: $\Omega(\log s) < \frac{s}{2} - d(s) < O(\sqrt{s\log s})$, where $d(s)$ denotes the largest integer such that every $2n$-vertex digraph of minimum out-degree at least $s$ contains an $n$-vertex subdigraph of minimum out-degree at least $d(s)$.
context_text: After disproving Alon's problem, the authors note that the true asymptotic behaviour of $\frac{s}{2} - d(s)$ lies somewhere between $\Omega(\log s)$ (established by their construction) and $O(\sqrt{s\log s})$ (established by Alon's probabilistic argument). The precise asymptotics of $d(s)$ remain open.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled environment; PDF source may garble some exponent notation.
paper_title: Subdigraphs of prescribed size and outdegree

title: Conjecture 1.6
statement_text: For all $t > 0$ there exists $c_t \geq 0$ such that $\mathrm{tw}(G) \leq c_t$ for every $G \in \mathcal{C}^*_t$.
context_text: Here $\mathcal{C}^*_t$ denotes the class of $(C_4, \text{diamond}, \text{theta}, \text{prism}, \text{even wheel}, K_t)$-free graphs — a relaxation of $\mathcal{C}_t$ in which pyramids are no longer excluded. The authors pose this as a slight generalization of Sintiari–Trotignon's Conjecture 1.5, noting that Theorem 1.4 (the main result of the paper) would serve as the base case if the conjecture could be proved by the same techniques.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions IV. (Even hole, diamond, pyramid)-free graphs

title: Grid Ramsey Problem (gr(G_{2x2}, K_n))
statement_text: What is the minimum $N$ such that any 2-edge-coloring of the Cartesian product $K_N \square K_N$ contains either a red rectangle or a blue $K_n$?
context_text: The authors introduce this novel Ramsey problem on grid graphs as a key intermediate tool in the proof of the main result on $r(K^{(3)}_4, S^{(3)}_n)$. The minimum is denoted $\mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2}, K_n)$ and Theorem 1.3 establishes $2^{c\log^2 n} \le \mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2}, K_n) \le 2^{c' n^{2/3}\log n}$, but the exact growth rate remains open. The authors remark the problem may be of independent interest.
notes: PDF source — stated verbatim in the abstract and introduction as a novel problem of independent interest; math notation reconstructed from PDF extraction.
paper_title: Hypergraph Ramsey numbers of cliques versus stars

title: Informal Conjecture: r(K^(3)_4, S^(3)_n) and gr(G_{2x2}, K_n) have the same order
statement_text: We strongly believe that $r(K^{(3)}_4, S^{(3)}_n)$ and $\mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2}, K_n)$ are of roughly the same order.
context_text: The paper shows $r(K^{(3)}_4, S^{(3)}_n) \le 2^{\mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2},K_n)}$, and the authors construct lower bounds for $r(K^{(3)}_4, S^{(3)}_n)$ by gluing together lower-bound constructions for $\mathrm{gr}(G_{2\times 2}, K_n)$, yielding comparable sizes. No direct implication between the lower bounds is known.
notes: PDF source — explicit 'we strongly believe' language in the introduction; math notation reconstructed from PDF extraction.
paper_title: Hypergraph Ramsey numbers of cliques versus stars

title: Informal Conjecture: r(K^(3)_{4-e}, S^(3)_n) = Theta(n^2 / log n)
statement_text: The close connection between $r(K^{(3)}_{4-e}, S^{(3)}_n)$ and $r(K_3, K_n)$ suggests that $r(K^{(3)}_{4-e}, S^{(3)}_n) = \Theta\!\left(\frac{n^2}{\log n}\right)$.
context_text: Proposition 1.1 gives bounds $c\,\frac{n^2}{\log^2 n} \le r(K^{(3)}_{4-e}, S^{(3)}_n) \le c'\,\frac{n^2}{\log n}$. The authors note 'it seems likely that a proof of this may be possible through a careful analysis of the $(K^{(3)}_{4-e})$-free process', drawing an analogy with $r(K_3,K_n)=\Theta(n^2/\log n)$, but they do not pursue it.
notes: PDF source — conjectural language 'suggests' and 'it seems likely'; $K^{(3)}_{4-e}$ denotes the complete 3-uniform hypergraph on 4 vertices minus one edge; math notation reconstructed from PDF extraction.
paper_title: Hypergraph Ramsey numbers of cliques versus stars

title: Optimal bound question
statement_text: Is it possible to decrease the bound to $n$ in Theorem 2 (or at least in Theorem 1)?
context_text: The paper proves that intersection graphs of $f$-space-filling systems of subsets of a metric space of Assouad-Nagata dimension $n$ have asymptotic dimension at most $2n+1$. The authors remark that this bound is likely not optimal, noting that interval graphs (intersection graphs of balls in $\mathbb{R}^1$) have asymptotic dimension $1$ rather than $3$. They also observe that $n$-dimensional grids have asymptotic dimension $n$ and can be represented as touching graphs of balls in $\mathbb{R}^n$, so the bound cannot be decreased below $n$.
notes: PDF source — stated as 'it is natural to ask' without a labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Asymptotic dimension of intersection graphs

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: For every forest $H$, there exists $c > 0$ such that $\chi(G) \leq \omega(G)^c$ for every $H$-free graph $G$.
context_text: This polynomial $\chi$-boundedness conjecture for forest-free graphs is described as beautiful and still open; it has been proved only for a smaller collection of forests and has not been settled for any tree containing $P_5$. Showing it for $H = P_5$ would imply the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture for $P_5$.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number. IV. A near-polynomial bound for excluding the five-vertex path

title: Open question on twin-width over infinite fields
statement_text: An intriguing question concerns the existence of such results over infinite fields (starting with $\mathbb{Q}$). We do not have a direct definition of twin-width of matrices over $\mathbb{Q}$ based on contraction sequences. However linear-minor freeness naturally carries to infinite fields, and thus, it is natural to consider that a class of matrices over $\mathbb{Q}$ has bounded twin-width if its closure under linear minors is not the set of all matrices.
context_text: After establishing FPT matrix multiplication algorithms for matrices over finite fields $\mathbb{F}_q$ with bounded twin-width (Theorems 5–7), the authors raise the question of whether analogous results hold for matrices over infinite fields such as $\mathbb{Q}$. The text is truncated at this point, so the precise formulation of the question (likely involving the grid rank of a matrix) is not fully visible.
notes: PDF source — the statement is cut off mid-sentence after the introduction of grid rank; full question not visible in the extracted text.
paper_title: Twin-width V: linear minors, modular counting, and matrix multiplication

title: Conjecture on tightness of the upper bound for all r ≥ 3
statement_text: We suspect that our upper bound $c_r \leq \left(1-\frac{1}{2b_0}\right)\left(1+\frac{1}{2^r b_0 - r}\right)$ is also tight for all $r \geq 3$, where $b_0 > 1$ is the unique root of $b^r - 2rb + r - 1 = 0$.
context_text: Theorem 1 gives a general upper bound for $c_r$ for all $r\geq 2$ and proves it is achieved (i.e., tight) for $r=2$, yielding $c_2=(2+\sqrt{3})/4$. The authors believe the same formula gives the exact value for all larger $r$, but remark that their methods are insufficient to prove this, directing the reader to brief concluding remarks for further discussion.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled environment: 'We suspect that our upper bound is also tight for all r ≥ 3, but our methods do not seem sufficient for proving this.' PDF extraction may have garbled exponent notation (2^r vs 2r) in the formula.
paper_title: The upper logarithmic density of monochromatic subset sums

title: Question 3.1
statement_text: Are there graphs with clique number $3$ and arbitrarily large chromatic number whose all triangle-free induced subgraphs have chromatic number at most $3$?
context_text: After Theorem 1.3 disposes of all cases $r \geq 5$ in Conjecture 1.1, the case $r = 4$ is the only one remaining open. This question, which captures exactly that case, was first asked by James Davies (private communication).
notes: First asked by James Davies (private communication), as acknowledged in the paper.
paper_title: A counterexample to a conjecture about triangle-free induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number

title: Question 3.2
statement_text: Is there a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every digraph $D$ with no induced directed cycle of odd length, we have $\vec{\chi}(D) \leq f(\omega(D))$?
context_text: Motivated by Theorem 1.5, which shows that the digraph analogue of the Scott–Seymour theorem on odd holes fails, the authors ask whether banning all odd-length induced directed cycles forces the dichromatic number to be bounded by a function of the clique number.
notes: The paper states that Questions 3.2 and 3.4 were subsequently answered in the negative by the same authors in arXiv:2203.15575.
paper_title: A counterexample to a conjecture about triangle-free induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number

also_stated_in.title: A counterexample to a conjecture about triangle-free induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number
A counterexample to a conjecture about triangle-free induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number

title: Conjecture 1.10
statement_text: For all $t \geq 0$ there exists $c = c(t)$ such that if $G$ is a graph with no $K_t$, no $K_{t,t}$, no subdivision of $W_{t \times t}$ and no line graph of a subdivision of $W_{t \times t}$ as induced subgraphs, then $\mathrm{tw}(G) \leq c \log(|V(G)|)$.
context_text: The authors propose this as a natural extension of the main results and describe it as a variant of a conjecture of Sintiari and Trotignon [17]. It posits that excluding the four known families responsible for large treewidth (complete graphs, complete bipartite graphs, subdivided walls, line graphs of subdivided walls) as induced subgraphs forces treewidth to be at most logarithmic in the number of vertices.
notes: PDF source — math appears clean.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions III. Three-path-configurations and logarithmic treewidth

title: Open Problem: 5-Coloring complexity for H-free graphs with a P4 component
statement_text: The complexity of the $5$-Coloring Problem restricted to $H$-free graphs remains open when $H$ has exactly one connected component $C$ isomorphic to $P_4$, and $H - V(C)$ is an induced subgraph of $rP_3$ containing at least one edge for some $r \geq 1$.
context_text: After proving that the $k$-Coloring Problem on $2P_4$-free graphs is NP-complete for all $k \geq 5$ (Theorem 7), the authors observe that combined with Theorem 4 this leaves the 5-Coloring complexity unresolved only for the described family of forbidden subgraphs $H$. This is the last remaining gap toward a full dichotomy for the $5$-Coloring Problem on $H$-free graphs.
notes: Stated implicitly as a remaining open case after Theorem 7; no labelled environment.
paper_title: Complexity dichotomy for List-5-Coloring with a forbidden induced subgraph

title: Open Question: NP-hardness of List-k-Coloring on rP3-free graphs
statement_text: Does there exist $k \in \mathbb{N}$ for which the List-$k$-Coloring Problem restricted to $rP_3$-free graphs is NP-hard?
context_text: The authors note that while Theorems 13 and 17 (the main results of Sections 2 and 3) are proved for the List-$k$-Coloring Problem on $rP_3$-free graphs for arbitrary $k$, the tools of Section 4 fail to extend beyond $k = 5$. The authors were unable to settle whether the general List-$k$-Coloring Problem on $rP_3$-free graphs can be NP-hard for some $k$.
notes: Stated in running prose at the end of Section 1; no labelled environment.
paper_title: Complexity dichotomy for List-5-Coloring with a forbidden induced subgraph

title: Conjecture 11
statement_text: If $G$ is a universally cell 3-colorable graph which does not have a subcontraction isomorphic to $K^+_{3,n-3}$ for some $n \geq 7$, then $G$ is 3-flowable.
context_text: The paper exhibits an infinite family of universally cell 3-colorable graphs ($K^+_{3,n-3}$ for $n \geq 7$) that do not admit a nowhere-zero 3-flow, showing the two properties are not equivalent in general. The authors believe excluding these graphs as subcontractions is sufficient to recover equivalence between universal cell 3-colorability and 3-flowability. Remarkably, a positive resolution of this conjecture would also imply Tutte's 3-Flow Conjecture.
paper_title: Coloring Drawings of Graphs

title: Problem 3
statement_text: Does $U_t(n) = tn - \binom{t+1}{2}$?
context_text: This generalises Problem 1 to the setting of shuffling $t$ distinguishable counters across $n$ positions using the fewest lazy transpositions. The upper bound $U_t(n)\leq tn-\binom{t+1}{2}$ is achieved by existing sweeping/divide-and-conquer constructions. When $t=n$ the problem reduces to Problem 1, which Theorem 2 answers negatively; the paper asks whether the trivial bound is tight for other values of $t$.
notes: PDF source — binomial-coefficient notation garbled in raw extraction; LaTeX reconstructed from context. The paper also proves (Theorem 4) that for $3\leq t\leq n$ the bound can likewise be beaten by a constant factor, so the answer is negative for all $t\geq 3$.
paper_title: Perfect shuffling with fewer lazy transpositions

title: Conjecture 3
statement_text: For sufficiently large $k$, if $k$ is even, then $m(P_k) = k!/2$ and if $k$ is odd, then $m(P_k) = \frac{k-1}{4}(k-1)!$.
context_text: The authors identify specific extremal colorings $\chi(a_0, b_0)$ of $K_{r(P_k)}$ that achieve exact counts of monochromatic $P_k$, demonstrating tightness of Theorem 2 up to the constant $c$. These colorings are believed to realize the threshold Ramsey multiplicity for all sufficiently large $k$.
notes: PDF source — fraction $\frac{k-1}{4}$ reconstructed from line-break artifact in extraction.
paper_title: Threshold Ramsey multiplicity for paths and even cycles

also_stated_in.title: Threshold Ramsey multiplicity for paths and even cycles

title: Union cover variant (open problem)
statement_text: For every graph $H$, and all $\varepsilon > 0$, there is an integer $N$ such that for every $H$-free graph $G$, $V(G)$ is the union of at most $N$ $\varepsilon$-restricted subsets (not necessarily pairwise disjoint).
context_text: The authors note this as a statement midway between Theorem 1.3 (partition into weakly $\varepsilon$-restricted sets) and their main Theorem 1.4 (partition into $\varepsilon$-restricted sets). They remark that 'this variation does not seem to be easy, although it does not imply 1.4 as far as we know.'
notes: Stated as a passing remark without a labelled environment; the authors signal it is open and non-trivial but do not explicitly phrase it as a conjecture or question.
paper_title: Strengthening Rodl's theorem

title: Informal Conjecture on Property (*) for d = o(n/log n)
statement_text: The irregular random subgraph $H = H(G)$ satisfies Property $(*)$ — that is, $m(H,k) = (1+o(1))n/(d+1)$ for all $0 \leq k \leq d$ — with high probability for all $d = o(n/\log n)$.
context_text: After proving Theorem 1.3 for $d = o(n/(\log n)^{12})$, the authors conjecture the range extends to $d = o(n/\log n)$. They also remark that for $d = \omega(n/\log n)$ the random subgraph model likely fails to satisfy Property $(*)$, and that new ideas would be required to establish Conjecture 1.2 in the full range.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled theorem environment: 'We conjecture that with high probability for all d = o(n/log n), the irregular random subgraph H satisfies Property *'.
paper_title: On random irregular subgraphs

title: Informal conjecture on the actual diameter
statement_text: The bound (1.4) for the almost diameter is sharp and one may speculate whether the actual diameter is around the same length, i.e.\ $(2+o(1))\log 3V_\Gamma$.
context_text: Having proved in Theorem 3 that for almost every $z\in\mathbb{H}$ one has $\min_{\gamma\in\Gamma}d(\gamma z,w)\leq(2+o(1))\log 3V_\Gamma$, the authors remark that this almost-diameter bound is sharp and speculate that the true diameter of the hyperbolic surface $\Gamma\backslash\mathbb{H}$ is of the same order. They note that this would imply the co-compact arithmetic lattice $\Gamma$ is generated by its elements of Frobenius norm $O_\varepsilon(V_\Gamma^{1+\varepsilon})$, bringing it into line with Theorem 1.
notes: PDF source — stated with hedged language ('one may speculate') but refers to a precise mathematical quantity and has a non-trivial stated implication.
paper_title: Small diameters and generators for arithmetic lattices in $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$ and certain Ramanujan graphs

title: Informal Conjecture (uniform twin-width strictly stronger than twin-width)
statement_text: Uniform twin-width is strictly stronger than twin-width, i.e. there are groups with finite twin-width and infinite uniform twin-width.
context_text: Uniform twin-width requires a uniform bound on the permutation width of the action of every group element, whereas finite twin-width only requires each action to have finite width individually. Having finite uniform twin-width implies finite twin-width, but the converse is not established. The group of finitely supported permutations on $\mathbb{Z}$ is proposed as a candidate counterexample.
notes: Stated in prose in Section 1.2 with 'We conjecture that'.
paper_title: Twin-width VII: groups

title: Question (explicit group with infinite twin-width)
statement_text: Can we find a more explicit construction of a group with infinite twin-width, or one with a direct proof that twin-width is infinite?
context_text: The group constructed in Theorem 1.1 relies on probabilistic methods and starts from a sequence of bounded degree graphs with unbounded twin-width; it does not yield an explicit bounded degree graph with unbounded twin-width. A more explicit or combinatorially direct construction would significantly advance understanding of twin-width for bounded degree graphs.
notes: Stated as a question in prose at the end of Section 1.3.
paper_title: Twin-width VII: groups

title: Open Problem (separating twin-width and queue number on sparse graphs)
statement_text: Separating twin-width and queue number on sparse graphs is an open problem.
context_text: Finite queue number implies finite twin-width, so the two parameters are not equivalent in general. However, no example of a group (or sparse graph) with finite twin-width and infinite queue number is known, and the same gap exists for the corresponding uniform variants. Many results of the paper hold simultaneously for both parameters, making a separation elusive.
notes: Explicitly declared an open problem in Section 1.4.
paper_title: Twin-width VII: groups

title: Conjecture 3
statement_text: The problem of counting perfect matchings in input graphs of independence number $2$ is $\#P$-complete.
context_text: The authors note that for all previously known graph classes on which EM is tractable (planar graphs, $K_{3,3}$-minor-free graphs, complete and complete bipartite graphs), counting perfect matchings is also polynomial. Their new results for graphs of independence number 2 break this alignment, since no polynomial counting scheme is known for this class and the authors believe counting is hard.
notes: The paper text is truncated before Section 6 (open problems), so additional open problems stated there could not be extracted.
paper_title: Exact Matching in Graphs of Bounded Independence Number

title: Informal Conjecture on R(n;r,s) vs R'(n;r,s) near Turán density
statement_text: We clearly have $R(n; r, s) \geq R'(n; r, s)$ and we believe that this estimate should be close to an equality when $s/r$ is close to the Turán density $1 - \frac{1}{n-1}$.
context_text: The authors introduce the variant $R'(n;r,s)$, defined as the minimum $N$ such that every $(r,s)$-coloring of $K_N$ yields a color class with chromatic number at least $n$. They observe that $R(n;r,s) \geq R'(n;r,s)$ always holds, and that for the special case $s = r-1$ near-equality is confirmed by prior work of Alon et al. [1]. The informal belief is stated in Section 4 while studying the fixed-$n$ regime.
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled. Statement reconstructed from prose; no labelled conjecture environment. The problems/further-remarks section mentioned at the end of the introduction is not present in the extracted text, so additional posed problems from that section may be missing.
paper_title: Set-coloring Ramsey numbers via codes

title: Question 1
statement_text: Assuming only that each $a_i \neq 0$, what geometric constraints can be imposed on a set $S \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ which imply non-trivial uniform upper bounds on $\Pr(X \in S)$?
context_text: The paper introduces a new generalisation of the Littlewood–Offord problem to $\mathbb{R}^d$ in which no assumption is made on the sizes of the nonzero vectors $a_1,\ldots,a_n$, only that they are nonzero. The authors observe that this makes the question much more about the geometry of $S$ than about spatial anti-concentration of $X = a_1\xi_1 + \cdots + a_n\xi_n$, giving it a very different flavour from previous Littlewood–Offord variants.
paper_title: Geometric and o-minimal Littlewood-Offord problems

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: In Theorem 1.1 we have $\Pr(X \in S) \leq C_S/\sqrt{n}$, for some $C_S$ depending only on $S$.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes $\Pr(X \in S) \leq n^{-1/2+\alpha}$ for any $\alpha > 0$ when $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ is definable with respect to an o-minimal structure and contains no line segment; the conjecture posits that the optimal $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ bound from the classical Erd\H{o}s–Littlewood–Offord theorem also holds in this geometric setting. The authors prove it when $S$ satisfies the generic intersection property, which covers convex or irreducible algebraic plane curves in $\mathbb{R}^2$, irreducible complex algebraic curves, and the boundary of a 3-dimensional ball. They also remark that a full resolution of the Gotsman–Linial conjecture would imply Conjecture 1.2 for all semi-algebraic sets not containing a line segment.
paper_title: Geometric and o-minimal Littlewood-Offord problems

title: Open Complexity Problem (2-F-PFC for oriented trees of maximum degree ≥ 3)
statement_text: The complexity of the $2$-$F$-PFC where the underlying undirected tree of $F$ has maximum degree at least $3$ remains partially open. In particular, the complexity for those graphs $F$ where successively deleting all leaves yields a star $K_{1,n}$ is not determined.
context_text: The paper proves NP-hardness of 2-$\vec{T}$-PFC when the oriented tree $\vec{T}$ reduces to an oriented path of length 2 or 3 after repeatedly removing all leaves. The analogous question for oriented trees whose leaf-pruned form is a star $K_{1,n}$ is explicitly left open in the introduction.
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction without a formal labelled environment. PDF source — math notation may be garbled.
paper_title: Colorings of oriented planar graphs avoiding a monochromatic subgraph

title: Informal conjecture on improving the $g(t)$ lower bound
statement_text: The factor $1/4$ in $g(t) \geq t(t+2)/8$ can likely be significantly improved. However it appears to be quite challenging, for example, to show that every graph with chromatic number at least $t$ contains a minor on $t$ vertices with at most one tenth of all possible edges missing.
context_text: The authors define $g(t)$ as the maximum number of edges guaranteed in a $t$-vertex minor of any graph with chromatic number at least $t$. Mader's theorem yields $g(t) \geq t(t+2)/8$; the authors believe this is far from the true value, posing a density of $9/10$ as an example of a seemingly hard intermediate target for general graphs.
notes: Stated as a remark in the introduction without a labelled environment; language 'can likely' and 'appears to be quite challenging' signals authorial belief rather than a formal conjecture.
paper_title: Dense minors of graphs with independence number two

title: Open Challenge: Efficient Approximation of Twin-Width for Unordered Graphs
statement_text: Efficiently approximating twin-width (that is, returning an $f(d)$-sequence when the twin-width of the input is at most $d$) remains an open challenge for unordered graphs.
context_text: The authors note that efficient approximation of twin-width has been achieved for classes of totally ordered binary structures and for all mentioned classes of bounded twin-width (bounded tree-width, proper minor-closed, hereditary subclasses of permutation graphs, etc.), but the problem is unresolved for general unordered graphs. This open challenge is stated in the introduction as a key missing piece in the twin-width landscape.
notes: Stated in running prose in the introduction without a labelled theorem environment. The PDF extraction is incomplete — the paper content cuts off mid-sentence in Section 2.1 (before the bulk of the technical sections), so additional labelled conjectures/problems/questions in later sections of the paper are not captured here.
paper_title: Twin-width VI: the lens of contraction sequences

title: Belief on 4-vertex-critical arrangement graphs
statement_text: We believe that the corona construction results in infinitely many 4-vertex-critical arrangement graphs.
context_text: The paper's corona construction maps $\triangle$-saturated arrangements with a pentagonal face to arrangements with 4-chromatic 4-regular arrangement graphs, yielding an infinite family. The authors believe these are all vertex-critical, though this is not proved.
notes: Statement appears as running prose in the outline of Section 3; the exact family is defined later in that section, which is only partially present in the extracted text.
paper_title: Coloring circle arrangements: New $4$-chromatic planar graphs

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: Every non-null 6-regular graph has a $K_6$ minor.
context_text: The authors have found $K_6$-minor-free graphs with minimum degree six and maximum degree at most nine, and $K_6$-minor-free graphs with minimum degree five, maximum degree seven, and average degree arbitrarily close to $98/15$. Conjecture 1.3 asks whether 6-regularity alone forces a $K_6$ minor.
paper_title: Bipartite graphs with no $K_6$ minor

title: Informal Conjecture: min degree $\geq 6$, max degree $\leq 8$ forces $K_6$ minor
statement_text: Every non-null graph with minimum degree at least six and maximum degree at most eight has a $K_6$ minor.
context_text: Stated parenthetically immediately after Conjecture 1.3: ``Indeed, as far as we know, every non-null graph with minimum degree at least six and maximum degree at most eight has a $K_6$ minor.'' It is a stronger open belief than Conjecture 1.3.
notes: Parenthetical aside following Conjecture 1.3; phrased as 'as far as we know'.
paper_title: Bipartite graphs with no $K_6$ minor

title: Open Question: minimum degree five in Theorem 1.6
statement_text: We do not know whether ``six'' can be replaced by ``five'' in 1.6 (every non-null bipartite graph with minimum degree at least six has a $K_6$ minor).
context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.6, the authors note that the analogous statement for minimum degree five in bipartite graphs is open. They also remark that minimum degree is more difficult than average degree to handle inductively.
notes: Stated in running prose without a labelled environment.
paper_title: Bipartite graphs with no $K_6$ minor

title: Informal Conjecture: average degree $\geq 5$ and max degree $\leq 6$ forces $K_5$ minor in bipartite graphs
statement_text: Perhaps average degree at least five and maximum degree at most six will guarantee a $K_5$ minor in a bipartite graph.
context_text: After observing that there exist $n$-vertex bipartite graphs with average degree at least four and maximum degree at most five that have no $K_5$ minor, the authors suggest this stronger degree bound might guarantee a $K_5$ minor, but explicitly state they have not verified it.
notes: Phrased as 'perhaps ... but we have not worked this out'; quite tentative.
paper_title: Bipartite graphs with no $K_6$ minor

title: Conjecture on r-free digraphs with r > 2n/3
statement_text: All $r$-free digraphs on $n$ vertices with $r > 2n/3$ are at most one edge away from being acyclic.
context_text: The authors first construct a family of $r$-free digraphs $G$ on $n$ vertices with $\beta(G) \geq n^2/(r+1)^2$, implying that if every $r$-free digraph satisfies $\beta(G) = O(1)$ then $r = \Omega(n)$. In the converse direction they pose this conjecture, noting tightness: for $n$ a multiple of $3$ there exists an $(r-1)$-free digraph $G$ on $n$ vertices with $r = 2n/3$ and $\beta(G) = 2$.
notes: Stated in running prose with explicit 'we conjecture' language; no labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Extremal results on feedback arc sets in digraphs

title: Suspected directed surplus of a random B-free orientation (Remark 1.6)
statement_text: A random orientation of a $\mathcal{B}$-free undirected graph on $n$ vertices with $m = \mathrm{ex}(n, \mathcal{B})$ edges likely satisfies $\beta(G) = m/2 - \Omega(\sqrt{mn})$.
context_text: In Remark 1.6 the authors note that the proof of Theorem 1.5 uses the auxiliary condition $\mathrm{ex}(n,\mathcal{B}) = \Theta(n^{2-\varepsilon(\mathcal{B})})$ only for convenience, and that without it a random $\mathcal{B}$-free orientation is suspected to already achieve the stated directed-surplus bound.
notes: Stated with 'we suspect' in Remark 1.6; no labelled theorem environment. PDF math is readable.
paper_title: Extremal results on feedback arc sets in digraphs

title: Open case of Theorem 3 for odd $n$
statement_text: Determine $M_{F_{2c}}(n)$ for odd $n \geq 5$. The upper bound $M_{F_{2c}}(n) \leq 2^{n-2}$ holds for all $n$, but the best construction for odd $n$ yields only $2^{n-2} - \binom{n-2}{(n-3)/2}$ graphs.
context_text: Theorem 3 establishes $M_{F_{2c}}(n) = 2^{n-2}$ for all even $n$ via matching upper and lower bounds. For odd $n$ the upper bound still holds, but the construction from the even case fails; the best the authors could achieve gives $2^{n-2} - \binom{n-2}{(n-3)/2}$, leaving a gap. For $n = 3$ the upper bound is attained (a triangle and the empty graph), but no general matching construction is known.
notes: Stated as Remark 2 without a formal Problem/Conjecture environment; the authors do not explicitly conjecture the true value for odd $n$ but clearly identify it as an open case.
paper_title: Structured Codes of Graphs

title: Open Question (unit segment graphs delineation)
statement_text: Are unit segment graphs delineated (by twin-width)?
context_text: The paper establishes that axis-parallel $H_t$-free unit segment graphs have bounded twin-width, while axis-parallel $H_4$-free two-lengthed segment graphs have unbounded twin-width, and that (triangle-free) pure axis-parallel unit segment graphs have unbounded twin-width. This places unit segment graphs at the frontier between delineated and non-delineated intersection graph classes, and the authors explicitly leave their delineation status as an open question.
notes: Stated verbatim in the abstract: 'We leave as an open question whether unit segment graphs are delineated.'
paper_title: Twin-width VIII: delineation and win-wins

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: Planar graphs have $r$-th weak coloring numbers in $O(r^2 \log r)$.
context_text: The best known lower bound for the maximum $r$-th weak coloring number of planar graphs is $\Omega(r^2 \log r)$ (proved in this paper via Corollary 3) and the best upper bound is $O(r^3)$ due to Van den Heuvel, Ossona de Mendez, Quiroz, Rabinovich, and Siebertz. The authors find it particularly intriguing to close this gap and believe the lower bound gives the correct order of magnitude.
notes: PDF source; the statement notation is sufficiently clear despite possible garbling. Section 4 (open problems) is absent from the extracted text, so additional open problems beyond Conjecture 4 may exist in the full paper.
paper_title: Improved bounds for weak coloring numbers

title: Conjecture 7
statement_text: Let $T$ be a tree of growth $k$ and of order $n$. If $R$ is a finite set of integers such that $\{0, 1, \ldots, k\} \subseteq R$ and $\sum_{r \in R}(2r+1) \geq n$, then $T$ is $R$-burnable.
context_text: The authors propose this as a stronger version of the Burning Number Conjecture via a reformulation using $R$-burnability: the BNC states that every connected graph on $n$ vertices is $\{0, 1, \ldots, \lceil\sqrt{n}\rceil - 1\}$-burnable, since $\sum_{r=0}^{\lceil\sqrt{n}\rceil - 1}(2r+1) = \lceil\sqrt{n}\rceil^2 \geq n$. Conjecture 7 generalises this to trees of bounded growth by requiring the set $R$ to contain all radii up to $k$ and have sufficient total volume.
paper_title: Improved pyrotechnics : Closer to the burning graph conjecture

title: Question (which hereditary classes are poly-χ-bounded?)
statement_text: Which hereditary classes are poly-$\chi$-bounded?
context_text: Esperet's conjecture that every $\chi$-bounded hereditary class is poly-$\chi$-bounded was recently disproved by Briański, Davies and Walczak. This motivates the overarching question of characterising which hereditary classes do have a polynomial binding function.
notes: Posed as a guiding open question in the introduction after noting Esperet's conjecture was disproved; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Open problem (poly-χ-bounded for k-multihole with only odd cycles)
statement_text: For any integer $k \geq 1$, is the class of graphs with no $k$-multihole in which all the cycles have odd length polynomially $\chi$-bounded?
context_text: This class is $\chi$-bounded [9], but it contains the class of $\{P_5, C_5\}$-free graphs. The authors state they cannot yet prove it is poly-$\chi$-bounded, referring to [15] for the best current bounds.
notes: Stated as an open limitation in the introduction; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

also_stated_in.title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Informal conjecture (polynomial Gyárfás–Sumner)
statement_text: It might be true that every forest $H$ is good, i.e., the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded (strengthening the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture from $\chi$-boundedness to polynomial $\chi$-boundedness).
context_text: Say a forest $H$ is good if the class of $H$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded. This has been proved only for a few simple kinds of tree and some of their disjoint unions; the polynomial strengthening of Gyárfás–Sumner is fully open.
notes: Introduced with 'It might be true that'; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Open problem (disjoint union of good trees)
statement_text: It is not known that if trees $H_1$, $H_2$ are good (i.e., the class of $H_i$-free graphs is polynomially $\chi$-bounded for $i=1,2$), then the disjoint union of $H_1$ and $H_2$ is also good.
context_text: Trees of diameter three are good [14], but whether disjoint unions of diameter-three trees are good is unknown. Self-isolation provides a sufficient condition: if $H_1$ and $H_2$ are good forests and one of them is self-isolating, their disjoint union is good.
notes: Stated as 'It is not known that …'; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Question (are all graphs self-isolating?)
statement_text: Could it be that all graphs are self-isolating?
context_text: The authors note that they know very few graphs that have the self-isolating property and none that are known not to have it. The only connected graphs known to be self-isolating are complete graphs, paths of arbitrary length, and complete bipartite graphs (proved in the paper and its predecessors).
notes: Posed explicitly as a question in Section 2; no formal label.
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes

title: Open problem on construction size
statement_text: It would be interesting to know if the size of the $(k+1)$-dichromatic example in $\mathcal{C}_3$ can be reduced below the current bound of $n^{2^{\mathrm{poly}(n)}}$, which is larger than the $2^{\mathrm{poly}(|G_k|)}$ bound achieved by Zykov's construction for triangle-free graphs.
context_text: The authors observe in the Further Works section that their Zykov-style digraph construction yields graphs whose size grows much faster than the analogous triangle-free undirected graphs, and they flag reducing this size as an open problem.
notes: PDF source — the size expression appears as 'n2poly(n)' in the extraction; the exact exponent tower has been reconstructed as $n^{2^{\mathrm{poly}(n)}}$ based on the Lemma 3 bound $n^{4r}$ and the recursive structure, but may differ from the original LaTeX.
paper_title: Chordal directed graphs are not $χ$-bounded

title: Conjecture on $R_\ell(\mathcal{H}_s, k)$
statement_text: For $s \geq 2$, if $\mathcal{H}_s$ is the family of graphs with chromatic number greater than $s$, then $R_\ell(\mathcal{H}_s, k) = s^k + 1$.
context_text: Theorem 8 of the paper establishes $\frac{1}{e} \cdot s^k \leq R_\ell(\mathcal{H}_s, k) \leq s^k + 1$, where the upper bound follows from $R_\ell(\mathcal{H}_s, k) \leq R(\mathcal{H}_s, k) = s^k + 1$. The authors conjecture that the upper bound is tight.
paper_title: Multicolor list Ramsey numbers grow exponentially

title: Determine the exponential constant in $R_\ell(H,k)$
statement_text: It would be interesting to determine $\lim_{k\to\infty} R_\ell(H,k)^{1/k}$, even in just the case $H = K_3$.
context_text: Theorems 1 and 2 together give $\frac{1}{e}\cdot 2^k \leq R_\ell(K_3,k) \leq (4+o(1))^k$, but the exact exponential constant is unknown. The authors note that their results do not even establish that the limit $\lim_{k\to\infty} R_\ell(H,k)^{1/k}$ exists.
notes: PDF source — the precise numerical bounds cited for $K_3$ may be garbled in extraction.
paper_title: Multicolor list Ramsey numbers grow exponentially

title: Question 1
statement_text: Is every graph without a $K_{4,4}$-minor $7$-choosable? Is every graph without a $K_{3,5}$-minor $7$-choosable?
context_text: After disproving Woodall's Conjecture for large comparable $s$ and $t$, the authors identify $K_{4,4}$ and $K_{3,5}$ as the smallest parameter pairs for which the conjecture is not yet settled by prior results, since Jørgensen's result gives only $8$-choosability for $K_{4,4}$-minor-free graphs and no sharp result is known for $K_{3,5}$.
notes: PDF source — math notation is clear.
paper_title: Disproof of a Conjecture by Woodall

title: Question 2
statement_text: Is it true that for all integers $1 \leq s \leq t$, every graph $G$ with $G \not\succeq K_{s,t}$ satisfies $\chi_{\ell}(G) \leq 2s + t$?
context_text: Theorem 1 shows that the bound $s+t-1$ in Woodall's conjecture is false for large comparable $s$ and $t$, and that the list chromatic number can exceed $(1-\varepsilon)(2s+t)$; the authors ask whether the matching upper bound $2s+t$ holds universally as the correct asymptotic threshold.
notes: PDF source — minor-containment symbol interpreted as $\succeq$.
paper_title: Disproof of a Conjecture by Woodall

title: Open problem: Woodall's conjecture for fixed s
statement_text: It remains an interesting open problem whether Conjecture 3 (that every graph without a $K_{s,t}$-minor is $(s+t-1)$-choosable) remains true if $s$ is fixed and $t$ is sufficiently large in terms of $s$.
context_text: The disproof via Theorem 1 requires $s$ and $t$ to be of comparable size ($s \leq t \leq Cs$); the regime of fixed $s$ with $t \to \infty$ is not covered and, unlike the chromatic number case (settled by Kostochka), no analogous result is known for the list chromatic number.
paper_title: Disproof of a Conjecture by Woodall

title: Open problem: smallest counterexample to Woodall's conjecture
statement_text: It would also be interesting to determine the smallest values of $s$ and $t$ (or of $s + t$) for which Conjecture 3 (that every graph without a $K_{s,t}$-minor is $(s+t-1)$-choosable) fails.
context_text: The probabilistic construction in the proof of Theorem 1 yields counterexamples only for astronomically large $s$ and $t$; the smallest explicit failing parameter pair is left as an open problem, motivating the study of small cases such as Question 1.
paper_title: Disproof of a Conjecture by Woodall

title: Open problem on parameters governing $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H)$
statement_text: What natural parameters (analogous to degeneracy and vertex count for undirected graphs) determine the growth order of $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H)$ for acyclic digraphs? In particular, is $\overrightarrow{r}_1(H)$ polynomial or super-polynomial in $n$ when $H$ has bounded degree?
context_text: For undirected graphs, the Ramsey number is governed by degeneracy and vertex count (Conlon–Fox–Sudakov conjecture, verified up to a $\log^2 d$ factor). The authors introduce the notion of 'multiscale complexity' as a candidate parameter for the directed setting, but stress that the full picture remains unresolved and deserves further research.
notes: Stated as prose in the introduction (no formal theorem environment); also reiterated in the conclusion.
paper_title: Ramsey numbers of sparse digraphs

title: Informal Conjecture on similar growth of $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ and $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$
statement_text: We suspect that $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ and $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$ have similar growth.
context_text: The authors note that the best known lower bound on $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ has the form $(1/\varepsilon)^{c\log(1/\varepsilon)}$ arising from the Behrend construction, while $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$ satisfies $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon) \leq \text{tower}(O(\log(1/\varepsilon)))$ (Fox [8]). Theorem 1.12 provides supporting evidence: if $N_{DFL}(\varepsilon)$ grows subexponentially in $\varepsilon^{-1}$, then so does $1/\delta_{TRL}(\varepsilon)$. If the suspected similarity holds, Theorem 1.10 and inequality (1.1) would together give comparable lower and upper bounds on $M_{TFL}(\varepsilon)$.
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled; stated as 'We suspect' in running prose without a labelled environment. The PDF extraction appears truncated after the start of Section 2, so further conjectures or open problems in later sections may be missing.
paper_title: Removal lemmas and approximate homomorphisms

title: Question (Section 3, implicit representation for speed $2^{O(n\log n)}$)
statement_text: Is it possible that hereditary families with speed $f(n) \leq 2^{O(n \log n)}$ always admit an implicit representation of size $O(n^{1/2} \log n)$?
context_text: Hatami and Hatami [13] showed that for every $\delta > 0$ there are hereditary families with speed $f(n) \leq 2^{O(n \log n)}$ for which any implicit representation requires labels of size at least $\Omega(n^{1/2-\delta})$, and they raise the question of whether the exponent $1/2$ can be improved. Alon here asks the complementary upper-bound question: whether $O(n^{1/2} \log n)$ always suffices in this speed range.
notes: Posed as an explicit question in Section 3 but without a labelled theorem environment; PDF source.
paper_title: Implicit representation of sparse hereditary families

title: Question (Section 3, implicit representation for speed $2^{n^{1+\varepsilon}}$)
statement_text: If the speed of a hereditary family satisfies $f(n) < 2^{n^{1+\varepsilon}}$ for a sufficiently small fixed $\varepsilon > 0$, is there always an implicit representation of size at most $O(n^{2/3} \log n)$?
context_text: This is posed as a follow-up to the $O(n^{1/2}\log n)$ question for the slower-speed regime. Theorem 1.1 guarantees only $O(n^{1-1/d}\log n)$ under the weaker bound $f(n) \leq 2^{(1/4-\varepsilon)n^2}$, and the question asks whether the intermediate speed range $f(n) < 2^{n^{1+\varepsilon}}$ forces a sub-polynomial improvement to $n^{2/3}$.
notes: Posed as an explicit question in Section 3 but without a labelled theorem environment; PDF source.
paper_title: Implicit representation of sparse hereditary families

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: There exists $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that if no induced subgraph of $G$ is a $k$-creature, then $G$ has at most $|V(G)|^{f(k)}$ minimal separators.
context_text: A $k$-creature is a graph whose vertex set partitions into sets $A, B, \{x_1,\ldots,x_k\}, \{y_1,\ldots,y_k\}$ satisfying connectivity, anticomplete, and matching conditions; every $k$-creature contains a theta, pyramid, prism, or turtle. The authors propose this as a stronger generalisation of Theorem 1.2, noting that even if true it does not fully characterise classes with the polynomial separator property (the class $\mathcal{D}$ of induced subgraphs of $k$-turtles with very long paths has the polynomial separator property yet contains $k$-creatures of arbitrarily large $k$).
notes: The paper proves a weaker version (Theorem 1.5) replacing $k$-creatures by the stricter notion of immature $k$-creatures.
paper_title: Graphs with polynomially many minimal separators

title: Question 9
statement_text: Is the upper bound in Theorem 2 tight up to lower-order terms?
context_text: Theorem 2 shows that for a convex $n$-point set $P$ and every $k \in \mathbb{N}$, $K(P)$ admits a partition into at most $\frac{n}{\sqrt{2k}}$ $k$-planar subgraphs (for appropriate $s$), while at least $\frac{n-1}{4.93\sqrt{k}}$ subgraphs are required. Question 9 asks whether these bounds match up to lower-order terms. The authors note that for $k=2$ they can show a lower bound of $\frac{3n}{10}$ almost matching the upper bound $\frac{n}{3}$, but omit this from the current version.
paper_title: Edge Partitions of Complete Geometric Graphs (Part 2)

title: Informal Question (Section 2)
statement_text: Does there exist a map $\phi$ from Dictator to XOR which is $C$-Lipschitz, where each output bit depends on at most $k$ input bits, and such that $\phi^{-1}$ is $o(\log n / \log k)$-Lipschitz as $n, k \to \infty$?
context_text: Theorem 1 shows that for general maps the inverse must be at least $\Omega(1/k)\cdot\log n$-Lipschitz, while for linear maps the bound is $\Omega(1/\log k)\cdot\log n$-Lipschitz. Rao and Shinkar's construction achieves $\delta(2,k)=O(1/\log k)$ for linear maps, matching the linear-map lower bound, but a gap remains for general maps between $\Omega(1/k)$ and $O(1/\log k)$.
notes: Stated in running prose as 'This raises the following question:' without a labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Lipschitz bijections between boolean functions

title: Polynomial Kempe sequences for degenerate graphs
statement_text: Can any two $k$-colorings of a $(k-1)$-degenerate graph on $n$ vertices always be connected by a sequence of Kempe changes whose length is polynomial in $n$?
context_text: Las Vergnas and Meyniel proved in 1981 that all $k$-colorings of a $d$-degenerate graph are Kempe equivalent when $k > d+1$, but their proof yields a sequence that may be exponential in the number of vertices. Whether a polynomial bound can be achieved is posed as an intriguing open question and is the central motivation for all three main results of the paper.
notes: PDF source — stated as an open question in the abstract and introduction without a labeled environment; no explicit attribution to prior authors.
paper_title: Kempe changes in degenerate graphs

title: Problem 4.4
statement_text: What is the complexity of $\Sigma$-$k$-\textsc{Dicolourability} for $k \in \{4, 5\}$ and $\Sigma$ different from the sphere?
context_text: The paper establishes that Σ-2-DICOLOURABILITY is NP-complete for any surface, and that Σ-k-DICOLOURABILITY is polynomial-time solvable for any surface Σ and any integer k ≥ 6. The complexity for k ∈ {4, 5} on surfaces other than the sphere is explicitly left open.
notes: Problem 4.4 is referenced in the introduction but the full text of Section 4 was not present in the PDF extraction; the statement is inferred from the surrounding context.
paper_title: On the dichromatic number of surfaces

title: Question 6
statement_text: For a non-negative integer $r$, let $\mathcal{G}_r$ be the class of plane graphs with no separating triangles such that each vertex is at distance at most $r$ from a face of length at least four. Does $\mathcal{G}_r$ have weak diameter chromatic number at most two?
context_text: The authors suspect that a substantial relaxation of the triangle-free assumption is sufficient to allow coloring by two colors. They note that the HEX-lemma-based example with triangulated grids shows that non-triangular faces are essential, and remark that the question has a positive answer in the special case where the graph has exactly one non-triangular face (since proximity to a fixed face forces bounded treewidth and hence asymptotic dimension one).
paper_title: Weak diameter coloring of graphs on surfaces

title: Conjecture (Section 7)
statement_text: The lower bound for the discrepancy of arithmetic progressions in grids given in Theorem 1.3 is tight up to the constant factor, i.e., for any positive integer $d$ and $N = (N_1, \ldots, N_d)$ with $N_1 \cdots N_d \geq 3$, $$\mathrm{disc}(\mathcal{A}_N) = \Theta_d\!\left(\max_{I \subseteq [d]} \left(\prod_{i \in I} N_i\right)^{\frac{1}{2|I|+2}}\right).$$
context_text: Theorem 1.3 establishes matching lower and upper bounds for the discrepancy of arithmetic progressions in general grids of differing side lengths, but the upper bound carries an extra sub-logarithmic factor $\frac{\log(N_1 \cdots N_d)}{\log\log(N_1 \cdots N_d)}$ relative to the lower bound. The authors conjecture in their concluding remarks (Section 7) that this gap is an artifact of the proof and the lower bound is in fact tight up to a constant depending only on $d$.
notes: Section 7 is not reproduced in the PDF extraction; the conjecture is described by reference in the paper's organization paragraph. The statement above is reconstructed from Theorem 1.3 and the prose description 'a conjecture that the lower bound for the discrepancy for grids in Theorem 1.3 is tight up to the constant factor'.
paper_title: Discrepancy of arithmetic progressions in grids

title: Conjecture 5
statement_text: For an $n$-set with a random partition with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k$, the clique algorithm has minimal average complexity among all AC algorithms.
context_text: In the model where each of $n$ items independently belongs to class $C_i$ with probability $p_i$ (with $p_1 \geq p_2 \geq \cdots \geq p_k$), the authors argue that an optimal algorithm should compare each new element first to the largest identified class. The clique algorithm does exactly this, comparing each new item to blocks in decreasing order of size. The conjecture is stated directly before Theorem 6, which characterises the asymptotic expected query count of the clique algorithm as $\sum_{i=1}^k i p_i n$, offered as supporting evidence.
paper_title: Active clustering for labeling training data

title: Problem 1
statement_text: Does there exist a polynomial-time algorithm which, given a near-triangulation $G$ with the outer face bounded by a 4-cycle $C$ and a 4-coloring $\psi$ of $C$, correctly decides whether $\psi$ extends to a 4-coloring of $G$?
context_text: The authors note that even very basic precoloring extension questions for planar graphs are wide open. There exist infinitely many near-triangulations $G$ whose outer 4-cycle has a precoloring that does not extend to a 4-coloring of $G$, yet no good characterization of such graphs is known.
notes: PDF source — math notation may be garbled
paper_title: Coloring count cones of planar graphs

title: Conjecture 8
statement_text: Every plane near-cubic graph $\tilde{G}$ with $d(\tilde{G}) = 5$ satisfies $n_{\tilde{G}} \in B'_5$.
context_text: The cone $B'_5$ is defined as the subcone of $B_5$ spanned by the rays $\operatorname{ray}(\tilde{R}_{5,1}), \ldots, \operatorname{ray}(\tilde{R}_{5,11})$, excluding the ray corresponding to the non-planar graph $\tilde{R}_{5,12}$. By Lemma 7, membership of $n_{\tilde{G}}$ in the full cone $B_5$ is equivalent to the Four Color Theorem, so this conjecture is a strict strengthening of the Four Color Theorem. The authors verify it for all plane near-cubic graphs with fewer than 30 vertices.
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled
paper_title: Coloring count cones of planar graphs

also_stated_in.title: Coloring count cones of planar graphs

title: Informal Conjecture (polynomial expansion and bounded twin-width)
statement_text: All classes of polynomial expansion may also have bounded twin-width.
context_text: The authors note that proper minor-closed classes have bounded twin-width, and observe that bounded twin-width is incomparable with bounded degree, bounded expansion, and nowhere denseness. They remark 'As far as we know, all classes of polynomial expansion may also have bounded twin-width,' suggesting this as an open direction. Figure 3 depicts this with a dash-dotted edge indicating possible inclusion.
notes: Stated in hedged prose ('as far as we know … may also'); clearly conjectural but no labelled environment.
paper_title: Twin-width I: tractable FO model checking

title: Question (common superclass for FPT FO model checking)
statement_text: Do bounded twin-width and nowhere dense classes admit a natural common superclass still admitting an FPT algorithm for FO model checking?
context_text: Figure 3 shows that bounded twin-width and nowhere dense classes together roughly subsume all current knowledge on fixed-parameter tractability of FO model checking. The authors explicitly pose whether a natural common superclass of both still admits an FPT algorithm for FO model checking.
notes: Posed as a rhetorical question in the caption of Figure 3, not in a labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Twin-width I: tractable FO model checking

title: Problem 1
statement_text: Does every $K_t$-minor-free graph $G$ satisfy $\chi_\ell(G) \leq 2t$?
