title: Informal conjecture on non-tightness of upper bound for binary focal families
statement_text: For $q = 2$ and large $n$, the upper bound $g^{q\text{-ff}}_r(n) \leq (r-1)\, q^{\lceil (r-2)n/(r-1) \rceil}$ of Theorem 1.3 is not tight.
context_text: Proposition 3.2 shows via Reed-Solomon codes that the upper bound is essentially tight when $q \geq n$ and $q$ is a prime power. The authors believe the binary case ($q = 2$) behaves differently, and support this belief by proving in Section 4 that the bound can be significantly improved when the family of binary vectors forms a linear code (closed under addition modulo 2).
notes: PDF source — math notation may be garbled.
paper_title: Near-sunflowers and focal families

title: Conjecture 3
statement_text: There exist $C, C' > 0$ such that, for any simple graph $G$ that is embeddable on a surface of Euler genus $g$, if every edge is assigned at most $Ck$ conflicts from $[k]^2$, then $G$ is conflict $k$-colourable, provided $k \geq C'\sqrt{g}$.
context_text: Theorem 1 gives an upper bound of the form $\chi_=(G) = O(\sqrt{g}\log g)$ in the $\mu = \Theta(\sqrt{g})$ regime, evocative of Heawood's bound. The authors note that $C < 1/2$ is forced by their second instructive example, and that a previous version of the manuscript incorrectly conjectured $C$ could be taken arbitrarily close to 1.
paper_title: Single-conflict colouring

title: Informal conjecture on single-conflict chromatic number of degenerate graphs
statement_text: There might yet be some constant $C' > 0$ such that the single-conflict chromatic number of any $d$-degenerate graph on $n$ vertices is at most $C'\sqrt{d}\log n$.
context_text: The authors discuss whether degeneracy could serve as an alternative approach to proving Theorem 1 in the $\mu = 1$ case, analogous to Heawood's original method. They note that a construction of Kostochka and Zhu shows $d$-degenerate graphs can have single-conflict chromatic number greater than $d$, but the bound $C'\sqrt{d}\log n$ would still imply the $\mu = 1$ case of Theorem 1; Theorem 4 gives the weaker bound $C_2(nd)^{1/4}\log(nd)$.
notes: Stated as 'there might yet be' and 'we have not been able to prove this thus far'; clearly conjectural but not a labelled environment.
paper_title: Single-conflict colouring

title: Problem 1.2
statement_text: What are the finite sets $\mathcal{F}$ of digraphs for which the class $\mathrm{Forb}_{\mathrm{ind}}(\mathcal{F})$ has bounded dichromatic number?
context_text: This is the central problem of the paper, extending the Gyárfás-Sumner interrogation to directed graphs via the dichromatic number $\vec{\chi}$. A finite set $\mathcal{F}$ of digraphs is called heroic if $\mathrm{Forb}_{\mathrm{ind}}(\mathcal{F})$ has bounded dichromatic number. The paper focuses primarily on characterizing heroic sets of size three.
paper_title: Extension of Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture to digraphs

title: Conjecture 4.2
statement_text: Let $H$ be a hero and let $F$ be an oriented forest. The set $\{\overleftrightarrow{K_2}, H, F\}$ is heroic if and only if: either $F$ is the disjoint union of oriented stars, or $H$ is a transitive tournament.
context_text: This is the main conjecture of the paper, a digraph analog of the Gyárfás-Sumner conjecture for oriented graphs. The 'only if' direction is proved in Section 6.1. The case where both conditions hold simultaneously (H a transitive tournament and F a disjoint union of oriented stars) is already settled by Chudnovsky, Scott and Seymour [6].
notes: PDF source — key symbols ($\overleftrightarrow{K_2}$, dichromatic number) inferred from context; the dichromatic number notation appears as '(cid:126)χ' in the raw PDF extraction.
paper_title: Extension of Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture to digraphs

title: Conjecture 4.4
statement_text: Given an oriented forest $F$ and for every integer $k$, $\{\overleftrightarrow{K_2}, K_k, F\}$ is heroic.
context_text: This conjecture is equivalent to the special case of Conjecture 4.2 where $H = TT_k$ is a transitive tournament, since every tournament on at least $2k$ vertices contains $TT_k$ as an induced subdigraph. It generalises the heroic-set result for $\{\overleftrightarrow{K_2}, K_k, P^+(3)\}$ proved in Theorem 4.3, where $K_k$ denotes the digraph on $k$ vertices with no arc.
notes: PDF source — the raw text reads 'for every integer l' but uses $K_k$ in the set; the variable $l$ is likely a PDF extraction artifact for $k$. Here $K_k$ denotes the independent set (edgeless digraph) on $k$ vertices, as per the paper's notation.
paper_title: Extension of Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture to digraphs

title: Research direction: minimal heroic tournament families
statement_text: Characterize all finite and minimal families $\mathcal{H} = \{H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_k\}$ of tournaments such that $\{\overrightarrow{K_2}, \overleftrightarrow{K_2}, H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_k\}$ is a heroic set.
context_text: Theorem 2.2 of Berger et al. [4] characterizes minimal heroic sets of this form with $|\mathcal{H}|=1$ (i.e., single-hero extensions). The authors note that a minimal heroic subset of order 2 or more cannot contain a hero tournament, making the general classification a natural open direction.
notes: Expressed as 'an interesting direction of research' in the paper prose, without a formal labelled conjecture or problem environment.
paper_title: Extension of Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture to digraphs

title: Conjecture 1.1
statement_text: Every planar graph has fractional vertex-arboricity at most two.
context_text: This is the fractional generalization of the Albertson-Berman Conjecture: since $|V(G)|/a(G) \leq \mathrm{va}_f(G)$, the conjecture implies $a(G) \geq |V(G)|/2$. If true, it would also imply that every planar graph has fractional chromatic number at most four.
paper_title: Fractional vertex-arboricity of planar graphs

title: Open Problem: Finding a Shortest Even Hole
statement_text: Finding a shortest even hole in a graph (if one exists).
context_text: After surveying polynomial-time algorithms for detecting even holes, the authors note that finding a shortest even hole remains open: "Another is finding a shortest even hole if there is one; this we have not yet been able to solve." This is posed as an analogue of the result of Chudnovsky, Scott and Seymour [8] that finds a shortest odd hole in $O(|G|^{14})$ time.
notes: Stated informally in the introduction without a labelled environment.
paper_title: Detecting a long even hole

title: Open Question: Detecting Holes of Length Divisible by Three
statement_text: Can holes whose length is a multiple of three be detected in polynomial time?
context_text: Having focused on even and odd holes, the authors remark: "what about holes of length a multiple of three, can we detect them in polynomial time? Triangle-free graphs with no holes of length a multiple of three have some very interesting properties [9], but we currently have no idea how to recognize such graphs." This suggests the problem of recognising graphs with no hole of length $\equiv 0 \pmod{3}$ as a natural next direction.
notes: Stated informally in the introduction as a rhetorical open question; no labelled environment.
paper_title: Detecting a long even hole

title: Conjecture 5
statement_text: The crossing number of $M_{n,t}$ is equal to $$H(n) - \frac{1}{2}\,t(k-1)(k-2),$$ where $k = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$.
context_text: Here $M_{n,t}$ denotes the graph obtained from $K_n$ by removing a matching of size $t$, and $k = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$. Corollary 4 shows that geodesic drawings of $M_{n,t}$ built from sets of strength $0$ achieve this crossing count; the existence of many such sets of strength $0$ motivates the conjecture that this value is optimal.
notes: PDF source; the algebraic expression is unambiguous.
paper_title: On a conjecture by Anthony Hill

title: Open Problem (induced st-path of excess length three)
statement_text: Is there a polynomial-time algorithm to test whether a graph contains an induced path between specified vertices $s, t$ of length at least three more than the shortest $st$-path?
context_text: Berger, Spirkl, and Seymour (including a co-author of the present paper) have a poly-time algorithm to detect an induced $st$-path longer than the shortest $st$-path. Whether a poly-time algorithm exists to detect one that is at least three longer remains open, as noted in the introduction.
notes: Stated in passing as 'It is open whether...' with no formal label. Arises from related work by Berger, Spirkl, and Seymour; one of the present paper's authors (Seymour) is involved in the related work.
paper_title: Detecting a long odd hole

title: Informal Conjecture (heavy path extension)
statement_text: If $C$ is a shortest long odd hole in a graph $G$ and $M$ is a set of $C$-major vertices such that one vertex of $M$ is nonadjacent to all the others, then there exists a path of $C$ of bounded length such that every vertex of $M$ has a neighbour in that path.
context_text: The authors attempted to extend the 'heavy edge' lemma from the odd hole algorithm of [6] to the long odd hole setting: a 'heavy path' of bounded length containing a neighbour of every vertex in $M$ would suffice for constructing the cleaning list. The authors believe the extension is plausible but were unable to prove it, and had to use a more complicated replacement argument instead.
notes: Stated as 'this extension might be true, but we were unable to prove it.' No formal labelled environment. PDF source — math notation may be partially garbled.
paper_title: Detecting a long odd hole

title: Open Question (running time improvement for long hole detection)
statement_text: Can the running times of $O(|G|^{\ell+1})$ for detecting a long hole and $O(|G|^{20\ell+40})$ for detecting a long odd hole — both $|G|^{O(\ell)}$ — be substantially improved for fixed $\ell$?
context_text: The paper gives algorithms with running time $|G|^{O(\ell)}$ for both long hole and long odd hole detection. Both problems are NP-hard when $\ell$ is part of the input, but whether the dependence on $\ell$ in the exponent can be reduced for fixed $\ell$ is left as an open question.
notes: Stated as 'We do not know if either can be substantially improved.' No formal labelled environment.
paper_title: Detecting a long odd hole

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: There is a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that every even-hole-free graph of degree at most $d$ has tree-width at most $f(d)$.
context_text: The class of even-hole-free graphs has unbounded tree-width in general (e.g., complete graphs and chordal graphs). Bounded degree is conjectured to be a sufficient structural restriction. If true, it would imply that even-hole-freeness is testable with constant query complexity and polylogarithmic running time in the bounded-degree graph model, via CMSO testability on bounded tree-width graphs. The paper proves the conjecture for $d \leq 3$ (subcubic graphs) and proves a weakening for $d = 4$.
paper_title: On the tree-width of even-hole-free graphs

title: Conjecture 3
statement_text: For every $d \in \mathbb{N}$ there is a function $f_d : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that every graph with degree at most $d$ and tree-width at least $f_d(k)$ contains a $(k \times k)$-wall or the line graph of a $(k \times k)$-wall as an induced subgraph.
context_text: This is an induced grid theorem analogue for bounded-degree graphs, motivated by the construction of (even hole, $K_4$)-free graphs of unbounded tree-width that requires vertices of large degree and large clique minors. The conjecture is described as 'wide open' and formally implies Conjecture 2. The paper's Theorem 1.1 (the induced grid theorem for minor-free graphs) can be seen as a step in the direction of its proof.
paper_title: On the tree-width of even-hole-free graphs

title: Open question on $R_\ell(K_{1,2}, k)$ for odd $k$
statement_text: We do not know whether $R_\ell(K_{1,2}, k)$ is $k + 1$ or $k + 2$ when $k$ is odd.
context_text: For even $k$, the paper establishes $R_\ell(K_{1,2}, k) = k+1 = R(K_{1,2}, k)$ using the result of Häggkvist and Janssen that $\chi'_\ell(K_n) = \chi'(K_n)$ for odd $n$. The case of odd $k$ is equivalent to the List Colouring Conjecture for cliques of even order, which asks whether $\chi'_\ell(K_n)$ equals $n$ or $n-1$ for even $n$.
notes: Stated in prose; directly tied to the open case of the List Colouring Conjecture for cliques of even order.
paper_title: List Ramsey numbers

title: Open problem on equality $R_\ell(K_r, 2) = R(K_r, 2)$ for $r > 3$
statement_text: For $r > 3$ we cannot even decide the equality of the two Ramsey numbers of $K_r$ when $k = 2$.
context_text: The classic probabilistic lower bound gives $2^{r/2} < R_\ell(K_r, 2) \leq R(K_r, 2) < 2^{2r}$. The equality $R_\ell(K_3, 2) = 6 = R(K_3, 2)$ is easily verified, but for larger complete graphs the question of whether the list and ordinary 2-colour Ramsey numbers coincide is entirely open.
notes: Stated in prose. The extracted text does not include Section 3 (the paper's dedicated open problems section), so further explicit problems posed there are not captured here.
paper_title: List Ramsey numbers

title: 4-colorability on Fixed Surfaces (Open Step in Thomassen's Program)
statement_text: Whether 4-colorability of graphs embedded in a fixed surface can be tested in polynomial time remains open; the authors remark that prospects for its resolution in the near future are not very bright.
context_text: After announcing that Theorem 1.3 completes one of two missing steps in Thomassen's polynomial-time colorability program, the authors identify the remaining open step as the analogous question for 4-coloring graphs on a fixed surface.
notes: Stated informally in the introduction as the outstanding second step of Thomassen's research program; no formal problem number is assigned.
paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces IV. Bounding face sizes of 4-critical graphs

title: Size of symmetric intersecting families in $[k]^n$
statement_text: How large can a symmetric, intersecting subfamily of $[k]^n$ really be (for fixed $k \geq 3$)?
context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes that any symmetric intersecting $A \subset [k]^n$ has size $o(k^n)$, but the precise asymptotics remain open. The best constructions known to the authors are set-intersecting families, e.g., those based on lines of a projective plane $PG(2,q)$, yielding size roughly $k^{n-\sqrt{n}}$.
notes: PDF source — math notation may be garbled. No labeled environment; described by the authors as 'the most obvious question raised by the present work' in Section 4.
paper_title: On symmetric intersecting families of vectors

title: Largest symmetric intersecting families are set-intersecting
statement_text: It seems possible (but maybe impossible to prove) that the largest symmetric intersecting families in $[k]^n$ are set-intersecting.
context_text: A family $A \subset [k]^n$ is set-intersecting if there exists a symmetric intersecting family $\mathcal{B}$ of subsets of $[n]$ and $\ell \in [k]$ such that $x \in A$ if and only if there is some $B \in \mathcal{B}$ with $x_i = \ell$ for all $i \in B$. The authors note that all their best constructions have this form, motivating the conjecture.
notes: PDF source — math notation may be garbled. Stated with the explicit hedge 'seems possible (but maybe impossible to prove)'; no labeled environment.
paper_title: On symmetric intersecting families of vectors

title: Polynomial improvement on symmetric intersecting family size bound
statement_text: Show that there exist constants $c, \delta > 0$ (possibly depending on $k$) such that for any symmetric intersecting $A \subset [k]^n$, we have $\log_k |A| \leq n - cn^{\delta}$.
context_text: This is posed in Section 4 as a concrete and more modest goal, falling short of identifying the exact extremal size: even a polynomial gain over the $o(k^n)$ bound of Theorem 1.1, expressed as $\log_k |A| \leq n - cn^\delta$, would be a significant advance.
notes: PDF source — math notation may be garbled. No labeled environment; introduced with 'it would be very interesting to at least show' in Section 4.
paper_title: On symmetric intersecting families of vectors

title: Open Problem: $\Delta + O(1)$ bound for neighbour sum distinguishing colourings
statement_text: It is an intriguing open problem whether a $\Delta + O(1)$ bound holds on the number of colours required for a neighbour sum distinguishing edge colouring.
context_text: A neighbour sum distinguishing colouring is a proper edge colouring $c$ with colours in $[\Delta+2]$ such that for every edge $uv$, the sum of colours incident to $u$ and that of $v$ are distinct — a strengthening of AVD-colourings conjectured by Flandrin et al. The best known asymptotic bound is $\Delta + O(\Delta^{1/2})$ due to Przybyło. The authors note that neither Hatami's proof for AVD-colourings nor their own entropy compression argument seems adaptable to this setting.
notes: Stated in running prose without a labelled environment; clearly posed as an open problem by the authors.
paper_title: Progress on the adjacent vertex distinguishing edge colouring conjecture

title: Informal Conjecture: probabilistic methods insufficient for the $\Delta+2$ bound
statement_text: One could suspect, as we do, that probabilistic methods such as the Local Lemma or entropy compression will probably not be enough on their own to prove the $\Delta+2$ bound from Conjecture 1, assuming the conjecture is true.
context_text: After reducing Hatami's $\Delta+300$ bound to $\Delta+19$ using entropy compression, the authors reflect on the limitations of probabilistic techniques for fully resolving the Zhang–Liu–Wang conjecture, noting that some extra colours seem inevitably wasted in any random process.
notes: Conjectural belief expressed in prose using 'one could suspect, as we do'; no labelled environment.
paper_title: Progress on the adjacent vertex distinguishing edge colouring conjecture

title: Question on extremal self-annihilating subspaces (Theorem 2.3)
statement_text: For $r < n/2$, are all extremal self-annihilating subspaces $W \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$ with $\dim W = \binom{n-1}{r-1}$ of the form $\{v \wedge z : z \in \bigwedge^{r-1} V\}$ for some fixed $v \in V$?
context_text: Theorem 2.3 shows that a self-annihilating subspace $W \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$ satisfies $\dim W \leq \binom{n-1}{r-1}$ for $r \leq n/2$, with equality achieved by spaces of the form $\{v \wedge z : z \in \bigwedge^{r-1} V\}$. The authors verify this characterization for $r = 1$ and $r = 2$ and ask whether it holds in general.
notes: Stated in running prose after Theorem 2.3: 'It is an interesting question to describe the extremal examples for Theorem 2.3 ... Could all extremal examples be of this form?' PDF source.
paper_title: Combinatorics in the exterior algebra and the Bollobás Two Families Theorem

title: Question on extremal mutually annihilating pairs (Theorem 2.4)
statement_text: For $r, s < n/2$, characterize all pairs of subspaces $U \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$ and $W \subseteq \bigwedge^s V$ that are mutually annihilating and attain $\dim U \cdot \dim W = \binom{n-1}{r-1}\binom{n-1}{s-1}$.
context_text: Theorem 2.4 bounds $\dim U \cdot \dim W$ for mutually annihilating pairs $U \subseteq \bigwedge^r V$, $W \subseteq \bigwedge^s V$ with $r, s \leq n/2$. Equality is achieved by fixing $v \in V$ and setting $U = \{v \wedge z : z \in \bigwedge^{r-1} V\}$ and $W = \{v \wedge z : z \in \bigwedge^{s-1} V\}$, and the authors ask for a full characterization of all extremal examples.
notes: Stated in running prose after Theorem 2.4: 'it would be interesting to characterize the extremal examples when $r, s < n/2$.' PDF source.
paper_title: Combinatorics in the exterior algebra and the Bollobás Two Families Theorem

title: Informal open question on the exact value of $b_\varepsilon$
statement_text: It is unclear whether the upper or the lower bound on $b_\varepsilon$ can be improved for $0<\varepsilon<\frac{1}{2}$. While the fact that $b_{1/2}=0$ matches the lower bound suggests that a better construction improving the upper bound in general could exist, it is also plausible that this is just a "dimension 2" artifact and in fact the lower bound might be possible to improve for $\varepsilon<1/2$ (possibly leading to discontinuity of $b_\varepsilon$ at $\varepsilon=1/2$).
context_text: After establishing $\frac{1}{2\varepsilon}-1\leq b_\varepsilon\leq\frac{1}{2\varepsilon}-\frac{1}{2}$ for $0<\varepsilon<\frac{1}{2}$ and $b_\varepsilon=0$ for $\frac{1}{2}\leq\varepsilon\leq 1$, the paper notes that the remaining gap of $\frac{1}{2}$ in the bounds on $b_\varepsilon$ is unresolved, and that the behaviour at $\varepsilon=1/2$ makes the problem structurally interesting.
notes: Stated as an open remark in the concluding summary. PDF source.
paper_title: A note on sublinear separators and expansion

title: Informal conjecture $b'_\varepsilon = b_\varepsilon$ for $0<\varepsilon<\tfrac{1}{2}$
statement_text: It seems likely that $b'_\varepsilon = b_\varepsilon$ when $0 < \varepsilon < \frac{1}{2}$ as well.
context_text: The parameter $b'_\varepsilon$ is defined analogously to $b_\varepsilon$ but with the one-sided condition $s_\mathcal{G}(n)=\Omega(n^{1-\varepsilon})$ rather than $\Theta(n^{1-\varepsilon})$; equality $b'_\varepsilon=b_\varepsilon=0$ already holds for $\frac{1}{2}\leq\varepsilon\leq 1$, but extending this to $0<\varepsilon<\frac{1}{2}$ requires showing that a one-sided lower bound on separators alone forces the same expansion lower bound, which is not obvious.
notes: Conjectural language 'It seems likely' appears in the final section. PDF source.
paper_title: A note on sublinear separators and expansion

title: Open problem: χ-bounded vs. polynomially χ-bounded
statement_text: Is there a hereditary graph class that is $\chi$-bounded but (provably) not polynomially $\chi$-bounded?
context_text: The authors remark that no hereditary graph class is currently known to be $\chi$-bounded while provably failing to be polynomially $\chi$-bounded, citing [Esp17]. This observation motivates the study of polynomial $\chi$-boundedness and frames the paper's main contribution as evidence toward a positive answer for the class of graphs of bounded cliquewidth.
notes: Stated as informal running prose in the introduction; citation [Esp17] (Esperet 2017, likely a survey) is appended, suggesting the observation may originate there. The paper text is truncated before Section 5, so any further explicitly labelled open problems or conjectures in later sections are not visible and could not be extracted.
paper_title: Graphs of bounded cliquewidth are polynomially $χ$-bounded

title: The Small Conjecture
statement_text: Every small hereditary class has bounded twin-width.
context_text: The paper proves that every bounded twin-width class is small (has at most $n! c^n$ labeled $n$-vertex graphs for some constant $c$), unifying results for bounded treewidth, proper subclasses of permutation graphs, and proper minor-closed classes. The small conjecture posits the converse. The authors explore it by verifying bounded twin-width for many natural small hereditary classes that could serve as counterexamples, including subdivisions of $K_n$, graphs of bounded stack or queue number, and cubic expanders from iterated random 2-lifts.
notes: Explicitly named 'the small conjecture' repeatedly in the abstract and introduction; no labelled theorem environment visible in the extracted text. The PDF extraction is truncated, so later numbered conjecture environments may exist.
paper_title: Twin-width II: small classes

title: Question: polynomial expansion and twin-width
statement_text: Do classes with polynomial expansion have bounded twin-width?
context_text: Towards the small conjecture, the authors identify showing that small sparse classes have bounded (sparse) twin-width as a first, challenging step. Since classes with polynomial expansion are small, confirming bounded twin-width for them would constitute meaningful progress.
notes: Stated as an explicit open question in the introduction without a labelled environment. PDF extraction is truncated; additional numbered questions may appear in later sections.
paper_title: Twin-width II: small classes

title: Conjecture 5
statement_text: If $G$ is a planar graph of girth at least five on $n$ vertices, then $R_6(G)$ has diameter $O(n)$.
context_text: Planar graphs of girth at least five are 2-degenerate and can be colored from lists of size three; combining Lemma 2 with Theorem 1 already shows 7 colors suffice for linear diameter. The authors believe this bound can be improved to 6, and suspect that a variation of their Thomassen-type method can be used to prove the conjecture, though several complications arise.
paper_title: A Thomassen-type method for planar graph recoloring

title: Open Problem (edge-EP property characterization for planar graph expansions)
statement_text: Finding a characterization for the planar graphs $J$ such that $J$-expansions have the edge-Erd\H{o}s-P\'osa property remains an intriguing open problem.
context_text: Robertson and Seymour showed that $J$-expansions have the vertex-EP property if and only if $J$ is planar. By contrast, it was shown in [4] that there exist planar graphs $J$ — in particular sufficiently large ladders and subcubic trees with sufficiently large pathwidth — for which $J$-expansions do not have the edge-EP property. The precise characterization of which planar $J$ yield the edge-EP property is unknown.
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction as 'an intriguing open problem'; no labelled theorem environment. Section 5, which the paper explicitly announces contains further open problems, appears to be missing from the PDF extraction — additional items likely exist there.
paper_title: Erdős-Pósa from ball packing

title: Open problem on queue-number of bounded layered treewidth graphs
statement_text: It is open whether graphs of bounded layered treewidth have bounded queue-number.
context_text: Dujmović et al. proved that every $n$-vertex graph with layered treewidth $k$ has queue-number $O(k \log n)$. Subsequent to the submission of this paper, improved bounds on queue-number were obtained, but the question of whether a constant bound holds for graphs of bounded layered treewidth remains open.
notes: Stated in a footnote without a formal labeled environment.
paper_title: Minor-closed graph classes with bounded layered pathwidth

title: Conjecture 2.4
statement_text: Let $H$ be a graph and let $P$ be a forest of $H$. Then every coherent ideal contains a $P$-filleting of $H$.
context_text: The paper's main theorem (2.3) proves the statement when $P$ is a path of $H$. The authors propose this strengthening, noting it would be best possible since the conclusion fails when $P$ contains a cycle of $H$ (as coherent ideals can have arbitrarily large girth). If true, it would imply the main theorem of [8] (strong Erdős-Hajnal property for all forests) in the same way Theorem 2.3 implies the main theorem of [18].
paper_title: Pure pairs. II. Excluding all subdivisions of a graph

title: Question 1.5
statement_text: For fixed $k > 1$, is there a polynomial-time algorithm that, given a graph $G$ and $u, v \in V(G)$, decides whether there is an induced $uv$-path $P$ in $G$ of length at least $d(u, v) + k$?
context_text: The paper's main result (Theorem 1.1) gives a polynomial-time algorithm for the case $k = 1$ (detecting any induced non-shortest path), and the authors note the algorithm can be adjusted for $k = 2$. The question remains open even for $k = 3$. Fixing $k$ is necessary, since Theorem 1.6 shows the problem is NP-hard when $k$ is part of the input (e.g., deciding whether there exists a $uv$-NSP of length at least $2d_G(u,v)$).
paper_title: Finding an induced path that is not a shortest path

title: Optimality of the exponent 1/4
statement_text: Is the coefficient $\frac{1}{4}$ of $g$ in the lower bound $\Omega\!\left((\delta-1)^{g/4}\right)$ for the cop number of graphs of girth $g$ and minimum degree $\delta$ best possible, i.e., can the exponent be improved to $\left(\frac{1}{4}+\varepsilon\right)g$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$?
context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.1, the authors note: 'One may naturally ask if the coefficient $\frac{1}{4}$ of $g$ is best possible. While we do not have an answer to this question …'. They exhibit conditional evidence (via Meyniel's conjecture and Conjecture 1.3) that $\frac{1}{4}$ is optimal, and also prove via Ramanujan graphs that the true exponent cannot exceed $\frac{3}{8}$.
notes: Stated as prose without a labelled environment; the abstract frames it as 'several reasons for conjecturing that the exponent $\frac{1}{4}g$ cannot be improved to $(\frac{1}{4}+\varepsilon)g$'.
paper_title: On the cop number of graphs of high girth

title: Conjecture 1.10
statement_text: There exists a fixed constant $c > 1$ so that the following holds. For every 3-edge-connected oriented graph $G = (V, E)$ of order $n$, every abelian group $\Gamma$ with $|\Gamma| \geq 6$, and every $f : E \to \Gamma$, there exist at least $c^n$ flows $\phi : E \to \Gamma$ with $\phi(e) \neq f(e)$ for every $e \in E$.
context_text: Theorem 1.9 establishes an explicit exponential lower bound on the number of $\Gamma$-connected flows for groups of size $k \geq 8$, but yields only the existence of a single flow for $k = 6, 7$. The authors attribute this gap to a shortcoming of their techniques and conjecture the exponential bound holds for all abelian groups of size at least 6. Theorem 1.9 confirms the conjecture for all groups except $\mathbb{Z}_6$ and $\mathbb{Z}_7$; Theorem 1.11 gives a superpolynomial (but not exponential) lower bound for $\mathbb{Z}_6$.
paper_title: Many flows in the group connectivity setting

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: Let $G$ be a simple $n$-vertex graph and $c$ be a colouring of $E(G)$ with $n$ colours, where each colour class has size at least $r$. Then $(G, c)$ contains a cycle $C$ of length at most $\lceil n/r \rceil$ such that no two incident edges of $C$ are the same colour.
context_text: Introduced by the authors as a weakening of Aharoni's Conjecture 3 (the rainbow condition on the whole cycle is replaced by a proper-edge-colouring condition on incident pairs). The authors prove that Conjecture 4 implies the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture via a direct colouring construction on the underlying undirected graph of a digraph.
notes: PDF source — ceiling notation garbled but mathematical content is unambiguous.
paper_title: Short rainbow cycles in graphs and matroids
paper_authors: Sebastián González Hermosillo de la Maza

title: Conjecture 12
statement_text: Let $M$ be a simple rank-$(n-1)$ matroid and $c$ be a colouring of $E(M)$ with $n$ colours, where each colour class has size at least $2$. Then $M$ contains a rainbow circuit of size at most $\lceil n/2 \rceil$.
context_text: Proposed as the natural matroid analogue of Theorem 5. The authors note it holds for graphic matroids (by Theorem 5) and prove it for cographic matroids (Theorem 13), but observe it fails for general binary matroids because the uniform matroid $U_{n-1,m}$ contains no circuits of size less than $n$.
notes: PDF source — ceiling notation garbled but mathematical content is unambiguous.
paper_title: Short rainbow cycles in graphs and matroids
paper_authors: Sebastián González Hermosillo de la Maza

title: Conjecture 5 (Improved Approximation Conjecture)
statement_text: Every graph $H$ satisfies the improved approximation property, i.e., there exists a constant $\varepsilon > 0$ such that MIS admits a (randomized) $n^{1-\varepsilon}$-approximation polynomial algorithm on every $H$-free $n$-vertex graph $G$.
context_text: This conjecture is introduced as an algorithmic weakening of the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture. It states informally that the inapproximability of MIS in general graphs can always be beaten in any proper hereditary class. It is strictly weaker than the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture, since MIS is polynomial in $P_6$-free graphs while it is open whether $P_5$ satisfies the Erdős-Hajnal property.
paper_title: An algorithmic weakening of the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture

title: Question: optimal approximation exponent for MIS in H-free graphs
statement_text: For a given $H$, what is the largest $\varepsilon > 0$ such that MIS admits an $O(n^{1-\varepsilon})$-approximation algorithm in $H$-free graphs?
context_text: After establishing that many graphs $H$ satisfy the improved approximation property, the authors ask how to optimise the approximation ratio. They investigate this in Sections 3 and 4, proving e.g. a $\sqrt{OPT}$-approximation in $C_4$-free graphs and a tight inapproximability bound in triangle-free and high-girth graphs.
notes: Posed as an open research direction in running prose in the 'Results and organization' section; partially investigated within the paper but not fully resolved.
paper_title: An algorithmic weakening of the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: There exists an absolute constant $d \geq 2$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a plane triangle-free graph, let $S$ be a set of vertices of $G$ and let $\psi : S \to \{1, 2, 3\}$ be an arbitrary function. If the distance between every two vertices of $S$ is at least $d$, then $\psi$ extends to a 3-coloring of $G$.
context_text: This is a natural extension of Theorem 1.3 to the case of precolored single vertices, where forbidding separating 4-cycles should not be necessary. The paper shows in Theorem 5.1 that Conjecture 1.4 is implied by the seemingly simpler Conjecture 1.5.
notes: The paper states that Conjecture 1.5 was subsequently confirmed by Dvořák and Lidický [16], which implies Conjecture 1.4 is also true.
paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces V. Coloring planar graphs with distant anomalies

also_stated_in.title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces V. Coloring planar graphs with distant anomalies

title: Informal Conjecture on reflexive graphs and coloring abundance
statement_text: Graphs with a large number of colorings tend to be reflexive.
context_text: The authors note that their results and extensive computational evidence support this observation, while acknowledging it appears counterintuitive: a large coloring complex might have too many colorings for the original graph to be reflexive. The underlying open question of why so many reflexive graphs exist (and why any exist at all) is stated as the central remaining problem.
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction without a labelled environment; backed by 'extensive computational evidence' but not given a precise mathematical formulation. The paper text is truncated mid-proof, so further conjectures in later sections may be missing.
paper_title: Reflexive coloring complexes for 3-edge-colorings of cubic graphs

title: Algorithm Termination Conjecture
statement_text: We conjecture that the algorithm always terminates.
context_text: The paper proposes an algorithm for computing homothetic pentagon contact representations based on systems of linear equations derived from five color forests. When the solution is non-negative it encodes distances between pentagon corners and the representation is constructed; otherwise negative variables guide a local change of the five color forest and the procedure restarts. The authors note that a proof of this conjecture would yield an existence proof for pentagon contact representations independent of Schramm's Monster Packing Theorem. Analogous algorithms for homothetic triangle and square contact representations exist but also lack termination proofs.
notes: Stated in running prose in the introduction without a labelled theorem environment. PDF extraction quality is adequate for this prose statement.
paper_title: Pentagon contact representations

title: Conjecture on unavoidable G-dependence in Theorem 2
statement_text: For some graph $H$, the dependence on $G$ in the multiplicative factor $a = a(G, H, r)$ of the bound $B(G \xrightarrow{r} H; t) \leq a \cdot b^t$ (Theorem 2) is unavoidable.
context_text: Theorem 2 shows that the exponential constant $b$ can be taken independent of $G$, but the multiplicative prefactor $a$ still depends on $G$. The authors believe this residual dependence is necessary for some $H$, and refer to the concluding remarks for further details.
notes: Stated in both the abstract and the introduction as a belief/conjecture without a labelled theorem environment; concluding remarks section was not fully captured in the PDF extraction.
paper_title: Extremal and Ramsey results on graph blowups

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: For each 3-graph $H$, there exists $C_H > 0$ such that any 3-graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with at least $C_H n^{5/2}$ edges contains a homeomorph of $H$.
context_text: The paper proves the exponent $3-1/5$ is universal but does not believe it to be tight. The conjectured optimal exponent $5/2 = 3 - 1/2$ is motivated by the Brown–Erdős–Sós result that $5/2$ is the correct exponent for the sphere $S^2$ (i.e., $K^3_4$), and by the analogy that homeomorphs in 3-graphs should appear roughly once spheres (homeomorphs of $S^2$) can be found, just as homeomorphs in graphs appear roughly once cycles (homeomorphs of $S^1$) can be found.
paper_title: A universal exponent for homeomorphs

title: Open problem on minimum independence ratio of Mycielski graphs
statement_text: The exact value of the minimum independence ratio of Mycielski graphs seems to be unknown.
context_text: While Cropper, Gyárfás and Lehel [CGL06] established asymptotic bounds $c_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\log m}}{m} \leq \mathrm{mir}(M_s) \leq c_2 \cdot \frac{\log m}{m}$ for every integer $m > 3$ (where $s$ is one less than the Ramsey number $R(3,m)$), no exact formula for $\mathrm{mir}(M_k)$ is known. The authors flag this gap while discussing why replacing the constant $2$ by a larger integer $k$ fails to generalise Observation 1.1.
notes: Stated informally in passing ('seems to be unknown'); no labelled environment. Question 1.2 is posed in the paper but immediately resolved negatively and so is not extracted as an open problem.
paper_title: Revisiting a theorem by Folkman on graph colouring

title: The $\varepsilon$-$t$-Net Problem
statement_text: How small are the smallest $\varepsilon$-$t$-nets for $H$? Can we compute them efficiently?
context_text: Given a finite hypergraph $H=(V,E)$, a positive integer $t$, and $\varepsilon\in(t/|V|,1)$, an $\varepsilon$-$t$-net is a family $S\subseteq\binom{V}{t}$ of $t$-element subsets of $V$ such that every hyperedge $e\in E$ with $|e|\geq\varepsilon|V|$ contains some $s\in S$. The problem generalises the classical $\varepsilon$-net problem ($t=1$) and the Mnet notion ($t=\Theta(\varepsilon|V|)$), and arises naturally in secret sharing and other combinatorial contexts.
notes: Stated as an explicitly labelled Problem environment in Section 1.2. PDF source causes epsilon to render as (cid:15) throughout, but the statement itself is unambiguous.
paper_title: The $ε$-$t$-Net Problem

title: Open Problem: Expansion vs. Corruption Detection
statement_text: There is still a significant gap between the expansion properties that suffice for solving the detection problem (Theorem 1.4) and the conditions in Proposition 1.6 that are necessary for such a solution. It will be interesting to obtain tighter relations between expansion and corruption detection.
context_text: Theorem 1.4 shows that strong spectral expansion (Ramanujan-type $(n,d,\lambda)$-graphs with $\lambda \leq 2\sqrt{d-1}$) suffices for identifying most truthful and corrupt vertices. Proposition 1.6 shows that if removing at most $\epsilon n$ vertices splits the graph into components of size at most $\epsilon n$, then no corruption detection is possible. The gap between these two regimes is noted by the authors as an open research direction.
notes: No labelled theorem environment; stated as a research direction at the end of Section 1.1. Section 4, where this is said to be 'further discussed', is not present in the extracted text (PDF extraction appears truncated).
paper_title: Distributed Corruption Detection in Networks

context_text: The polynomial-time algorithm of Grötschel, Lovász, and Schrijver for MWIS in perfect graphs relies on the ellipsoid method. The authors explicitly note that designing a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for this problem remains an important open problem.

title: Open Problem: asymptotic behavior of $Q(s,r)$ when $s$ and $r$ grow together
statement_text: Determine the asymptotic behavior of $Q(s, r)$ when $s$ and $r$ grow together (i.e., when $s$ is not fixed as $r \to \infty$).
context_text: The paper resolves $Q(s,r) = (1+o(1))\log_2 r$ for every fixed $s$ as $r \to \infty$, but explicitly states in the abstract that the problem of determining the asymptotic behavior of $Q(s,r)$ when both $s$ and $r$ grow simultaneously remains open.
notes: Section 4 (concluding remarks and open problems) is referenced in the introduction but its full content is not present in the extracted PDF text; additional open problems from that section may be missing.

title: Problem 3
statement_text: What is the minimum integer $\kappa$ such that for every planar graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, $R_{\kappa}(G)$ has diameter $O(n)$?
context_text: Planar graphs are 5-degenerate and have maximum average degree less than 6; prior results give $R_8(G)$ diameter $O(n(\log n)^7)$ and $R_{12}(G)$ diameter at most $6n$ for planar $G$. The paper improves the upper bound to $\kappa \leq 10$ via Theorem 4, and an isolated 6-coloring of the icosahedron shows $\kappa \geq 7$; the authors note that $\kappa = 7$ cannot be excluded.

title: Remark 1.2 (Open Problem on Sparse Densities)
statement_text: The problem of finding the asymptotic rate across the whole range of sparse densities remains open.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes the asymptotic rate for moderate deviations of subgraph counts in $G(n,m)$ when $t(n)$ is bounded away from 1, with a partial extension to sparser regimes satisfying $\max\{t^{1/2}n^{-1/2}\log n,\, t^{e-3/2}\} \ll \alpha_n \ll \min\{t^{2e-5/2}n^{1/2},\, t^{e+2}n^{1/2}\}$. The authors note they initially proved results only in the dense case $t\in(0,1)$ constant and have only partially extended to sparser regimes.
notes: Stated in Remark 1.2 as an open problem in prose form, without a dedicated labeled theorem environment. PDF source — math notation in surrounding theorems may be partially garbled.

title: Informal Conjecture on Existence of Hoppers for Large Euler Genus
statement_text: Hoppers may show up when the Euler genus is large enough.
context_text: Hoppers are a mysterious special class of graphs that arise as building blocks for obstructions of connectivity two for arbitrary Euler genus. The paper proves that hoppers do not exist when the genus is small and the authors are unable to construct any example for larger genus, yet they believe hoppers will eventually appear for sufficiently large genus.
notes: Expressed as a belief in running prose: 'we believe that they may show up when the genus is large enough.'

title: Problem 4
statement_text: Determine the smallest function $g : \mathbb{Z}^+ \to \mathbb{R}^+$ such that for every graph $G$, $\chi_f(G) \leq \rho(G)\,g(|V(G)|)$.
context_text: Since $\chi_f(G) = O(\rho(G) \log n)$ follows from a greedy independent-set argument, and the graphs produced by Corollary 3 have doubly exponential size in their fractional chromatic number (giving $g(n) = \Omega(\log\log n)$), the true growth rate of the smallest such $g$ is unknown.
paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio

title: Problem 5
statement_text: Do there exist, for some constant $c > 0$, graphs $G$ of arbitrarily large fractional chromatic number such that $\rho(G) \leq c$ and $\alpha_{\deg_H}(H) \geq |E(H)|/c$ for every $H \subseteq G$?
context_text: To lower-bound $\chi_f$ of the probabilistically constructed graphs in Theorem 1, the authors assign vertex weights proportional to degrees. Problem 5 asks whether such degree-weighted independence certificates can simultaneously be large while the ordinary Hall ratio remains bounded, motivating a search for intermediate weight families $\mathcal{F}$ between $\{0,1\}$-valued weights (Hall ratio) and all non-negative weights (fractional chromatic number).
notes: PDF source — the notation $\alpha_{\deg_H}$ for the degree-weighted maximum independent set weight may be slightly garbled.
paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio

title: Problem 8
statement_text: Is it true that a class $\mathcal{C}$ has bounded expansion if and only if there exists a function $f_6 : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $r \in \mathbb{N}$ and every $G \in \mathcal{C} \mathbin{\triangledown} r$, $\rho(G) \leq f_6(r)$?
context_text: Corollary 7 gives an analogous characterisation of bounded expansion via $\chi_f$ in shallow minors. The forward direction (bounded expansion implies bounded Hall ratio in shallow minors) is clear; the converse argument fails by Theorem 2. Proposition 1 shows a nowhere-dense class with unbounded Hall ratio, but no class with unbounded expansion and bounded Hall ratio in all shallow minors is known.
paper_title: 1-subdivisions, fractional chromatic number and Hall ratio

title: Combinatorial Polynomial-Time Algorithm for MIS/MWIS in Perfect Graphs
statement_text: No combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm is known for any of MIS, MWIS, MC, and MWC in perfect graphs; finding one is a major open problem in the field. At the moment we do not even have a polynomial-time combinatorial algorithm to solve MIS in perfect graphs with no hole of length four.
context_text: The Grötschel–Lovász–Schrijver algorithm solves MWIS on perfect graphs via the ellipsoid method, which the authors distinguish from combinatorial algorithms (roughly, algorithms not using division and operating directly on vertices and edges). Even the restricted case of MIS in perfect graphs with no $C_4$ (hole of length four) lacks a combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm.
notes: A long-standing community open problem cited as motivation in the introduction; not introduced by these authors. PDF source — math may be garbled.
paper_title: On the Maximum Weight Independent Set Problem in graphs without induced cycles of length at least five

title: FPT Algorithm for MWIS in (Long-Hole, k-Prism)-Free Graphs
statement_text: We do not see how to turn Theorem 1.1 into an FPT-algorithm (without using the result of [1]).
context_text: Theorem 1.1 gives a polynomial-time algorithm running in time $n^{O(k)}$ for MWIS in (long-hole, $k$-prism)-free $n$-vertex graphs for any fixed integer $k > 0$. Immediately after stating the theorem the authors remark that extending this to a fixed-parameter tractable algorithm parameterized by $k$ appears to be out of reach with their techniques, unless the result of Abrishami et al. [1] is invoked.
notes: Implicit open question stated in prose without a labelled environment. PDF source — math may be garbled.
paper_title: On the Maximum Weight Independent Set Problem in graphs without induced cycles of length at least five

title: Problem 1.4
statement_text: Do there exist functions $f_i : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$, $1 \leq i \leq 3$ such that (i) if the maximum degree of $G$ is $\Delta$, then $HG(G) \leq f_1(\Delta)$; (ii) if $G$ is $d$-degenerate, then $HG(G) \leq f_2(d)$; (iii) if the minimum degree of $G$ is $\delta$, then $HG(G) \geq f_3(\delta)$, and $f_3(\delta)$ tends to infinity when $\delta$ tends to infinity.
context_text: The authors seek to understand how basic graph parameters control the hat guessing number. The existence of $f_1$ is already known via the Lovász Local Lemma ($HG(G) < e^\Delta$), but whether $f_2$ (degeneracy bound) or $f_3$ (minimum-degree lower bound) exist is unclear. Theorems 1.6 and 1.8 provide partial progress toward part (ii).
paper_title: The hat guessing number of graphs

title: Open question: exact value of $HG(K_{n,n})$
statement_text: What is the exact value of $HG(K_{n,n})$?
context_text: After establishing $\Omega\!\left(n^{\frac{1}{2}-o(1)}\right) = HG(K_{n,n}) \leq n+1$ by combining results of Gadouleau–Georgiou with Theorem 1.2, the authors note that the determination of the exact value of $HG(K_{n,n})$ is left as an interesting open question.
notes: No labelled environment; stated as an open question in running prose immediately after Theorem 1.2.
paper_title: The hat guessing number of graphs

title: FPT on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs for Grundy Coloring and b-Chromatic Core
statement_text: Are \textsc{Grundy Coloring} and \textsc{b-Chromatic Core} fixed-parameter tractable on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs when parameterized by $k$?
context_text: The W[1]-hardness constructions for both problems rely heavily on large half-graphs as color-propagation units; excluding large half-graphs may restore tractability. However, the key combinatorial lemma that gives FPT on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs collapses on $H_{t,t}$-free graphs, so the question is explicitly left open.
notes: Stated as an explicit open problem in the 'Limits and further questions' section; no formal numbered theorem environment.

statement_text: Does \textsc{Grundy Coloring} admit a fixed-parameter tractable algorithm on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs when parameterized by $k$?
context_text: The paper establishes FPT algorithms for b-Chromatic Core and Partial Grundy Coloring on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs (parameterized by $k+t$), but the analogous question for Grundy Coloring on $K_{t,t}$-free graphs is explicitly left unsettled.
notes: Stated explicitly as unsettled in the 'Limits and further questions' section; no formal numbered theorem environment.

title: Parameterized complexity of Partial Grundy Coloring on general graphs
statement_text: What is the parameterized complexity of \textsc{Partial Grundy Coloring} on general graphs, parameterized by $k$?
context_text: The authors were unable to prove W[1]-hardness for Partial Grundy Coloring because half-graphs cannot serve as color-propagation units (the partial Grundy number is already large on a large half-graph $H_{t,t}$). The class of $H_{t,t}$-free graphs is suggested as a promising intermediate case for understanding the general-graph complexity.
notes: Stated as an open problem in the 'Limits and further questions' section; no formal numbered theorem environment.
paper_title: Grundy Coloring & friends, Half-Graphs, Bicliques

title: Problem 2.15
statement_text: Let $F$ be a fixed (multi-)digraph. Instance: A (multi-)digraph $D$. Decide whether $D$ is circularly $F$-colourable.
context_text: A digraph $D$ is circularly $F$-colourable if there exists a circular homomorphism from $D$ to $F$. This problem generalises Problem 1 and serves as a directed analogue of the $H$-colouring problem for graphs. Conjecture 2.16 asserts that NP-completeness holds whenever $F$ contains a directed cycle, and Theorem 2.17 establishes this for almost all such cases.
notes: The complete dichotomy (all F with a directed cycle) is conjectured but not fully proved within the paper.
paper_title: On the Complexity of Digraph Colourings and Vertex Arboricity

title: Conjecture 2.16
statement_text: Let $F$ be a digraph which contains a directed cycle. Then the circular $F$-colouring problem is NP-complete.
context_text: The authors conjecture that, under the assumption $\mathsf{P}\neq\mathsf{NP}$, the only polynomially solvable instances of Problem 2.15 are those where $F$ is acyclic (for which only acyclic digraphs map circularly to $F$). Theorem 2.17 confirms this conjecture for almost all cases.
paper_title: On the Complexity of Digraph Colourings and Vertex Arboricity

title: Problem 3.21
statement_text: Let $p\geq 1$ be a fixed real number. Instance: A (multi-)digraph $D$. Decide whether $\vec{\chi}_{f}(D)\leq p$.
context_text: This problem asks for the complexity of deciding whether the fractional dichromatic number of a digraph is at most $p$. The paper proves NP-completeness for all real $p>1$ with $p\neq 2$, mirroring results of Feder et al. for the circular dichromatic number; the case $p=2$ is explicitly left open.
notes: The case $p=2$ remains unresolved after this paper.
paper_title: On the Complexity of Digraph Colourings and Vertex Arboricity

title: Problem 5.40
statement_text: For given $k,b\in\mathbb{N}$ with $k\geq b$, is there a directed graph $\vec{K}(k,b)$ with vertex set $\binom{[k]}{b}$ such that the following holds? The subdigraph of $\vec{K}(k,b)$ induced by any $\{B_{1},\ldots,B_{l}\}\subseteq\binom{[k]}{b}$ is acylic if and only if $\bigcap_{i=1}^{l}{B_{i}}\neq\emptyset$.
context_text: Such 'directed Kneser graphs' $\vec{K}(k,b)$ would allow $b$-tuple $k$-colourings of digraphs to be equivalent to the existence of circular homomorphisms to $\vec{K}(k,b)$, tying the fractional dichromatic number to circular homomorphism theory. Existence is confirmed for $k=b+1$ via the directed cycle of length $b+1$, but the general case is open.
paper_title: On the Complexity of Digraph Colourings and Vertex Arboricity

title: Question 6
statement_text: Given a polynomial $p$, do there necessarily exist real numbers $c, s \geq 1$ and a positive integer $d$, such that the following holds: every graph $G$ satisfying $\mathrm{col}_r(G) \leq p(r)$ for every non-negative integer $r$ admits a $(c, \sqsubseteq_s)$-tame representation in $\mathbb{R}^d$?
context_text: Theorem 4 shows that graphs with $(c,\sqsubseteq_s)$-tame representations in $\mathbb{R}^d$ have strong coloring numbers bounded by $\delta r^d$, paralleling Krauthgamer–Lee's characterization (Theorem 5) of polynomial-growth graph classes via grid embeddings. Answering Question 6 would determine whether Theorem 4 yields an analogous geometric characterization of graphs with polynomially bounded strong coloring numbers.
notes: The notation $(c,\sqsubseteq_s)$-tame and $\sqsubseteq_s$ are rendered as '(cid:118)s' in the PDF extraction; interpreted as the paper's comparability relation defined via volume overlap.
paper_title: Sublinear separators in intersection graphs of convex shapes

title: Informal conjecture: negative answer to Question 6
statement_text: The answer to Question 6 is negative: there exist graphs $G$ with $\mathrm{col}_r(G)$ polynomially bounded in $r$ that do not admit any $(c, \sqsubseteq_s)$-tame representation in any $\mathbb{R}^d$.
context_text: The authors write 'We suspect that the answer to Question 6 is negative based on the following.' They then demonstrate (Theorem 7) that a box-shaped version of such a representation fails, which motivates the suspicion that the full statement also fails.
notes: Stated as suspicion in prose ('we suspect'); Theorem 7 provides partial supporting evidence by ruling out box-shaped tame representations, but the full Question 6 remains open. PDF source — math notation may be garbled.

title: Optimal n for $L^d_n$-list-colorability
statement_text: Fixing $d$, what is the optimal value of $n$ such that $[n]^d$ is $L^d_n$-list-colorable?
context_text: Theorem 1.2 establishes that $[n]^d$ is $L^d_n$-list-colorable whenever $n \geq d+2$. The paper shows this threshold is not achieved for $n < d+2$ in specific cases (e.g., $[2]^3$ is not $L^3_2$-list-colorable), making the optimal threshold an open question whose notion of optimality is justified by Proposition 3.3.

title: Conjecture 2.2
statement_text: There is a polynomial $P$ such that every graph $G$ that contains no prism, pyramid, theta or turtle has at most $P(|V(G)|)$ minimal separators.
context_text: The authors survey families of graphs with exponentially many minimal separators (k-prisms, k-pyramids, k-thetas, k-turtles, k-ladders) and observe that every known such example contains a prism, pyramid, theta, or turtle. They propose Conjecture 2.2 as 'in some sense the best possible statement regarding bounding the number of minimal separators.' The paper proves a weaker result (Theorem 2.3) for the class $\mathcal{C}$ of (square, prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs.

title: Open problem: MIS complexity in even-hole-free graphs
statement_text: The complexity of computing a maximum independent set in an even-hole-free graph is not known.
context_text: Stated in the introduction as motivation for the paper's approach. The paper proves a polynomial-time algorithm for the strictly smaller class of (even hole, pyramid)-free graphs, with the hope that understanding the pyramid-free case may shed light on the full even-hole-free class.
notes: Stated as a known open problem in the field (referencing a survey [10]); no specific attribution to individual authors is given in the text.
paper_title: Maximum independent sets in (pyramid, even hole)-free graphs

title: Open problem: polynomial separator property for (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs
statement_text: It is unknown whether (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs have polynomially many minimal separators.
context_text: The authors note that a weakening of Conjecture 2.2 is obtained by restricting to (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs (since prisms, thetas, and turtles all contain even holes). They explicitly state they 'were not able to prove that (prism, pyramid, theta, even wheel)-free graphs have polynomially many minimal separators,' which is why the main result (Theorem 2.3) additionally excludes squares.

title: Conjecture 8
statement_text: For every planar triangulation whose 4-coloring complex is disconnected, there is a component of even parity and a component of odd parity.
context_text: After resolving Tutte's original question by constructing triangulations with arbitrarily many same-parity components, the authors identify this closely related conjecture based on extensive computation. It refines the picture by asserting that disconnected coloring complexes always contain both parities.
notes: The conjecture is described verbatim in both the abstract and the introduction with a forward reference to 'Conjecture 8', but the formal conjecture environment itself is not present in the extracted PDF text (the paper body appears truncated after Section 5). Statement reconstructed from the abstract and introduction prose.
paper_title: The Last Temptation of William T. Tutte

title: Conjecture 4.1
statement_text: For $d \geq 2$, there exists a constant $\varepsilon(d)$ such that every graph on $n$ vertices with VC-dimension at most $d$ contains a clique or an independent set of size $n^{\varepsilon(d)}$.
context_text: This is the VC-dimension specialisation of the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture proposed by the paper's authors. The best known bound for graphs of bounded VC-dimension is $e^{(\log n)^{1-o(1)}}$, established in a companion paper by the same authors; Conjecture 4.1 asks for the much stronger polynomial bound.
paper_title: Bounded VC-dimension implies the Schur-Erdos conjecture

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: For every planar graph $G$, we have $\mathrm{fvs}(G) \leq 2 \cdot \mathrm{fp}(G)$, where $\mathrm{fp}(G)$ is the maximum size of a face-packing of $G$, i.e., a cycle-packing where, for some embedding of $G$, every cycle bounds a face.
context_text: The authors pose this stronger conjecture to highlight how little is understood around Jones' Conjecture. They note that the example of many nested disjoint cycles shows the embedding cannot be fixed, and that the simple discharging argument giving $\mathrm{fvs}(G) \leq 3\,\mathrm{cp}(G)$ does not imply this conjecture even with factor 3 instead of 2.
paper_title: Jones' Conjecture in subcubic graphs

title: Informal Question (correct exponential constant in r(n,m))
statement_text: Is $3$, the lower bound on $r(n,m)$ given by considering a random set, the right exponential constant? That is, does $r(n,m) = N^{1-\varepsilon_m}$ hold with the same exponential base $3$ as in the random lower bound $r(n,m) \geq N^{1-(m+1)3^{-m}}$, rather than the base $C \approx 13.901$ appearing in the upper bound $r(n,m) \leq (1+o(1))N^{1-C^{-m}}$?
context_text: The lower and upper bounds for $r(n,m)$ (the maximum size of a subset of $\mathbb{F}_3^n$ containing no affine $m$-dimensional subspace) both have the form $N^{1-\varepsilon_m}$ with $\varepsilon_m \to 0$ exponentially fast in $m$, but with different exponential constants: $3$ in the random lower bound and $C \approx 13.901$ in the upper bound derived from the cap set supersaturation result. The authors note that resolving this question is closely tied to whether $n_3(\alpha,\beta)$ is small enough for $\beta = \alpha^{3+o(1)}$, and their main result provides evidence that the approach via popular differences likely cannot resolve it.
notes: Stated as an open question in running prose in the introduction without a formal labelled environment. PDF source — exponent notation in r(n,m) bounds may be garbled.
paper_title: Popular progression differences in vector spaces II

title: Conjecture (Theorem 2 extends to primes p < 19)
statement_text: The bounds on the tower height of $n_p(\alpha,\beta)$ given in Theorem 2 should also hold for all primes $p < 19$.
context_text: Theorem 2 is proved only for primes $p \geq 19$. The authors note that the upper bounds in Theorem 2 hold for all primes, and the lower bound holds when $\varepsilon = \alpha^3 - \beta$ is small. The obstacle for $p < 19$ in the large-$\varepsilon$ regime is that the best known bounds on the largest subset of $\mathbb{F}_p^n$ without a three-term arithmetic progression are not strong enough to imply the required lower bound estimate in that case.
notes: Stated in prose immediately before Theorem 2 with the words 'We conjecture these bounds should also hold for p < 19.' No formal labelled environment.
paper_title: Popular progression differences in vector spaces II

title: Conjecture 1.6
statement_text: For all $s \geq 1$ there exists an integer $k$ such that if $M$ is a simple binary matroid with no induced $I_s$-restriction or $PG(1, 2)$-restriction, then $\chi(M) \leq k$.
context_text: After showing that the full matroidal Gyárfás-Sumner analogue fails (Theorem 1.3), the authors conjecture that fixing $t = 2$—that is, excluding a triangle $PG(1,2)$ rather than an arbitrary projective geometry—restores a bound on the critical number. The conjecture is verified for $s \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ by Corollary 5.2, which gives $k = 1$; new ideas are needed for larger $s$.
paper_title: The structure of binary matroids with no induced claw or Fano plane restriction

title: Open Problem (Extending Induced Arithmetic Removal to Higher Complexity and General Groups)
statement_text: Extend the induced arithmetic removal lemma beyond complexity 1 patterns over finite fields to higher complexity patterns and to general abelian groups (even the case of $\mathbb{Z}$ is open).
context_text: After establishing Theorem 1.14 for complexity 1 patterns over $\mathbb{F}_q$, the authors note that extending to higher complexity and to general groups remains open. They observe that for affine-invariant patterns (which avoid a regularisation difficulty near the origin), the problem for bounded complexity patterns over finite fields has been solved, but the general induced setting is still unresolved.
paper_title: Induced arithmetic removal: complexity 1 patterns over finite fields

title: Informal Conjecture (Induced Arithmetic Removal for Arbitrary Abelian Groups)
statement_text: A statement analogous to Theorem 1.14 holds for arbitrary patterns with coefficients in $\mathbb{Z}$ over arbitrary abelian groups, subject to appropriate caveats concerning infinite complexity patterns and trivial solutions.
context_text: Immediately following the statement of the main theorem (Theorem 1.14) and its remark on tower-type bounds, the authors write: 'We conjecture that a similar statement holds for arbitrary patterns (with coefficients in $\mathbb{Z}$) in arbitrary abelian groups.' They decline to give a precise formulation due to unresolved issues with infinite complexity and trivial solutions.
notes: Authors explicitly decline to state a precise conjecture: 'we do not state a precise conjecture here'. Classified as Informal accordingly.
paper_title: Induced arithmetic removal: complexity 1 patterns over finite fields

title: Informal Conjecture (Polynomial Quantitative Bound for Complexity 1 Systems)
statement_text: For complexity 1 systems, the dependence of $\delta$ on $\epsilon$ in Definition 1.10 satisfies $\delta = \epsilon^{O_A(1)}$, i.e., polynomial dependence.
context_text: In Section 1.2, after defining complexity 1 (Definition 1.10), the authors remark that the general dependence of $\delta$ on $\epsilon$ is a wide open problem and state conjecturally that polynomial dependence holds. Reference [20] is cited for further discussion of this quantitative question.
notes: Stated as 'conjecturally' in running prose without a labelled environment and without explicit attribution to named individuals; reference [20] is cited for discussion but no author names are given in the extracted text.
paper_title: Induced arithmetic removal: complexity 1 patterns over finite fields

title: Conjecture 1.1 (The Edge-statistics Conjecture)
statement_text: For every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $k_0 = k_0(\varepsilon)$ such that for all integers $k > k_0$ and $0 < \ell < \binom{k}{2}$ we have $\mathrm{ind}(k, \ell) \leq 1/e + \varepsilon$.
context_text: The authors observe that the random graph $G(n,p)$ with $p = \binom{k}{2}^{-1}$ gives $\mathrm{ind}(k,1) \geq 1/e + o_k(1)$, and that the complete bipartite graph with smaller part of size $n/k$ achieves the same asymptotic lower bound for $\ell = k-1$. These constructions, together with additional data, motivate the conjecture that $1/e$ is an asymptotically tight upper bound for every non-trivial $\ell$.
paper_title: Edge-statistics on large graphs

title: Conjecture 1.2 (The Large Inducibility Conjecture)
statement_text: $\limsup\{\mathrm{ind}(H) : H \notin \{K_{|H|}, \overline{K}_{|H|}\}\} = 1/e$.
context_text: This conjecture for graph inducibilities is presented as an analogue of the Edge-statistics Conjecture (Conjecture 1.1), and would be implied by it. It asserts that no graph other than a complete or empty graph can have inducibility exceeding $1/e$ in the limit over large host graphs.
paper_title: Edge-statistics on large graphs

title: Open Problem (constant c vs. |H|)
statement_text: Find good bounds on $c$ as a function of $|H|$ in the bounding function $f(k) = ck\log(k+1)$; in particular, determine whether $c$ could depend polynomially on $|H|$.
context_text: The constant $c = c(H)$ obtained in the proof of Theorem 1.1 is enormous and not even known to be computable, whereas Chekuri and Chuzhoy's earlier result achieves $c$ depending polynomially on $|H|$ (at the cost of a poly-logarithmic factor $\log^d k$). The authors explicitly leave the question of good bounds on $c$ as a function of $|H|$ as an open problem.
notes: Stated in prose in the introduction without a labelled environment: "Finding good bounds on c as a function of |H| is left as an open problem, in particular it would be interesting to determine whether c could depend polynomially on |H|."
paper_title: A tight Erdős-Pósa function for planar minors

title: Conjecture 1.1
statement_text: If $n/6 \leq b < n/4$, then every graph on $n$ vertices with at least $\lfloor n^2/4 \rfloor$ edges and book number at most $b$ which is not the balanced complete bipartite graph has at least $b^2(n - 4b)$ triangles, with equality if and only if the graph is $S_{b,n}$.
context_text: The authors introduce the 3-prism blow-up $S_{b,n}$ — formed by blowing up the 3-prism graph with four parts of size $b$ and two parts of size $\lfloor(n-4b)/2\rfloor$ and $\lceil(n-4b)/2\rceil$ — as the conjectured extremal construction. The conjecture implies an asymptotically tight bound on $t(n,b)$ for $n/6 \leq b \leq n/4 - \omega(1)$, fully addressing Mubayi's question about the $\gamma(\beta)$ dependency. A footnote notes the construction is essentially identical to one in an earlier arXiv version of Mubayi's paper.
paper_title: Books versus triangles at the extremal density

title: Open problem: Combinatorial algorithm for MIS on perfect graphs
statement_text: It remains an open question to find a combinatorial algorithm for the maximum independent set problem on perfect graphs. In fact, we do not even have a combinatorial FPT algorithm for the maximum independent set problem on perfect graphs.
context_text: The maximum independent set problem on perfect graphs is solvable in polynomial time via the ellipsoid method, but a combinatorial algorithm (i.e., one not relying on the ellipsoid method) is unknown. This is mentioned as a side remark motivating the study of related graph classes.
notes: Stated as a known open problem in the community without a specific attribution cite; no labeled theorem environment.
paper_title: The independent set problem is FPT for even-hole-free graphs

title: Open problem: p-entropic matroids linear for prime p
statement_text: Our results leave open the possibility for $p$-entropic matroids to be linear whenever $p$ is prime.
context_text: The paper proves that $p$-entropic matroids are $\mathbb{F}_p$-representable for $p=2$ and $p=3$, and shows they are not linear for $p=9$ (a composite). The central remaining open problem is whether $p$-entropic matroids are always $\mathbb{F}_p$-representable when $p$ is prime, with only partial cases resolved in this work.
notes: PDF source — statement appears in the abstract as a description of what remains open; no labeled conjecture/problem environment visible in the extracted text. Full paper text is truncated, so additional explicit conjecture environments later in the paper may not have been captured.
paper_title: Entropic matroids and their representation

title: Classical MIS Dichotomy Conjecture
statement_text: For every connected graph $H$, Maximum Independent Set in $H$-free graphs is in $\mathsf{P}$ if and only if $H \in \{P_\ell\}_{\ell} \cup \{S_{i,j,k}\}_{i \leq j \leq k}$.
context_text: The polynomial-time complexity of MIS in $H$-free graphs has been studied since the early 1980s. Alekseev showed that if $H$ is neither a path nor a subdivided claw then MIS is NP-complete on $H$-free graphs, motivating this conjecture about the full dichotomy. The authors note that an even stronger conjecture is postulated by Lozin [28].
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled. $\{P_\ell\}_\ell$ denotes the family of paths and $\{S_{i,j,k}\}_{i \leq j \leq k}$ denotes the family of subdivided claws. Presented as a conjecture formalising a well-known open problem; no explicit citation in the header.
paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time

title: Parameterized MIS Dichotomy
statement_text: Is MIS (randomized) FPT or $W[1]$-hard in $H$-free graphs?
context_text: This is the parameterized counterpart of the classical MIS dichotomy question. Alekseev's NP-hardness reduction does not transfer to $W[1]$-hardness, leaving a priori more candidate graphs $H$ for which the parameterized status is open. Dabrowski et al. initiated a systematic study of this question.
notes: PDF source. Formally labelled with a bullet marker in the paper alongside the conjectures.
paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time

title: Conjecture 1
statement_text: For any integer $t$, MIS can be solved in FPT time in $P(t,t,t,t)$-free graphs.
context_text: MIS was already shown FPT on $P(t,t,t)$-free graphs; this conjecture proposes the natural next step for four-vertex paths with clique substitutions. The present paper proves the special case $P(1,t,t,t)$-free graphs as partial progress toward Conjecture 1.
notes: PDF source.
paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: For any integers $t$ and $\ell$, MIS is FPT in $P_\ell(t)$-free graphs, where $P_\ell(t) = P(t, t, \ldots, t)$ with the sequence $t, t, \ldots, t$ of length $\ell$.
context_text: This is described as a far more distant milestone than Conjecture 1, extending the FPT claim to clique-substituted paths of arbitrary length. The authors remark that even the parameterized complexity of MIS in $P_7$-free graphs (the $t=1$, $\ell=7$ case) is currently open.
notes: PDF source.
paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time

title: Informal belief on FPT candidates
statement_text: There will be very few connected candidates $H$ (as described by the structural characterisation of FPT candidate graphs) which will not end up in (randomized) FPT.
context_text: After settling all four remaining five-vertex graph cases as (randomized) FPT, the authors express a broader belief that almost all graphs $H$ satisfying the structural conditions identified in Figure 1 will yield FPT algorithms for MIS in $H$-free graphs.
notes: PDF source — informal belief stated in prose, not a formally labelled environment.
paper_title: When Maximum Stable Set can be solved in FPT time

title: Informal Conjecture (f(n) = 2n−2)
statement_text: We suspect that the above construction reflects the truth and that $f(n) = 2n - 2$ for all $n \geq 2$.
context_text: The authors give a construction (attributed to Segal-Halevi [6]) showing $f(n) \geq 2n-2$ for all $n \geq 2$, where $f(n)$ is the maximum number of cuts needed for disproportionate division with $n$ agents. Theorem 1.1 establishes $f(n) \leq 3n-4$, leaving a gap. The authors believe the lower bound is tight and that the truth is $f(n) = 2n-2$.
paper_title: Disproportionate division

title: Conjecture 3.1
statement_text: For any $n \geq 2$ non-atomic probability measures $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_n$ on the unit circle $S^1$ and non-negative reals $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n$ summing to $1$, there exists a partition of the set $[n] = P \cup Q$ into two nonempty sets and a partition of the circle $S^1 = X \cup X^c$ into two intervals such that $$\min_{i \in P} \mu_i(X) = \sum_{j \in P} \alpha_j \quad \text{and} \quad \min_{i \in Q} \mu_i(X^c) = \sum_{j \in Q} \alpha_j.$$
context_text: The authors introduce this conjecture as a topological measure-partitioning statement whose truth would imply, by a simple inductive argument, that $f(n) = 2n-2$ for all $n \geq 2$, which would be optimal for disproportionate division. The $n=2$ case is known and is essentially the Stromquist–Woodall theorem, but the conjecture is open already for $n=3$.
notes: PDF source — the display-math formula in the original is garbled in extraction (sum symbols rendered as 'X', superscript 'c' split off); statement reconstructed from context.
paper_title: Disproportionate division

title: Research Question (Introduction)
statement_text: Is it possible to determine the computational complexity of Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1, H_2)$-free graphs for all pairs $H_1, H_2$?
context_text: Kratsch and Schweitzer [22] initiated the classification of Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1,H_2)$-free graphs, and Schweitzer [30] later proved that only finitely many cases remain open without specifying them. This question frames the paper's programme to complete the dichotomy.
notes: Explicitly posed as a research question in the introduction; the paper resolves all but six pairs $(H_1,H_2)$, leaving those six as the residual open cases.
paper_title: Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1,H_2)$-free Graphs: An Almost Complete Dichotomy

title: Open Question: FPT Algorithm for Graph Isomorphism Parameterized by Clique-Width
statement_text: Whether an FPT algorithm exists for Graph Isomorphism when parameterized by clique-width is still open.
context_text: Lokshtanov et al. [23] gave an FPT algorithm parameterized by treewidth, and Grohe and Schweitzer [20] proved XP membership parameterized by clique-width (i.e., polynomial-time solvability for each fixed clique-width). The question of whether the parameter clique-width admits an FPT algorithm remains unresolved.
notes: Stated as an existing open problem in the introduction without attribution to specific authors; not a formally labelled environment. PDF source — math notation appears clean for this item.
paper_title: Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1,H_2)$-free Graphs: An Almost Complete Dichotomy

title: Open Cases: Clique-Width Classification for $(H_1,H_2)$-Free Graphs
statement_text: Determine, for each of the five remaining pairs $(H_1, H_2)$, whether the class of $(H_1, H_2)$-free graphs has bounded or unbounded clique-width.
context_text: The paper continues a project [4,8,11,12,14,15] classifying the boundedness of clique-width of $(H_1,H_2)$-free graph classes. By showing that (gem, $P_1+2P_2$)-free graphs have unbounded clique-width, the authors reduce the number of open pairs from six to five.
notes: Not a formally labelled problem environment; inferred from the paper's statement that five open cases for clique-width boundedness remain after their contribution. PDF source.
paper_title: Graph Isomorphism for $(H_1,H_2)$-free Graphs: An Almost Complete Dichotomy

title: Conjecture 1.8
statement_text: Every multigraph is widespread.
context_text: Scott [6] conjectured that every multigraph is pervasive in the ideal of all graphs, but this was disproved by a construction in [5]. The authors introduce the notion of 'widespread' and present Conjecture 1.8 as a resuscitated version of Scott's conjecture. They note they are very far from proving it, cannot verify it even for every chandelier, and that all multigraphs proved widespread so far are subdivisions of outerplanar graphs.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. VI. Banana trees

title: Conjecture 1.10
statement_text: For all graphs $J$ and for all integers $\tau \geq 0$, there exists $c$ such that if $G$ is a graph with chromatic number more than $c$, then either some induced subgraph of $G$ is a subdivision of $J$ or $\chi_2(G) > \tau$.
context_text: This conjecture is shown to be equivalent to Conjecture 1.8 (every multigraph is widespread), using the result from [3] that for all $\rho \geq 2$ and every multigraph $J$, every $\rho$-controlled class of $J$-subdivision-free graphs is $2$-controlled. The authors present 1.10 as a nicer reformulation of 1.8, though they do not use it directly in the paper.
notes: PDF source — subscript in $\chi_2(G)$ rendered as 'χ2(G)' in the extraction; reconstructed as $\chi_2(G)$.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. VI. Banana trees

title: Open Question: first pervasiveness characterisation
statement_text: Which multigraphs $H$ have the property that for all $\kappa$ there exists $c$ such that every $H$-subdivision-free graph with clique number at most $\kappa$ has chromatic number at most $c$?
context_text: The paper poses two bullet-point questions about characterising multigraphs $H$ by a pervasiveness-type condition. For the second (stronger) question, which involves all subdivisions of $H$, the answer is known for controlled classes; the first question, which asks about $H$-subdivision-free graphs directly, is explicitly stated to remain open.
notes: No numbered label in the paper; appears as a bullet-point question with the subsequent remark 'the first remains open'.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. VI. Banana trees

title: Conjecture on polynomial-time solvability of bounded sub-determinant integer programs
