statement_text: Integer programs with bounded sub-determinants can be solved in polynomial time. In particular, the stable set problem on graphs with $\mathrm{ocp}(G) \leq k$ is polynomial for every fixed $k$.
context_text: The authors note that recent work links the complexity of integer programs to the magnitude of their sub-determinants, and that it is tempting to believe bounded sub-determinant integer programs are polynomial-time solvable. This would imply the stable set problem is polynomial for every fixed bound $k$ on the odd cycle packing number, which remains open for $k \geq 2$.
notes: Stated as 'it is tempting to believe'; the paper explicitly confirms the problem is open for $k \geq 2$. No labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: The stable set problem in graphs with bounded genus and bounded odd cycle packing number

title: Open question on stable set for bounded ocp and arbitrary Euler genus
statement_text: Is the stable set problem polynomial-time solvable for graphs $G$ with $\mathrm{ocp}(G) \leq k$ (for fixed $k$) embedded in a surface of arbitrary Euler genus?
context_text: The paper's main result (Theorem 2) gives a polynomial-time algorithm when both the odd cycle packing number and the Euler genus are fixed constants. Immediately after stating this result the authors note they do not know whether the genus bound can be dropped while retaining polynomial-time solvability.
notes: Stated as 'we do not know whether'; no labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: The stable set problem in graphs with bounded genus and bounded odd cycle packing number

title: Open problem on quantum ergodicity over small spectral windows (Remark 1.2)
statement_text: The problem of controlling the other eigenvectors well enough to say that the quantum-ergodicity average in a small spectral window $I \subset (-2\sqrt{d}, 2\sqrt{d})$ of width roughly $1/\log(m)$ is not equidistributed is not pursued here and remains open. It would also be interesting to determine whether the constructions of Theorem 1.3 can prove a lower bound on the length of the smallest such window that is possible.
context_text: The works [ALM15, BLML15] prove quantum ergodicity on sequences of expander graphs when the spectral average (3) is taken over a window $I$ of width roughly $1/\log(m)$. Theorem 1.3 produces many localized eigenvectors concentrated in a very small spectral window due to high eigenvalue multiplicity, but whether this implies that the QE average itself fails to be equidistributed in that window requires controlling all other eigenvectors in the window, which the paper does not achieve.
notes: Stated in prose inside Remark 1.2, not a labelled theorem environment. PDF source — surrounding math may be partially garbled.
paper_title: High-girth near-Ramanujan graphs with localized eigenvectors

title: Question 1.12
statement_text: For every positive integer $k$, does every graph $G$ either not contain two disjoint $P_k$, or contain two disjoint avoidable $P_k$?
context_text: The analogous statement for non-adjacent avoidable $P_k$ is established as Corollary 1.11. The authors note the answer is positive for $k \in \{1,2\}$ by results of Beisegel et al., but then provide an explicit counterexample for every $k \geq 3$ (a cycle on $2k-1$ vertices with one extra vertex adjacent to two consecutive cycle vertices), resolving the question negatively within the same paper.
notes: The question is posed and then fully answered (negatively for $k \geq 3$) within the same paper; it is not open.
paper_title: Avoidable paths in graphs

title: Question 4.1
statement_text: Does there exist a family $\mathcal{H}$ of connected graphs, not containing any path, such that any graph is either $\mathcal{H}$-free or contains an avoidable element of $\mathcal{H}$?
context_text: Having established avoidable paths of every length, the authors ask whether avoidability extends to other graph structures. They note that even allowing a family rather than a single pattern, no candidate beyond paths seems to survive tests on chordal graphs or simple ad hoc constructions, and that the notion of avoidability for non-path structures is deliberately left open to interpretation.
notes: PDF source — mathematical content is clear; the family $\mathcal{H}$ notation is inferred from context.
paper_title: Avoidable paths in graphs

title: Conjecture 1
statement_text: For every positive integer $t$, if an $n$-vertex graph $G$ does not contain $K_{t+1}$ as a minor then $\alpha(G) \geq n/t$.
context_text: This conjecture is a natural weakening of Hadwiger's conjecture: since Hadwiger's conjecture would imply every $n$-vertex $K_{t+1}$-minor-free graph has chromatic number at most $t$ and thus an independent set of size at least $n/t$, this bound has attracted considerable attention (see Seymour's survey [31]). Duchet and Meyniel proved the bound holds within a factor of 2 in 1982, and subsequent improvements have been made by Fox, Balogh–Kostochka, and others. The present paper studies this conjecture restricted to triangle-free graphs.
notes: No explicit attribution appears in the conjecture header; the paper describes it as having 'received quite a lot of attention' and directs the reader to Seymour's survey [31], indicating it is a community/folklore conjecture rather than one originated by the paper authors. Classified as 'states' per the no-attribution-in-header rule.
paper_title: Independence number in triangle-free graphs avoiding a minor

title: Conjecture on $MR_t(s,k)$ for $s \geq 4$
statement_text: For all $s \geq 4$, a similar bound must hold: $MR_t(s, k) \leq t^{1-\varepsilon}k$ for sufficiently large $t$, and here $\varepsilon$ cannot be larger than $1/s$.
context_text: The paper's main result (Theorem 1) establishes $MR_t(3,k) \leq t^{1-\varepsilon}k$ for any $0 < \varepsilon < 1/26$ and all sufficiently large $t$; Erdős's construction (Theorem 2) shows $\varepsilon \leq 1/3$ is essentially tight for $s=3$. The authors conjecture in Section 4 that an analogous upper bound holds for $K_s$-free graphs with $s \geq 4$, with tightness at $\varepsilon = 1/s$.
notes: Section 4 is not included in the provided text; this conjecture is described only by a forward reference in the introduction. The exact formal statement may differ from what is reconstructed here.
paper_title: Independence number in triangle-free graphs avoiding a minor

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: Every simple graph with $m$ edges has at most $O(\kappa_1^m)$ cycles, where $\kappa_1 = (2+2\sqrt{2})^{1/5} \approx 1.3701$.
context_text: The Arman–Tsaturian construction [2] gives simple graphs with $\Omega(\kappa_1^m)$ cycles, which is the best known lower bound. The authors verified by computer several similar constructions and could not find a better one, leading them to conjecture that this construction is optimal. Remark 9 notes that any counterexample would necessarily have average degree in the range $(4.24, 7.18)$.
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled; value of $\kappa_1$ reconstructed from surrounding prose.
paper_title: Bounding the number of cycles in a graph in terms of its degree sequence

title: Conjecture 5
statement_text: There is a function $f$ such that, for any fixed tree $T$ with $m$ leaves, every $f(m)$-edge-connected graph with minimum degree at least $f(|E(T)|)$ and number of edges divisible by $|E(T)|$ has a $T$-decomposition.
context_text: This conjecture is posed by the paper's authors as an alternative to Conjecture 2 (which fails for trees of maximum degree three), bounding the required edge-connectivity in terms of the number of leaves of $T$ rather than its maximum degree. The paper's main result implies $f(2) = 3$ is achievable, and the authors note that establishing the existence of $f(3)$ would already contain the claw case.
paper_title: Edge-partitioning 3-edge-connected graphs into paths

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: For every integer $k \geq 1$, if $G$ is a bipartite digraph, with $n > 0$ vertices in each part, and every vertex has out-degree more than $n/(k+1)$, then $G$ has girth at most $2k$.
context_text: The central bipartite analogue of the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture, motivated by an extremal construction with bipartition into parts of cardinality $(k+1)t$ in which every vertex has out-degree exactly $n/(k+1)$ and no directed cycle of length at most $2k$ exists. The conjecture is implied by the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture.
paper_title: Short directed cycles in bipartite digraphs

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: For every integer $k \geq 1$, and every pair of reals $\alpha, \beta > 0$ with $k\alpha + \beta > 1$, if $G$ is a non-null bipartite digraph with bipartition $(A, B)$, where every vertex in $A$ has out-degree at least $\beta|B|$, and every vertex in $B$ has out-degree at least $\alpha|A|$, then $G$ has girth at most $2k$.
context_text: A conjecture stronger than Conjecture 1.2 (the case $\alpha = \beta$) that also implies the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture (via $\beta \to 0$). It is best possible for infinitely many pairs $(\alpha, \beta)$, as witnessed by an explicit circulant-type extremal construction; the maximal bad pairs all lie on the lines $\alpha + k\beta = 1$ or $k\alpha + \beta = 1$.
paper_title: Short directed cycles in bipartite digraphs

title: Gap problem for 1-ball reconstruction threshold
statement_text: Determine the correct threshold $q^*(n)$ such that almost every $q$-colouring of the hypercube $Q_n$ is reconstructible from the multiset of its coloured 1-balls, narrowing the gap between the lower bound $q = \Omega(n)$ and the upper bound $q \geq n^{2+\varepsilon}$.
context_text: Theorem 1.2 shows that for $q \geq n^{2+\varepsilon}$ almost every $q$-colouring of $Q_n$ is 1-distinguishable, while a counting argument shows that $\Omega(n)$ colours are necessary for reconstruction from 1-balls. The authors note explicitly that 'it would be interesting to narrow the gap', with further discussion deferred to Section 6 (open questions).
notes: Stated informally in the introduction without a labelled environment: 'It is easy to show that Ω(n) colours are required for reconstructability, and it would be interesting to narrow the gap (see Sections 1.1 and 6 for further discussion).' Section 6, which reportedly contains further open questions, was not included in the provided text extract.
paper_title: Shotgun reconstruction in the hypercube

title: Problem 1.3
statement_text: What is the infimum of fractional chromatic numbers of planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5?
context_text: The authors prove that every planar graph without cycles of length 4 or 5 is $(11:3)$-colorable, giving an upper bound of $\frac{11}{3}$ on the fractional chromatic number. They note the counterexample to Steinberg's conjecture from [4] is $(6:2)$-colorable, so the answer could be as low as $3$.
paper_title: Planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5 are (11:3)-colorable

title: Informal conjecture on optimality of 11/3
statement_text: It is not clear whether the constant $\frac{11}{3}$ from Theorem 1.1 is the best possible, and we suspect this is not the case.
context_text: Immediately after stating Problem 1.3, the authors express their belief that $\frac{11}{3}$ is not the tight infimum of fractional chromatic numbers for planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5, motivating the open problem.
paper_title: Planar graphs without cycles of length 4 or 5 are (11:3)-colorable

title: Informal question on Behrend-style sets avoiding affine patterns with mixed-sign coefficients
statement_text: We do not know if there are subsets of $[N]$, in the style of Behrend's construction, with $N^{1-o(1)}$ elements avoiding such patterns (e.g., $2x + 2y = 3w + z$).
context_text: In Section 3.1, the authors discuss why the Ruzsa–Bergelson–Host–Kra approach for constructing popular-difference counterexamples fails for certain linear identities $a_1P(b_1)+\cdots+a_4P(b_4)=0$ in which there are two positive and two negative $a_i$'s (rather than the symmetric case). They note that it is unknown whether large Behrend-style sets avoiding such patterns exist.
notes: Stated in running prose in Section 3.1 without a labelled environment. PDF source.
paper_title: Triforce and Corners

title: Informal conjecture on extending Mandache's Theorem 1.1 to all abelian groups
statement_text: It seems likely that the result [the lower bound $M(\delta) \geq m_{\mathrm{convex}}(\delta)$] can be extended to all abelian groups via Bohr sets, though as far as we know this has not yet been worked out.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 (Mandache) establishes the lower bound $M(\delta) \geq m_{\mathrm{convex}}(\delta)$ only for $G_n = \mathbb{F}_p^n$ with fixed prime $p$, via an arithmetic regularity argument combined with Fourier-analytic ideas. The authors believe a Bohr-set analogue would extend this to arbitrary abelian groups.
notes: Stated in running prose in the introduction without a labelled environment. PDF source.
paper_title: Triforce and Corners

title: Question 1
statement_text: What is the largest minor-closed class $\mathcal{G}_2$ of undirected graphs with dichromatic number at most 2?
context_text: The authors note that while $\vec{\chi}(K_6) = 2$ and $\vec{\chi}(K_7) = 3$, so $\mathcal{G}_2$ is a subclass of the $K_7$-minor-free graphs, it seems to be much smaller: there exist $K_{3,3}$-minor-free graphs with dichromatic number greater than 2.
paper_title: A Note on Graphs of Dichromatic Number 2

title: Θ(√m) excess conjecture for hypergraph cuts
statement_text: For any fixed $2 \leq r \leq k$, the smallest maximum $r$-cut over all $m$-edge $k$-graphs has excess $\Theta(\sqrt{m})$.
context_text: The authors describe this as a 'second plausible conjecture', motivated by: chromatic-number methods giving $\Omega(\sqrt{m})$ lower bounds; the standard deviation of a random cut in a linear $k$-graph being $\Theta(\sqrt{m})$; and the bound being tight for $r=k=2$ and for $r=2$, $k=3$. The paper's main Theorem 1.2 disproves this for all cases with $r \geq 3$ or $k \geq 4$, showing the excess is in fact $\Omega(m^{5/9})$ in those cases.
notes: Stated in the introduction as a 'natural guess' without a labelled environment; largely disproved by Theorem 1.2 of this same paper — the conjecture holds only for $r=2$, $k \in \{2,3\}$.
paper_title: Hypergraph cuts above the average

title: Open Question: Recognition of Switchable Graphs
statement_text: It is not clear whether graphs in the Switchable class (the hereditary class of graphs for which the reconfiguration graph of perfect matchings under flips is connected) can be recognized in polynomial time.
context_text: The authors of [14] characterize the hereditary class of general (non-bipartite) graphs for which the reconfiguration graph of perfect matchings under flips is connected, calling it Switchable. The paper notes that the recognition complexity of this class remains open.
notes: Stated as a passing remark rather than a labelled problem; paper text is truncated before any concluding open-problems section, so additional conjectures may be present in the full paper.
paper_title: The Perfect Matching Reconfiguration Problem

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: If $H_1, \ldots, H_n$ are complete multipartite graphs and $G = \prod_{i=1}^{n} H_i$, then $\alpha(G) = IR(G)$.
context_text: The authors propose this as a further strengthening of Burcroff's Conjecture 1.1, removing the assumption that the complete multipartite graphs in the direct product are balanced. It is stated at the end of the introduction after the main results are summarised.
paper_title: Isoperimetry, Stability, and Irredundance in Direct Products

title: Open Problem: Complexity of 3-colourability of $P_t$-free graphs
statement_text: Determine the complexity of $3$-colourability of $P_t$-free graphs for $t \geq 8$.
context_text: Polynomial-time algorithms for 3-colourability of $P_t$-free graphs are known for $t \leq 7$ (combining results for $t \leq 5$, $(k,t)=(3,7)$, and hardness for $P_7$-free graphs). The paper notes this problem remains open for $t \geq 8$, and provides a subexponential-time algorithm as partial progress.
notes: Stated as background open problem in prose without a labelled environment and without an explicit citation; PDF source — the inequality sign $\geq$ was dropped in extraction and has been reconstructed from context.
paper_title: $H$-colouring $P_t$-free graphs in subexponential time

title: Open Problem: Polynomial-time maximum independent set on $P_t$-free graphs
statement_text: Determine whether maximum independent set is decidable in polynomial time for $P_t$-free graphs for $t \geq 7$.
context_text: Subexponential-time algorithms for maximum independent set on $P_t$-free graphs were given by Brause and independently by Bascó, Marx and Tuza; the present paper extends the subexponential approach to a broader class of problems including maximum independent set, but polynomial-time decidability for $t \geq 7$ remains open.
notes: Stated as background open problem in prose without a labelled environment and without an explicit citation; PDF source — the inequality sign $\geq$ was dropped in extraction and has been reconstructed from context.
paper_title: $H$-colouring $P_t$-free graphs in subexponential time

title: Open Problem: Characterization of parameters satisfying (CC)
statement_text: Characterize the (hereditary, connected) graph parameters $f$ satisfying property (CC): for all integers $p$ and $s$, there exist two integers $p'$ and $s'$ such that $\mathrm{col}_{f,p'}(G) \leq s'$ for every graph $G$ satisfying $\chi^\ell_{f,p}(G) \leq s$.
context_text: The authors show that the fan parameter does not satisfy (CC) (Lemma 2), and provide two sufficient conditions (via property (CD) and via Lemma 4) covering all parameters previously studied in the literature, but explicitly state they were unable to fully describe the class of parameters satisfying (CC). This leaves the characterization problem open.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled environment: 'While we were unable to fully describe the graph parameters satisfying (CC), we provide two sufficient conditions…'. The paper text appears truncated before Section 2, so additional conjectures or open problems in later sections may not be visible in the supplied text.
paper_title: On generalized choice and coloring numbers

title: 2-Approximation Conjecture for Cluster-VD
statement_text: Cluster-VD can be 2-approximated in polynomial time.
context_text: The authors establish a 9/4-approximation algorithm for the weighted Cluster Vertex Deletion problem (finding a minimum cost vertex set whose deletion leaves no induced $P_3$). Since Cluster-VD is UGC-hard to approximate to within any ratio better than 2 (via a reduction from Vertex Cover), a 2-approximation is the natural target. The authors note their 9/4-approximation already achieves ratio 2 when the largest clique has size at most 4, and can be modified to a 2-approximation on diamond-free graphs, providing partial support for the conjecture.
notes: Stated in prose in both the abstract and the introduction without a labelled conjecture environment; language is unambiguously conjectural ('We further conjecture that …').
paper_title: Improved approximation algorithms for hitting 3-vertex paths

title: Conjecture on LB and QLB equivalence
statement_text: The two lower bounds $\mathrm{LB}(P)$ and $\mathrm{QLB}(P)$ are within a constant factor of each other for all posets $P$.
context_text: In the Conclusion, the authors present a table comparing the classical quantity $\mathrm{LB}(P) = n(\ln n - H(P))$ and the quantum quantity $\mathrm{QLB}(P) = n(H_n - QH(P))$, where $H(P) = \min_{z \in C(P)} h(z)$ is the entropy and $QH(P) = \mathbb{E}_{z \in C(P)}[h(z)]$ is its averaged variant. The authors state their findings support a conjecture relating these two bounds, but the remainder of the statement is corrupted in the PDF source.
notes: PDF source — the statement is truncated after 'QLB(P)' and replaced with garbled content (a list of arXiv IDs). The statement above is inferred from context; the exact formulation cannot be verified.
paper_title: Information-theoretic lower bounds for quantum sorting

title: Open Problem (Section 1.1)
statement_text: It is an open problem whether some variation on the local search approach can give PTAS for all monotone FO optimization problems.
context_text: Cabello–Gajser and Har-Peled–Quanrud showed that the trivial local search algorithm (performing bounded-size changes on an initial solution as long as it can be improved) gives PTASes for maximum independent set and minimum dominating set, as well as many related problems, on classes with strongly sublinear separators. The question is whether this approach, or a variation thereof, can be extended to cover all monotone first-order optimization problems on such classes.
notes: Stated as prose ('It is an open problem whether…') in Section 1.1 without a labelled theorem environment; no prior attribution given. PDF source — remainder of paper text is cut off, so additional items later in the paper may be missing.
paper_title: Baker game and polynomial-time approximation schemes

title: Informal Conjecture (vertex-disjoint paths, stability number two)
statement_text: We suspect the $k$ vertex-disjoint paths problem might be NP-complete for digraphs with stability number two.
context_text: The authors discuss whether their polynomial-time result for semicomplete digraphs (Theorem 1.2) can be extended to digraphs with bounded stability number. The edge-disjoint version is known to be polynomial-time solvable in this setting, but the vertex-disjoint version remains out of reach. The authors remark that they suspect NP-completeness already at stability number two.
notes: Stated as 'we suspect' in running prose with no labelled theorem environment. The full paper text appears truncated after Section 3; additional conjectures or open problems in later sections may have been missed.
paper_title: Disjoint paths in unions of tournaments

title: Open problem on sublinear round complexity
statement_text: It remains interesting to obtain a bound on the round complexity that is sublinear in $n$ regardless of the value of $d$.
context_text: After noting that network decompositions can replace the $O(d^4 \log^3 n)$ round complexity of Theorem 1.3 by $d^3 2^{O(\sqrt{\log n})}$, the authors remark that these alternative bounds are unsatisfying. The problem of achieving a round complexity that is sublinear in $n$ independent of $d$ remains open.
notes: Stated as an open direction in prose, no labelled environment; PDF source.
paper_title: Distributed coloring in sparse graphs with fewer colors

title: Question on randomized list-coloring round complexity
statement_text: Is it possible to avoid the multiplicative factor polynomial in $\Delta$ in a randomized version of the distributed $\Delta$-list-coloring algorithm (Corollary 2.1)?
context_text: Panconesi and Srinivasan gave a randomized $\Delta$-coloring algorithm running in $O(\log^3 n / \log \Delta)$ rounds. The paper's list-coloring extension (Corollary 2.1) achieves $O(\Delta^2 \log^3 n)$ rounds deterministically, but it is noted to be unclear whether a randomized variant can similarly remove the polynomial dependence on $\Delta$.
notes: Implicit question in prose: "it is not clear whether we can similarly avoid the multiplicative factor polynomial in $\Delta$ in a randomized version of our algorithm"; PDF source.
paper_title: Distributed coloring in sparse graphs with fewer colors

title: Conjecture 1.8
statement_text: For all integers $\kappa, \ell \geq 0$, there exists $c \geq 0$ such that every graph with chromatic number greater than $c$ contains either a complete subgraph on $\kappa$ vertices or holes of $\ell$ consecutive lengths.
context_text: The authors proved in [14] (a prior paper in this series) that every triangle-free graph with sufficiently large chromatic number contains holes of $\ell$ consecutive lengths (result 1.7 of the present paper). Conjecture 1.8 extends this to graphs with clique number bounded by any $\kappa$. The conjecture remains open; this paper makes a partial step by proving 1.9, which guarantees holes of two consecutive lengths both greater than $\ell$.
notes: Explicitly labelled 'Conjecture' in the paper. The authors state it was conjectured in their earlier paper [14]; the theorem header carries no parenthetical attribution, so classified as 'states' per schema rules. Theorem 1.9 of this paper (holes of two consecutive long lengths) is proved as a step towards this conjecture.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. X. Holes of specific residue

title: Conjecture 1.3
statement_text: There is a function $x_1(d)$ that satisfies $x_1(d) \to \infty$ as $d \to \infty$ such that $\mathrm{ch}_{\mathrm{sep}}(G) \geq x_1(d)$ for any graph $G$ with minimum degree $d$.
context_text: The authors prove Theorem 1.2 that separation choosability grows logarithmically in minimum degree for bipartite graphs, and ask whether the bipartiteness assumption can be dropped. The difficulty is that a bad list assignment with maximum separation on a graph does not necessarily retain maximum separation when edges are added, so the standard bipartite-subgraph reduction used for ordinary choosability fails.
notes: PDF source — math appears cleanly extracted for this statement.
paper_title: Separation choosability and dense bipartite induced subgraphs

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: There are functions $x_2(d)$ and $x_3(d)$ satisfying $x_2(d) \to \infty$ and $x_3(d) \to \infty$ as $d \to \infty$ such that any graph with minimum degree at least $d$ contains a complete subgraph on $x_2(d)$ vertices or a bipartite induced subgraph with minimum degree at least $x_3(d)$.
context_text: This conjecture, if true, would imply Conjecture 1.3: since $\mathrm{ch}_{\mathrm{sep}}(K_{d+1}) \sim \sqrt{d}$ (Kratochvíl–Tuza–Voigt) and Theorem 1.2 handles the dense bipartite induced subgraph case, the two alternatives together would give a separation-choosability lower bound tending to infinity for any graph of large minimum degree.
notes: PDF source — math appears cleanly extracted for this statement.
paper_title: Separation choosability and dense bipartite induced subgraphs

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: There is a constant $C > 0$ such that any triangle-free graph with minimum degree at least $d$ contains a bipartite induced subgraph of minimum degree at least $C \log d$.
context_text: This is a quantitative sharpening of Conjecture 1.4 restricted to triangle-free graphs; the logarithmic bound would be sharp up to the choice of $C$ (Theorem 3.7). The second part of the paper offers partial progress through Theorem 1.7 and several subcases proved in Section 3.
notes: PDF source — math appears cleanly extracted for this statement.
paper_title: Separation choosability and dense bipartite induced subgraphs

title: Conjecture 1.6
statement_text: There exist $d_0$ and $g_0$ such that any graph of girth at least $g_0$ with minimum degree at least $d_0$ contains a bipartite induced subgraph of minimum degree at least $3$.
context_text: This is a further variation on Conjecture 1.4 using large girth instead of triangle-freeness. The weaker statement with 3 replaced by 2 (i.e., containing an even hole) is true with $g_0 = 4$ and $d_0 = 3$ by Radovanović–Vušković; detecting bipartite induced subgraphs of minimum degree at least 3 is NP-complete.
notes: PDF source — math appears cleanly extracted for this statement.
paper_title: Separation choosability and dense bipartite induced subgraphs

title: Informal: Δ = o(n) condition not necessary for Corollary 2
statement_text: The condition $\Delta = o(n)$ in Corollary 2 is probably not necessary.
context_text: Corollary 2 states that for a graph on $n$ vertices with $\Delta = o(n)$, Glauber dynamics for $(\Delta+1)$-colourings starting at a uniformly random colouring gives an almost uniform sampler. The authors remark immediately after the corollary that the constraint $\Delta = o(n)$ is likely an artifact of the proof technique.
notes: Stated in running prose immediately after Corollary 2: 'The condition Δ = o(n) is probably not necessary.' PDF source.
paper_title: Frozen $(Δ+1)$-colourings of bounded degree graphs

title: Informal: Exponent in Theorem 1 not optimal
statement_text: The dependence on $\Delta$ in the exponent of the bound $\mathbb{P}(\alpha \text{ is frozen}) \leq (6/7)^{n/(\Delta+1)}$ in Theorem 1 is not best possible.
context_text: Theorem 1 gives the upper bound $(6/7)^{n/(\Delta+1)}$ on the probability a uniform random $(\Delta+1)$-colouring is frozen. Proposition 4 provides a matching lower bound with exponent $e^{-3\log(\Delta)/\Delta \cdot n}$, leaving a gap of a $\log\Delta$ factor. The authors believe the upper bound can be tightened to close this gap.
notes: Stated in running prose: 'We believe that the dependence on Δ in the exponent in Theorem 1 is not best possible.' PDF source — verify exponent expressions.
paper_title: Frozen $(Δ+1)$-colourings of bounded degree graphs

title: Conjecture 1.8
statement_text: For every graph $T$, family of graphs $\mathcal{F}$ such that no $F \in \mathcal{F}$ is a subgraph of a blow-up of $T$, and $\epsilon > 0$, it is NP-hard to approximate $\mathrm{ex}(G, T, \mathcal{F})$ up to additive error of $n^{v(T)-\epsilon}$ for a given input graph $G$ on $n$ vertices.
context_text: The authors prove Theorem 1.7 as a special case (for $T = K_m$, $\mathcal{F} = \{K_k\}$ with $k \geq m+2$) and note that Proposition 1.5 covers the complementary easy regime (when some $F \in \mathcal{F}$ is a subgraph of a blow-up of $T$). They believe that excluding those easy cases no significantly better approximation than $\epsilon n^{v(T)}$ is achievable in polynomial time, leading to this conjecture. Section 6 of the paper contains further remarks on this conjecture and related open problems.
paper_title: Additive Approximation of Generalized Turán Questions

title: Problem 1
statement_text: Does there for every integer $d \geq 0$ exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every $d$-degenerate graph with an assignment of lists of size $d + 1$ is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible? Or at least $\varepsilon$-flexible?
context_text: Theorem 2 shows that lists of size $d+2$ suffice for weighted $\varepsilon$-flexibility of $d$-degenerate graphs. The question is whether one extra color (size $d+1$, matching choosability) also guarantees flexibility. For $d=0$ trivial; for $d=1$ (forests with lists of size 2) weighted $1/2$-flexibility is easy; the first open case is $d=2$.
notes: PDF source; math is simple enough to read cleanly.
paper_title: List coloring with requests

title: Conjecture 7
statement_text: (a) The diagonal online random Ramsey numbers satisfy $$\tilde{r}_{rand}(n, n) = 2^{(1+o(1)) \frac{2}{3} n}.$$ (b) The off-diagonal online random Ramsey numbers ($m \geq 3$ fixed and $n \to \infty$) satisfy $$\tilde{r}_{rand}(m, n) = n^{(1+o(1)) \frac{2}{3} m}.$$
context_text: The authors define the online random Ramsey number $\tilde{r}_{rand}(m,n)$ as the maximum over $p \in (0,1)$ of $\tilde{r}(m,n;p)$, where Painter independently colors each edge red with probability $p$. These conjectures on the growth rate are motivated by a connection with the Subgraph Query Problem; Theorem 10 and Conjecture 9 together imply both parts of this conjecture.
paper_title: Online Ramsey Numbers and the Subgraph Query Problem

title: Conjecture 9
statement_text: For any $m \geq 4$, $$f(K_m, p) = 2^{o(m)} p^{-\frac{2}{3}m + c_m},$$ where $c_m$ is a constant depending on $m \bmod 3$ (exact case formula garbled in PDF extraction).
context_text: Here $f(K_m, p)$ is the minimum number of queries needed to find a copy of $K_m$ with probability at least $1/2$ in the Subgraph Query Game on $G(\mathbb{Z}, p)$. The upper bound in this conjecture is proved in Section 5.2, and together with Theorem 12 (the matching lower bound) it is fully verified for $m = 4$ and $m = 5$.
notes: PDF source — the piecewise formula defining $c_m$ for $m \equiv 0, 1, 2 \pmod{3}$ is garbled in the extraction and cannot be read cleanly.
paper_title: Online Ramsey Numbers and the Subgraph Query Problem

title: Informal conjecture on generalization of Theorem 4 to all $\tilde{r}(m,n)$
statement_text: We suspect that Theorem 4 can be generalized to $\tilde{r}(m, n)$ in the same way.
context_text: Theorem 4 establishes $\tilde{r}(3,n) = \Omega(n^3/\log^2 n)$ via a Painter strategy that avoids red triangles, in an argument close in spirit to Erdős's alteration method. The authors note that Krivelevich generalized this alteration method to all $r(m,n)$ and conjecture informally that the same can be done for $\tilde{r}(m,n)$.
notes: No explicit quantitative bound for general $m$ is stated; only the suspicion of generalizability is expressed.
paper_title: Online Ramsey Numbers and the Subgraph Query Problem

title: Conjecture 2
statement_text: For every $n$-vertex graph $G$, the extension complexity of $\mathrm{COR}(G)$ is $2^{\Omega(\mathrm{tw}(G)+\log n)}$.
context_text: The authors prove the matching upper bound $2^{O(\mathrm{tw}(G)+\log n)}$ in Theorem 1 and conjecture that this is tight in general. If true, the conjecture would improve the bound of Göös, Jain and Watson ($2^{\Omega(n/\log n)}$ for the stable set polytope) to $2^{\Omega(n)}$, and would yield explicit 0/1-polytopes with extension complexity exponential in their dimension, as predicted by the counting argument of Rothvoß.
notes: The paper proves this conjecture for the special case of proper minor-closed graph classes (Theorem 3), but the general statement remains open.
paper_title: Extension Complexity of the Correlation Polytope

title: Question 1.3
statement_text: Let $G$ be a $\lfloor (1-\varepsilon)(\Delta(G)+1) \rfloor + 1$-critical graph with $\omega(G) \leq (1-\alpha)(\Delta(G)+1)$. What is the largest $\delta = \delta(\varepsilon,\alpha)$, such that $G$ is $\delta$-sparse?
context_text: This question isolates the first of two steps in the King–Reed proof strategy for Reed's conjecture: showing that a graph which is critical with respect to a list assignment and has bounded clique number must have sparse neighbourhoods. Improving $\delta$ directly strengthens the $\varepsilon$ achievable in Theorem 1.2.
notes: PDF source; statement is clearly readable.
paper_title: Colouring Graphs with Sparse Neighbourhoods: Bounds and Applications

title: Question 1.4
statement_text: Let $G$ be a $\delta$-sparse graph. What is the largest $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(\delta)$ such that $\chi(G) \leq (1-\varepsilon)(\Delta+1)$?
context_text: This isolates the second step in the King–Reed proof: given neighbourhood sparsity, bound the chromatic number below $\Delta+1$. Molloy–Reed obtained $\varepsilon(\delta)\approx 0.0238\delta$ and Bruhn–Joos improved to $\approx 0.1827\delta - 0.0778\delta^{3/2}$; the paper further improves this using an iterative naive colouring procedure.
notes: PDF source; statement is clearly readable.
paper_title: Colouring Graphs with Sparse Neighbourhoods: Bounds and Applications

title: Conjecture 1.5 (sparse strong EH-property)
statement_text: A graph $H$ has the sparse strong EH-property if and only if $H$ is a forest.
context_text: The sparse strong EH-property of $H$ requires that there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every $H$-free graph $G$ with $|V(G)| \geq 2$ either has a vertex with at least $\varepsilon|V(G)|$ neighbours or contains two anticomplete sets of vertices each of cardinality at least $\varepsilon|V(G)|$. Erdős's construction shows the property forces $H$ to be a forest; the conjecture asserts the converse.
notes: The paper explicitly notes that since submission both conjectures in 1.5 have been proved in reference [4].
paper_title: Caterpillars in Erdős-Hajnal

title: Open Problem: 3-colourability of ternary graphs
statement_text: It may be that all ternary graphs are 3-colourable.
context_text: Bonamy, Charbit and Thomassé proved that every ternary graph is $c$-colourable for some absolute constant $c$, and a stronger result by two of the present authors shows bounded chromatic number whenever clique number is bounded and no induced cycle has length $p\bmod q$. The paper notes that whether the bound can be taken to be 3 for ternary graphs specifically remains open.
notes: Stated as a parenthetical remark in the introduction ('it may be that all ternary graphs are 3-colourable, and this remains open'); no formal conjecture environment.
paper_title: Proof of the Kalai-Meshulam conjecture

title: Informal conjecture on improved color ratio in Theorem 5
statement_text: It might be possible to improve the ratio of extra colors assigned to the vertex $x$ in Theorem 5 a bit; e.g., it could be true that there exists a coloring by subsets of $\{1, \ldots, 9\}$ such that all vertices get at least three colors and $x$ gets five.
context_text: Following Theorem 5, the authors observe that the ratio cannot be improved arbitrarily: when $G$ is the graph obtained from the wheel with five spokes by subdividing each spoke once, $x$ is the hub, and $\varphi$ is a coloring by subsets of $\{1,\ldots,k\}$ with every vertex receiving at least 3 colors, then $|\varphi(x)| \leq \frac{k}{3} + \frac{2k}{9}$. They nonetheless suggest a modest improvement may be achievable.
notes: PDF source — doubly hedged language ('might be possible', 'could be true'); included because it specifies a concrete mathematical claim. The bound formula in the surrounding text may be garbled.
paper_title: Fractional coloring of planar graphs of girth five

title: Question 1.4
statement_text: What is the smallest possible exponent $C_{p,k}$ for which Theorem 1.3 is true?
context_text: The paper proves Theorem 1.3 with exponent $C_{p,k} = \frac{k-2}{c_{p,3}} + 1$, and shows via constructions of $k$-colored sum-free sets that the smallest possible exponent must be at least $\frac{k-2}{c_{p,k}} + 1$. The achieved exponent is within a factor of $O(\log p)$ from this lower bound (independently of $k$), but the exact optimum remains open.
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled; the exponent expressions involve infimum definitions of constants $c_{p,k}$ and $c_{p,3}$ that appear clean in context.
paper_title: A polynomial bound for the arithmetic $k$-cycle removal lemma in vector spaces

title: Conjecture on maximum cliques with no $K_t$-immersion
statement_text: The maximum number of cliques in a graph on $n \geq t-2$ vertices with no $K_t$-immersion is $2^{t-2}(n-t+3)$.
context_text: The authors construct a graph on $n$ vertices with no weak $K_t$-immersion achieving $2^{t-2}(n-t+3)$ cliques: begin with a clique $K$ on $t-2$ vertices and attach $n-t+2$ additional vertices each with neighborhood $K$. Since every vertex outside $K$ has degree $t-2 < t-1$, no $K_t$-immersion exists. The authors conjecture this construction is optimal.
notes: PDF source — stated in running prose in the introduction without a labeled conjecture environment; math notation garbled in extraction and reconstructed.
paper_title: On the number of cliques in graphs with a forbidden subdivision or immersion

title: Conjecture on optimal exponential constant for $K_t$-subdivision clique count
statement_text: The maximum number of cliques in an $n$-vertex graph with no $K_t$-subdivision is $3^{2t/3+o(t)}n$, i.e., the optimal exponential constant in the exponent is $\frac{2}{3}\log_2 3 \approx 1.06$.
context_text: Theorem 1.2 establishes an upper bound of $2^{1.817t+o(t)}n$, improving on Lee and Oum. Wood's lower bound construction (which also forbids clique subdivisions) shows the constant cannot be reduced below $\frac{2}{3}\log_2 3 \approx 1.06$. The authors proved $c(t) = 3^{2t/3+o(t)}$ for $K_t$-minors in earlier work [10] and conjecture the same bound holds for $K_t$-subdivisions.
notes: PDF source — stated in both the abstract and the introduction in running prose without a labeled conjecture environment; math notation garbled in extraction and reconstructed. The conjecture is presented consistently in two places.
paper_title: On the number of cliques in graphs with a forbidden subdivision or immersion

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: For every real number $x \geq 1$ and any integer $g \geq 3$, there exists a positive number $k(x,g)$ such that every graph $G$ with $\chi_f(G) \geq k(x,g)$ contains a subgraph $H$ of girth at least $g$ and with $\chi_f(H) \geq x$.
context_text: Proposed immediately after Theorem 3 as the natural full generalisation to arbitrary girth. Theorem 3 of this paper establishes the case $g = 4$ (triangle-free subgraphs), and Conjecture 4 asks whether the same holds for every girth.
notes: PDF source — math appears readable.
paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number

title: Conjecture 6
statement_text: For every positive integers $k$ and $g \geq 3$, there exists a positive number $c(k,g)$ such that every graph with chromatic number at least $c(k,g)$ contains a subgraph of girth at least $g$ and with average degree at least $k$.
context_text: Proposed by the authors as a weakening of both the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture and Thomassen's conjecture: the hypothesis uses chromatic number (rather than average degree) and the conclusion asks only for large average degree (rather than large chromatic number) in the subgraph.
notes: PDF source — math appears readable.
paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number

also_stated_in.title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: For every graph $H$, $\chi^*(\mathcal{M}_H) \leq 2\,\mathrm{td}(H) - 2$.
context_text: Theorem 3 establishes the matching lower bound $\chi^*(\mathcal{M}_{H_k}) \geq 2k-2$ for graphs $H_k$ with $\mathrm{td}(H_k) = k$, showing that defective and clustered chromatic numbers can differ. The authors conjecture this lower bound is tight, and note the conjecture is proved in Section 4 for the special case $\mathrm{td}(H) = 3$.
notes: PDF source — math notation partially garbled; statement reconstructed from context. Introduced with the phrase 'We conjecture an analogous upper bound'.
paper_title: Clustered Colouring in Minor-Closed Classes

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: For every planar graph $H$, the Erdős-Pósa property holds for $H$-models with a $O(k \log k)$ bounding function.
context_text: Chekuri and Chuzhoy established a $O(k \log^c k)$ bounding function for planar $H$-models with $c$ at least a double-digit integer; their approach necessarily yields $c \geq 2$. Since a $\Omega(k \log k)$ lower bound already holds for any planar $H$ containing a cycle, proving this conjecture would completely settle the growth rate of the Erdős-Pósa function for all planar graphs $H$. The paper proves the conjecture for $H = W_t$ (wheels) as its main result.
paper_title: A tight Erdős-Pósa function for wheel minors

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: There is a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for all integers $r, k \geq 1$, every graph $G$ of treewidth at least $f(r) \cdot k \log(k + 1)$ has $k$ vertex-disjoint subgraphs $G_1, \ldots, G_k$, each of treewidth at least $r$.
context_text: This is the treewidth-level analogue of Conjecture 1.2: it asserts that $c' = 1$ suffices in Theorem 1.3 of Chekuri and Chuzhoy (at least ignoring the precise dependence on $r$), whereas their proof only gives $c' \geq 1$. The authors note it is implied by Conjecture 1.2 by taking $H$ to be the $r \times r$ grid.
notes: Section 4 (open problems) is cut off in the source text; additional items may be present but are not extractable.
paper_title: A tight Erdős-Pósa function for wheel minors

title: Question 1.1
statement_text: Is it true that for every (fixed) integer $t > 0$, the 3-coloring problem can be solved in polynomial time when restricted to the class of $P_t$-free graphs?
context_text: Theorem 1.1 (from the literature) states that if the $k$-coloring problem is polynomial on $H$-free graphs then every connected component of $H$ must be a path. For $k=3$ the converse may hold — it is known to fail for $k>4$ — so the question of whether $P_t$-free graphs always admit a polynomial-time 3-coloring algorithm remains open. The present paper contributes a positive result for the subclass of $(P_6+rP_3)$-free graphs.
notes: The question has no explicit attribution citation in the header; it is recorded as a standing open problem in the field by the paper's authors. Full paper text was truncated in the supplied PDF extraction, so later sections could not be checked for additional conjectural statements.
paper_title: List-three-coloring graphs with no induced $P_6+rP_3$

title: Closing Question
statement_text: What is the supremum of all real numbers $c$ such that for any graph $G$ of order at least $2$, $$\max\{\lambda_2(G), \lambda_2(\bar{G})\} \geq c\,?$$
context_text: The paper proves the supremum is at least $\tfrac{2}{5}$. The self-complementary path $P_4$ satisfies $\lambda_2(P_4) = 2 - \sqrt{2} < 0.5858$, giving an upper bound on the supremum. The exact value is left open.
paper_title: The Algebraic Connectivity of a Graph and its Complement

title: Informal Conjecture on exact asymptotics of maxcode_L for even L
statement_text: We believe that in fact $\mathrm{maxcode}_L(\varepsilon) = c_L \varepsilon^{-1} + O(1)$ for even $L$.
context_text: Theorem 2 establishes the two-sided bound $c_L \varepsilon^{-1} + O(1) \leq \mathrm{maxcode}_L(\varepsilon) = O(\varepsilon^{-1})$ for every even $L \geq 2$, where $c_L = 2^{-\lfloor L/2 \rfloor}\binom{L}{\lfloor L/2 \rfloor}$ comes from Theorem 1. The authors conjecture that the lower bound is tight up to an additive constant, i.e., that the exact leading coefficient is $c_L$.
notes: Stated as 'We believe that in fact …' in prose immediately after Theorem 2; no labelled theorem environment. PDF source — math notation may be garbled.
paper_title: List-decodable zero-rate codes

title: Conjecture 3.5
statement_text: There is an integer $\ell$ such that if $G$ is a directed-triangle-free digraph with $\alpha(D) = \alpha$, then $\gamma(G) \leq \alpha^{\ell}$.
context_text: The same authors previously conjectured in [8] that the dichromatic number $\vec{\chi}(G)$ of a directed-triangle-free digraph can be bounded as a polynomial function of $\alpha(G)$. Because $\gamma(G) \leq \alpha(G) \cdot \vec{\chi}(G)$ (Observation 3.4), Conjecture 3.5 is presented as a necessary consequence: if the dichromatic-number conjecture holds, so must this domination bound.
notes: The hypothesis uses $\alpha(D) = \alpha$ in the PDF source, but the digraph is consistently called $G$ throughout the paper; this appears to be a typo for $\alpha(G) = \alpha$.
paper_title: Domination and fractional domination in digraphs

title: Classification of planar graphs by cop number
statement_text: Classify which planar graphs have cop number $i$, for $1 \leq i \leq 3$.
context_text: By Theorem 1, every connected planar graph has cop number 1, 2, or 3. Cop-win graphs are precisely the dismantlable ones, which may eventually aid in classifying planar cop-win graphs, but a complete classification for all three cop number values remains open.
notes: PDF source; stated as open in survey prose
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Classification of outerplanar graphs by cop number
statement_text: Classify which outerplanar graphs are $1$-cop-win and which are $2$-cop-win.
context_text: By Theorem 3, every outerplanar graph has cop number at most 2. The smallest outerplanar graph with cop number 2 is the four cycle, but a full classification of outerplanar graphs by cop number remains open.
notes: PDF source
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Tightness of linear capture time bound for planar graphs
statement_text: Is the bound $\mathrm{capt}_3(G) \leq 2n$ tight for planar graphs of order $n$? Does there exist a planar graph $G$ of order $n$ with $\mathrm{capt}(G) > n$?
context_text: Theorem 5 establishes $\mathrm{capt}_3(G) \leq 2n$ for planar graphs of order $n$, improving the earlier $O(n^2)$ bound of Theorem 4. Whether this linear bound is tight is unknown; even finding a single planar graph with capture time exceeding $n$ remains open.
notes: PDF source
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Capture time bounds for higher genus graphs
statement_text: What are the bounds on the capture time for graphs of higher genus? If Schroeder's conjecture holds and $\gamma(G) = g$, is it true that $\mathrm{capt}_k(G) = O(n^{g+4})$ for every $k \geq g+3$?
context_text: The paper identifies bounding the capture time of graphs embedded on surfaces as an interesting open question. If Schroeder's conjecture holds, giving $c(G) \leq g+3$ for genus-$g$ graphs, then a natural bound on $\mathrm{capt}_k(G)$ would follow for $k \geq g+3$.
notes: PDF source
paper_title: Topological directions in Cops and Robbers

title: Open Problem (exact exponent for maximal cliques in c-closed graphs)
statement_text: Determine the exact exponent of $n$ (between $3/2$ and $2 - 2^{1-c}$) in the expression for the maximum number of maximal cliques in a $c$-closed graph on $n$ vertices.
context_text: Theorem 1.4 establishes an upper bound of $O(n^{2-2^{1-c}})$ maximal cliques in any $c$-closed graph on $n$ vertices, while Theorem 1.7 gives a matching lower bound of $\Omega(c^{-3/2} 2^{c/2} n^{3/2})$, leaving the exact exponent of $n$ undetermined for intermediate values of $c$. The two bounds coincide at $3/2$ and diverge as $c$ grows.
notes: Stated in running prose as 'It is an open problem'; no labelled theorem environment. PDF source — math notation may be garbled.
paper_title: Finding Cliques in Social Networks: A New Distribution-Free Model

title: Open Question on s((Z/kZ)^n) for fixed k
statement_text: It remains an interesting question to estimate $s((\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z})^n)$ when $k$ is fixed and $n$ is large.
context_text: Alon and Dubiner proved $s((\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z})^n) \leq (cn\log n)^n k$ for some absolute constant $c$, showing that for any fixed $n$ the quantity grows linearly in $k$. Elsholtz obtained lower bounds $s((\mathbb{Z}/k\mathbb{Z})^n) \geq 2.08^n$ for odd $k\geq 3$ and large $n$, but a large gap remains between these bounds and the upper bounds of Corollary 2 for fixed odd $k\geq 3$ and large $n$.
notes: Stated as a general open problem in the field with no labeled environment; no specific attribution to prior work given.
paper_title: Erdős-Ginzburg-Ziv constants by avoiding three-term arithmetic progressions

title: Question 1.1
statement_text: Is it true that for any finite family of graphs $\mathcal{F}$ that contains at least one forest, there exists a constant $k_0 = k_0(\mathcal{F})$ such that $\mathcal{F}$ is $k$-nice for all $k \geq k_0$?
context_text: It is easy to see that if $\mathcal{F}$ contains no forest then it is not $k$-nice for any $k$. The question asks whether a converse holds: every family containing at least one forest is eventually $k$-nice. The paper proves this for all families containing a graph with at most 2 edges and for several two-graph families of 3-edge connected graphs.
paper_title: Ramsey-nice families of graphs

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: Any finite family of graphs $\mathcal{F}$ that contains at least one forest is $k$-nice for infinitely many integers $k$.
context_text: This is posed as a weaker version of Question 1.1. Theorem 1.4 shows that $\mathcal{F}_7 = \{K_3, P_4, S_3\}$ is $k$-nice for infinitely many $k$, and Theorem 1.6 gives further support for families containing a star $K_{1,r+1}$.
paper_title: Ramsey-nice families of graphs

title: Open Problem (better query complexity bounds for property testing)
statement_text: It remains a major open problem if better bounds hold for the various property testing results.
context_text: General results in combinatorial property testing show that natural properties can be tested with constant query complexity depending only on $\varepsilon$ and the property, but the upper bounds arising from proofs are often enormous: wowzer-type or Ackermann-type in $1/\varepsilon$, or established only via compactness with no explicit bound at all. The paper makes progress on this problem for permutations by establishing a universal polynomial-in-$1/\varepsilon$ query complexity bound for two-sided testing of hereditary properties with respect to the rectangular distance.
notes: PDF source — paper text is truncated (ends mid-sentence in the introduction); this open problem is presented as a known community-level motivation rather than a fresh conjecture introduced by the paper's authors. No formal labelled environment. Any conjectures or questions posed in later sections of the paper are not visible in the supplied extract.
paper_title: Fast property testing and metrics for permutations

title: Question 1.1
statement_text: For which subsets $K \subset E^n$ does every red/blue-coloring of $E^n$ contain a red pair of points of distance one or a blue isometric copy of $K$?
context_text: This is the central question studied throughout the paper. The notation $E^n \to (\ell_2, K)$ means every red/blue-coloring of $E^n$ contains a red copy of $\ell_2$ or a blue copy of $K$, and $E^n \not\to (\ell_2, K)$ means there is a coloring avoiding both. The paper says it follows Erdős et al. [4] in studying this question.
notes: The theorem header carries no parenthetical attribution; the paper notes it follows Erdős et al. [4] in studying this general question.
paper_title: Lines in Euclidean Ramsey theory

title: Open problem: $E^n \not\to (\ell_3, \ell_m)$ for large $m$
statement_text: For every natural number $m$, does there exist a natural number $n$ such that $E^n \not\to (\ell_3, \ell_m)$?
context_text: In the concluding remarks the authors say they were unable to decide this question. They consider it unlikely to hold for large $m$ but were unable to exhibit a coloring confirming their suspicion. Erdős et al. [3] showed that $E^n \to (\ell_6, \ell_6)$ for all $n$, providing related context.
notes: PDF source — stated in prose in the concluding remarks without a labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Lines in Euclidean Ramsey theory

title: Open problem: removing diameter dependence from Theorem 1.1
statement_text: Can the dependence on the diameter $R$ be removed from Theorem 1.1? That is, does there exist a function $f(n)$, independent of the diameter of $K$, such that $E^n \not\to (\ell_2, K)$ for every $1$-separated set $K \subset E^n$ with $|K| > f(n)$?
context_text: Theorem 1.1 requires $|K| > 10^4 n \log R$ where $R$ is the diameter of $K$. The authors note in the concluding remarks that a complete answer to the Erdős et al. problem on a universal two-coloring bound would follow from resolving this problem, and describe it as interesting in its own right.
notes: PDF source — stated in prose in the concluding remarks without a labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Lines in Euclidean Ramsey theory

title: Polynomial dependence of $\delta$ on $d$ in Rödl's theorem
statement_text: A polynomial dependence of $\delta$ on $d$ holds in Theorem 2.2, i.e., the $\delta$ satisfying Rödl's theorem can be taken to be polynomial in $d$; and this would imply the Erdős-Hajnal conjecture itself.
context_text: In the discussion following Rödl's theorem (2.2), the authors note that Rödl's original proof gives a tower-type bound for $1/\delta$ in terms of $1/d$, while Fox and Sudakov [7] improve this to $\delta = 2^{-|V(H)|^{15}(\log(1/d))^2}$. A polynomial dependence is stated as a community conjecture whose truth would imply the full Erdős-Hajnal conjecture.
notes: Stated in passive voice ('It is conjectured that…') without a specific citation; appears to be a community/folklore conjecture presented without explicit attribution. For $H = C_5$ the authors note one can achieve the intermediate bound $\delta = 2^{-O((\log(1/d))^2 / \log\log(1/d))}$.
paper_title: Towards Erdos-Hajnal for graphs with no 5-hole

title: Question 1.5
statement_text: Does there exist a function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that, for every $c > 1$ and every graph $G$ of maximum degree at most $\Delta$ with no clique of size greater than $\Delta^{1/f(c)}$, we have $\chi_\ell(G) \leq \Delta/c$?
context_text: A linear improvement on the greedy bound for graphs without large cliques was notably missing before this paper. Molloy's Theorem 1.2 gives a bound of $200\omega\,\frac{\Delta\ln\ln\Delta}{\ln\Delta}$, and Spencer's Ramsey-theory result shows the bound cannot be improved when $\omega = \Omega(\Delta^{1/2})$, motivating the question of whether a sublinear clique-number condition suffices for a $\Delta/c$ bound on $\chi_\ell$.
notes: The paper answers Question 1.5 in the affirmative via Theorem 1.6, with $f(c) = (72c)^2$ sufficing for large enough $\Delta$.
paper_title: Bounding $χ$ by a fraction of $Δ$ for graphs without large cliques

title: List-chromatic analogue of Reed's Conjecture
statement_text: It is possible that Conjecture 1.3 is also true for the list-chromatic number, i.e., $\chi_\ell(G) \leq \left\lceil \frac{1}{2}(\Delta + 1 + \omega) \right\rceil$ for every graph $G$ of maximum degree at most $\Delta$ with no clique of size greater than $\omega$.
context_text: After stating Reed's Conjecture 1.3 for the ordinary chromatic number and noting Reed's partial result as evidence, the authors remark that the same bound may extend to the list-chromatic number. The correspondence chromatic number is always at least as large as the list-chromatic number, so such an extension would be nontrivial.
notes: Stated informally in the introduction: 'It is possible that Conjecture 1.3 is also true for the list-chromatic number.'
paper_title: Bounding $χ$ by a fraction of $Δ$ for graphs without large cliques

title: Conjecture 1.1
statement_text: Every triangle-free planar graph contains an induced 2-degenerate subgraph on at least $\frac{7}{8}$ of its vertices.
context_text: The authors introduce this as the central open problem motivating the paper, noting it would be tight for the cube (the unique 3-regular triangle-free planar graph on 8 vertices). The paper's main theorems (Theorems 1.2 and 1.3) prove weaker bounds of $\frac{4}{5}$ and $\frac{7}{8}n - 18n_3$ respectively as partial progress.
paper_title: Induced 2-degenerate Subgraphs of Triangle-free Planar Graphs

title: Informal belief: 5/6 bound attainable
statement_text: We believe the argument we use can be strengthened to give a bound $\frac{5}{6}$, however the technical issues are substantial and since we do not see this as a viable way to prove Conjecture 1.1 in full, we prefer to present the easier argument giving the bound $\frac{4}{5}$.
context_text: Immediately after stating Theorem 1.2 (the $\frac{4}{5}$ bound), the authors remark that their method is likely improvable to $\frac{5}{6}$ but that pursuing this is not a practical route to proving the full $\frac{7}{8}$ conjecture.
notes: Stated as an authorial belief in running prose, not a labelled environment.
paper_title: Induced 2-degenerate Subgraphs of Triangle-free Planar Graphs

title: Informal Conjecture (generalization of 1.3 to bounded clique number)
statement_text: The corresponding result to 1.3 is true for graphs with bounded clique number rather than just triangle-free graphs: for all integers $\nu > 0$ and $k \geq 3$ there exists $n$ such that if $G$ has no clique of cardinality $k$ and has chromatic number at least $n$, then for some $t$, $G$ has a hole of length $t+i$ for $1 \leq i \leq \nu$.
context_text: Immediately after the statement of the main result (1.3), which establishes $\nu$ consecutive hole lengths for triangle-free graphs, the authors conjecture the same conclusion holds under the weaker assumption that $G$ has bounded clique number. They note they have made no progress in proving this.
notes: PDF source — exact statement inferred from prose; the phrase 'the corresponding result' refers to Theorem 1.3 in context
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: Let $F$ be an infinite set of positive integers with bounded gaps. Then $F$ is constricting.
context_text: A set $F$ of integers is $k$-constricting if there exists $n$ such that every graph with chromatic number at least $n$ contains either a clique with $k$ vertices or a hole with length in $F$; $F$ is constricting if it is $k$-constricting for every $k$. The authors prove (1.4) that every infinite set with bounded gaps is 3-constricting, and conjecture 1.5 as the natural generalization to all $k$.
notes: PDF source — math appears cleanly readable
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IV. Consecutive holes

title: Problem 1.6
statement_text: Is there an infinite set $F$ of positive integers such that $F$ is constricting and has density $0$?
context_text: The authors discuss which infinite sets are constricting, noting that not every infinite set is constricting (not even sets with upper density one), while sets with bounded gaps are 3-constricting. Lower density seems closer to the truth, and the authors conjecture the answer to Problem 1.6 is positive.
notes: PDF source — math appears cleanly readable; authors conjecture the answer is positive

context_text: Statement 2.1 is proved in the paper for all $\ell \geq 5$ (with $\ell = 7$ handled by a prior paper). The authors remark that 2.1 might also hold for $\ell = 4$ but that they have not been able to decide this.

title: Informal Conjecture (constant-size M)
statement_text: I conjecture that there actually always exists a balanced separator $C \cup M$ of $G$ with $q(C) \leq q(V(G))/\ell$ for some set $M$ of constant size (dependent on the class and $\ell$, but not on $|V(G)|$).
context_text: Theorem 5 guarantees a weighted balanced separator $C \cup M$ with $q(C) \leq q(V(G))/\ell$, but the bound on $|M|$ is only polylogarithmic in $n$ for graphs from a class with polynomial $\omega$-expansion. The author conjectures this polylogarithmic dependence on $n$ can be eliminated entirely, yielding a set $M$ of size depending only on the graph class and $\ell$.
notes: Stated as inline prose in the bullet-point remarks following Theorem 5; no labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: On classes of graphs with strongly sublinear separators

title: Antisymmetric variant of Jaeger's weak 3-flow conjecture
statement_text: There is a constant $k$ such that every directed $k$-edge-connected graph has a $\mathbb{Z}_5$-antisymmetric flow.
context_text: The authors present this as the natural antisymmetric analogue of Jaeger's weak 3-flow conjecture (which was solved by Thomassen and later improved to 6-edge-connectivity by Lovász et al.). The note $\mathbb{Z}_5$ is best possible since $\mathbb{Z}_2$, $\mathbb{Z}_3$, and $\mathbb{Z}_4$ cannot support antisymmetric flows in general.
notes: Stated in prose as 'The natural antisymmetric variant … would be the following' without a labelled environment.
paper_title: Additive bases and flows in graphs

also_stated_in.title: Additive bases and flows in graphs

title: Conjecture 3
statement_text: There exists a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that $\chi(G) \leq f(k)$ for every $k \geq 1$ and every graph $G$ admitting a spaghetti tree-decomposition $(T, \{B_t\}_{t \in V(T)})$ and a path-decomposition $(P, \{B_p\}_{p \in V(P)})$ such that $|B_t \cap B_p| \leq k$ for every $t \in V(T)$ and $p \in V(P)$.
context_text: The authors conclude the paper with this conjecture as an open problem in the spirit of exploring how the Asplund–Grünbaum result could be extended. A spaghetti tree-decomposition is a tree-decomposition rooted at some vertex $r$ such that, orienting edges away from $r$, the subtree induced by bags containing any fixed vertex $v$ forms a directed path. The authors remark that the conjecture could even hold with two spaghetti tree-decompositions.
paper_title: Burling graphs, chromatic number, and orthogonal tree-decompositions

title: Motivating Question (Introduction)
statement_text: For which values of $p$ is it true that $\mathrm{ex}(G(n,p), K_m, H) = (1+o(1))\binom{k-1}{m}\left(\frac{n}{k-1}\right)^m p^{\binom{m}{2}}$ w.h.p.?
context_text: For any fixed $H$ with $\chi(H)=k>m$ the deterministic extremal result gives $\mathrm{ex}(n,K_m,H)=(1+o(1))\binom{k-1}{m}(n/(k-1))^m$. Analogous to the edge case characterised by Theorem 1.1, the authors ask for which values of $p$ this extremal count is achieved with high probability in $G(n,p)$. The paper answers the question fully when $m_2(H)\geq m_2(K_m)$ (Theorem 1.2) and only partially when $m_2(H)<m_2(K_m)$ (Theorem 1.4), leaving the exact transition threshold in the latter case open; the paper closes with a dedicated Section 6 of open problems not included in the provided text.
notes: Posed in introductory prose without a labelled environment. Section 6 (open problems) is absent from the provided PDF extraction, so further explicit open-problem formulations may be missing. PDF source — math may be garbled.
paper_title: Many cliques in $H$-free subgraphs of random graphs

statement_text: It seems plausible that $\chi_c(G(n, 1/2)) = (1/2 + o(1)) \log n$ whp.

The authors note that the constant $\frac{1}{2000}$ in their lower bound can certainly be improved but that the method as presented does not suffice to pin down the tight constant.

title: Conjecture 8
statement_text: $c(g) = g^{\frac{1}{2}+o(1)}$ and $ec(g) = g^{\frac{1}{2}+o(1)}$. In other words, for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $g_0$ such that for every $g \geq g_0$, $$g^{\frac{1}{2}-\varepsilon} < c(g) < g^{\frac{1}{2}+\varepsilon}$$ and $$g^{\frac{1}{2}-\varepsilon} < ec(g) < g^{\frac{1}{2}+\varepsilon}.$$

title: Open problem on lower bound for concentration interval
statement_text: For $p$ fixed, nothing is known from below concerning concentration: it has not even been shown that the chromatic number cannot be concentrated in an interval of constant length.
context_text: After establishing that $\chi(G)$ for $G \in \mathcal{G}(n,p)$ with $p$ fixed is concentrated in an interval of length $\omega(n)\sqrt{n}/\log n$, the author notes that all known results give only upper bounds on the concentration width. Whether $\chi(G)$ could in fact be concentrated on a bounded (or even constant) number of values remains completely open. Bollobás [4, 6] is cited for further discussion of this question.

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: A minor-closed class of graphs $\mathcal{G}$ satisfies $\mathrm{col}^\star(\mathcal{G}) \leq t$ if and only if there exists $m \geq 1$ such that $K_{t,m} \notin \mathcal{G}$ and $I_{t-1}+P_m \notin \mathcal{G}$.
context_text: Observation 3 shows that any class $\mathcal{G}$ with $\mathrm{col}^\star(\mathcal{G}) \leq t$ can contain only finitely many graphs of the form $K_{t,m}$ or $I_{t-1}+P_m$; the conjecture asserts that this necessary condition is also sufficient for minor-closed classes. Theorem 7 confirms the conjecture for the special case of minor-closed classes of bounded treewidth, and Conjecture 4 would imply $\chi^\star_{\ell}(\mathcal{G}) = \mathrm{col}^\star(\mathcal{G})$ for every minor-closed class.
